**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Hatchback, Sedan, Coupe, and Station Wagon body styles with reference to a real-world Indian example of each. For each body style, state the *typical aerodynamic drag coefficient* range and explain the engineering reason for the difference.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The **European Union's motor vehicle classification** is a regulatory taxonomy established under EU Directives 2001/116/EC (M and N) and 2002/24/EC (L), later refined in Regulation (EU) 2018/858. India follows the same framework via CMVR.

**Decision Flowchart:**

```
Motor Vehicle
   |
   |-- Wheels < 4 or (Wheels = 4 AND Unladen Mass ≤ 400 kg) --> L
   |
   |-- Wheels ≥ 4 AND Purpose = Passenger --> M
   |      |-- Seats ≤ 8 + driver --> M1
   |      |-- Seats > 8 AND GVW ≤ 5 t --> M2
   |      |-- Seats > 8 AND GVW > 5 t --> M3
   |
   |-- Wheels ≥ 4 AND Purpose = Goods --> N
          |-- GVW ≤ 3.5 t --> N1
          |-- 3.5 t < GVW ≤ 12 t --> N2
          |-- GVW > 12 t --> N3
```

**M vs. N Distinction:**

| Aspect | M Category | N Category |
|:-------|:-----------|:-----------|
| **Purpose** | Carriage of passengers | Carriage of goods |
| **Body** | Enclosed passenger cabin, often with windows all around | Cabin + open/closed cargo bay (tray, box, or tank) |
| **Sub-division basis** | Seating capacity AND GVW | GVW only |
| **Example – M1** | Maruti Swift (5-seater) | – |
| **Example – M3** | Volvo 9600 Coach (53-seater) | – |
| **Example – N1** | – | Tata Ace Gold (750 kg payload) |
| **Example – N3** | – | Tata Signa 4825 (28 t GVW) |

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

| Body Style | Indian Example | Typical $C_d$ | Engineering Reason |
|:-----------|:---------------|:--------------|:-------------------|
| **Hatchback** | Maruti Swift | 0.30 – 0.35 | Short, vertical tailgate creates a large **rear turbulent wake**; the air separates abruptly, increasing pressure drag. |
| **Sedan** | Hyundai Verna | 0.26 – 0.30 | The **separate, tapered boot** lets the airflow re-attach and exit gradually, reducing wake size. |
| **Coupe** | BMW Z4 / Mini Coupe | 0.24 – 0.28 | A *fastback* or aggressive teardrop shape; the roofline flows seamlessly into the rear deck, minimising separation. |
| **Station Wagon** | Skoda Octavia Combi | 0.30 – 0.34 | Long roofline to the tail causes **extended flow separation** at the rear; a small spoiler is often added. |

**Drag Force Equation:**

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2
$$

Substituting $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $A \approx 2.2$ m², $v = 27.78$ m/s (100 km/h), and $C_d = 0.28$ (sedan):

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (27.78)^2 \approx 291.4 \text{ N}
$$

For a hatchback at the same conditions with $C_d = 0.33$:

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.33 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (27.78)^2 \approx 343.3 \text{ N}
$$

**Difference:** $343.3 - 291.4 = 51.9$ N more drag on the hatchback. This directly translates to a **fuel-economy penalty** of roughly 8 – 12 % on highway drives.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss in Body Style Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label the "number of boxes"** (2-box vs. 3-box). Always state explicitly. Examiner looks for this in the first 30 seconds of valuation.
> 2. **Confusing "Coupe" with "Sedan".** A coupe is *always* 2-door, *always* sportier, *always* lower roofline. A 2-door sedan is a sedan with two doors, NOT a coupe.
> 3. **Listing "Sunroof" as a body style.** A sunroof is a *feature*, not a body style.
> 4. **Writing "Crossover" or "SUV" when the question asks for HSC CSS only.** Stick to the syllabus. Body styles outside the HSC CSS family (e.g., SUV, Pickup) are taught in Module 2 / 3, not Module 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Module 1 (L/M/N + HSC CSS)**

- [x] **L-category** = vehicles with < 4 wheels OR 4-wheeled quadricycles ≤ 400 kg.
- [x] **L1e / L2e** are *speed-capped* at 45 km/h and power-capped at 4 kW.
- [x] **L6e** = Light quadricycle (≤ 350 kg, ≤ 45 km/h). Example: Bajaj Qute.
- [x] **L7e** = Heavy quadricycle (≤ 400 kg, can exceed 45 km/h). Example: Renault Twizy 80.
- [x] **M-category** = ≥ 4 wheels + passenger carriage. Sub-class split: **M1** (≤ 8 seats + driver), **M2** (seats > 8, GVW ≤ 5 t), **M3** (seats > 8, GVW > 5 t).
- [x] **N-category** = ≥ 4 wheels + goods carriage. Sub-class split: **N1** (≤ 3.5 t), **N2** (3.5–12 t), **N3** (> 12 t). **GVW is the ONLY criterion** for N-sub-classification.
- [x] **Hatchback** = 2-box, FWD-typical, urban, short wheelbase, steep tailgate.
- [x] **Sedan/Saloon** = 3-box, low $C_d$, separate boot, family/highway.
- [x] **Coupe** = 2-door, sporty, low $C_d$ (0.24–0.28), fixed B-pillar, fastback roofline.
- [x] **Convertible** = retractable roof, leisure, often 2-seat or 2+2, slightly higher $C_d$ due to cabin turbulence.
- [x] **Station Wagon** = extended roofline, large cargo volume (up to 1 700 L), family + luggage.
- [x] **Drag force** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$. **Drag power** $P_d = F_d \cdot v = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3$. Note the **cubic** dependence on $v$.
- [x] **Purpose-based M/N split** is the most-frequently tested concept. Do NOT classify by *body shape* (a pickup is N, not a separate class).
- [x] **Always state the *parameter* (wheels, seats, GVW, mass, speed) when defining a sub-class** — examiners award marks for this *explicit linkage*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IC Engine Components: Head Block and Sump, Cylinder, Piston, Piston Pin, Crank, Connecting Rod, Valve Train and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IC Engine Components: Head Block and Sump, Cylinder, Piston, Piston Pin, Crank, Connecting Rod, Valve Train and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Internal Combustion (IC) Engine Components

An **Internal Combustion Engine** is a prime mover that converts the chemical energy of a fuel–air mixture into mechanical work (rotational energy on the crankshaft) through a controlled sequence of **suction, compression, power (combustion), and exhaust** strokes within a closed cylinder. The structural, kinematic, and sealing sub-systems that make this energy conversion possible are collectively termed **IC Engine Components**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, these components are classified under three broad functional groups:

1. **Stationary Components** — Cylinder Block, Cylinder Head, Oil Sump (Crankcase), Cylinder Liner/Sleeve.
2. **Reciprocating Components** — Piston, Piston Rings, Piston Pin (Gudgeon Pin / Wrist Pin), Connecting Rod.
3. **Rotating Components** — Crankshaft, Flywheel, Valve Train (Camshaft, Valves, Springs, Rocker Arms, Push Rods).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to *identify, classify, and explain the constructional features, materials, and functional requirements* of each of these components. Sketches with proper part names are mandatory for full marks in ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Engine as the "Human Respiratory + Circulatory System"

Think of a car engine as the **human body** during exercise:

| Engine Component | Human Body Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylinder + Head** | Chest cavity + Lungs | Sealed chamber where "breathing" (combustion) happens |
| **Piston** | Diaphragm muscle | Pumps up and down, doing the work |
| **Connecting Rod** | Forearm bone (radius/ulna) | Translates straight motion into rotary motion |
| **Crankshaft** | Shoulder joint + Upper arm | Converts push-pull into rotation |
| **Camshaft + Valves** | Epiglottis (voice box valve) | Times the opening and closing of intake/exhaust |
| **Oil Sump** | Stomach holding digestive juices | Reservoir of lubricant |
| **Piston Pin** | Elbow joint pin | Pivot between piston and connecting rod |

The **reciprocating motion** of the piston is analogous to the up-down movement of a syringe plunger, while the **rotating crankshaft** acts like a windmill being pushed by alternating gusts of wind.

---

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

The following industry-standard values are critical for KTU numerical problems:

- **Bore (D):** Internal diameter of the cylinder = typically **70 mm to 100 mm** for passenger cars.
- **Stroke (L):** Length of piston travel = typically **75 mm to 90 mm**.
- **Compression Ratio (r):** Ratio of total cylinder volume to clearance volume = **8:1 to 12:1** (SI engines), **15:1 to 22:1** (CI engines).
- **Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP):** Theoretical constant pressure producing same work = **8–10 bar** (SI), **7–9 bar** (CI).
- **Piston Speed:** $V_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ m/s, limited to **≤ 20 m/s** for automotive engines.
- **Engine Speed (N):** Passenger car engines typically **1500–6000 rpm**.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard SI unit for pressure inside the combustion chamber is **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa). Use **bar** in KTU numericals, not psi.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Component-wise Engineering Breakdown

#### A. Cylinder Block (Engine Block)

- **Function:** The cylinder block is the **main structural framework** of the engine. It houses the cylinders, supports the crankshaft via main bearings, supports the camshaft (in OHV engines), and contains water jackets for coolant circulation.
- **Material:** **Cast Iron (CI)** for heavy-duty diesel engines (high wear resistance) and **Aluminum Alloy (Al-Si alloy, e.g., A356)** for modern light-weight petrol engines.
- **Construction:** In-line (4/6 cylinders), V-type (V6/V8), or Boxer (horizontally opposed). The block is cast as a single unit with sand casting or pressure die casting.
- **Key Features:** Cylinder bores, coolant passages, oil passages, main bearing saddles, crankcase, mounting bosses, and head bolt holes.

#### B. Cylinder Head

- **Function:** Closes the cylinder from the top, houses the **valves, spark plug (SI), injector (CI), and combustion chamber**.
- **Material:** Cast iron (older designs) or **aluminum alloy** (modern — better heat dissipation, ~3x faster heat transfer).
- **Types:**
  - **I-Head (Overhead Valve - OHV):** Valves in head, camshaft in block. Pushrods used.
  - **F-Head:** Inlet in head, exhaust in block side.
  - **T-Head:** Two separate camshafts, valves on both sides.

#### C. Oil Sump (Crankcase / Oil Pan)

- **Function:** Lower enclosure of the engine that serves as a **reservoir for lubricating oil** and houses the crankshaft's lower half (in wet sump) or acts only as a structural member (in dry sump).
- **Material:** Pressed steel sheet, cast aluminum, or stamped composite plastic.
- **Capacity:** Typically **4 to 8 litres** for a 4-cylinder engine.

#### D. Cylinder Liner (Sleeve)

- **Function:** A replaceable hollow cylindrical liner inserted into the cylinder block to provide a **wearing surface** for the piston rings.
- **Types:**
  - **Wet Liner:** Surrounded by coolant on its outer surface.
  - **Dry Liner:** Cast directly into the block, no contact with coolant.
- **Material:** **Pearlitic Cast Iron** with **0.2%–0.4% Phosphorus** for hardness, sometimes **Ni-Cr coated**.

#### E. Piston

- **Function:** Reciprocates inside the cylinder, transmits the force of combustion gases to the connecting rod via the piston pin, and seals the combustion gases via piston rings.
- **Material:** **Aluminum Alloy (Al-Si-Cu-Ni alloy)** — lightweight, good thermal conductivity, low inertia.
- **Typical Dimensions:** Crown thickness **6–15 mm**, barrel wall thickness **3–8 mm**, piston skirt length **0.6 × Stroke**.
- **Crown Shapes:** Flat, Domed, Bowl-in-crown (diesel), Heron bowl (indirect injection).

#### F. Piston Rings

- **Function:** Seal combustion gases (compression ring), scrape oil back to sump (oil control ring), and transfer heat from piston to cylinder wall.
- **Types per piston:**
  - **Compression Ring (2 per piston):** Upper ring usually **chrome-plated**, second ring sometimes **tapered or napier**.
  - **Oil Control Ring (1 per piston):** Multi-piece expander type with drain holes.

#### G. Piston Pin (Gudgeon Pin / Wrist Pin)

- **Function:** Connects the piston to the small end of the connecting rod. It is a **hollow cylindrical pin** subjected to oscillating and bending stresses.
- **Diameter:** Typically **0.28 × Bore** (e.g., for 80 mm bore → pin diameter ≈ 22.4 mm).
- **Material:** **Case-hardened low-carbon steel (e.g., 16MnCr5)**, surface hardened to **58–62 HRC**.

#### H. Connecting Rod

- **Function:** Connects the piston pin (small end) to the crankshaft journal (big end) and converts reciprocating motion into rotary motion.
- **Length-to-Crank Radius Ratio (L/r):** Typically **3.5 to 4.5**. Larger L/r → better secondary force balance, more vertical piston motion.
- **Material:** **Forged steel (C45, 40Cr)** or **powder-forged steel** for high-performance engines.
- **Cross-Section:** I-section (most common), H-section (for high stress), or rectangular.

#### I. Crankshaft

- **Function:** Converts reciprocating motion of pistons into rotary motion; carries the flywheel and drives the camshaft (via timing belt/chain/gears).
- **Components:** Main journals, crank pins (rod journals), crank webs (cheeks), counterweights, and front/rear seals.
- **Material:** **Forged steel (C40, C45)** or **ductile cast iron (60-40-18)**.
- **Firing Order:** Common 4-cylinder = **1-3-4-2** (most used for in-line 4).

#### J. Valve Train Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Intake Valve** | Admits air (CI) or air-fuel mixture (SI) into cylinder |
| **Exhaust Valve** | Expels burnt combustion gases |
| **Camshaft** | Controls valve timing and lift profile |
| **Rocker Arm** | Pivot lever amplifying cam lobe motion |
| **Push Rod** | Transmits motion from cam follower to rocker arm (OHV only) |
| **Valve Spring** | Returns valve to closed position |
| **Tappet / Lifter** | Follows cam profile and lifts push rod |
| **Timing Belt/Chain** | Synchronizes camshaft with crankshaft rotation (1:2 ratio) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valve Timing Diagram:** For KTU 2024 ESE, students must be able to draw and explain the **valve timing diagram for a 4-stroke SI engine**, including Intake Valve Opens (IVO), Intake Valve Closes (IVC), Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO), Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC), and the various valve overlap/lag angles in **degrees of crank rotation**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swept Volume ($V_s$) | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | m³ or cc | — |
| 2 | Clearance Volume ($V_c$) | $V_c = \dfrac{V_s}{r - 1}$ | m³ or cc | — |
| 3 | Total Cylinder Volume ($V_1$) | $V_1 = V_s + V_c$ | m³ or cc | — |
| 4 | Compression Ratio (r) | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | 8:1 to 22:1 |
| 5 | Piston Speed ($V_p$) | $V_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ | m/s | ≤ 20 m/s |
| 6 | Mean Piston Speed | $\bar{V_p} = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ | m/s | — |
| 7 | Crank Angle (θ) | $\theta = \omega t = \dfrac{2 \pi N t}{60}$ | rad | — |
| 8 | Inertia Force on Piston | $F_I = m \cdot r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | N | — |
| 9 | Piston Acceleration | $a = r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | m/s² | — |
| 10 | Connecting Rod Ratio (n) | $n = \dfrac{L}{r}$ | dimensionless | 3.5 to 4.5 |
| 11 | Gas Force on Piston | $F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot P_g$ | N | — |
| 12 | Pin Diameter (empirical) | $d_p \approx 0.28 D$ | mm | — |
| 13 | Engine Displacement | $V_d = \text{No. of cylinders} \times V_s$ | cc or L | 800–5000 cc |
| 14 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | 0.25–0.4 |
| 15 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | % | 25–40 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Inertia Force Formula:**
> - θ = 0° → TDC during power stroke → Maximum gas force.
> - θ = 180° → BDC → Gas force ≈ atmospheric pressure (during intake).
> - n = L/r → For infinite connecting rod (n → ∞), inertia force reduces to $F_I = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ (simple harmonic motion).

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Block** determines the engine's rigidity, NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) behavior, and overall vehicle weight.
- **Piston design** is critical in **Formula 1 engines** where RPM exceeds 18,000 and piston acceleration can exceed **10,000 g**.
- **Crankshaft balancing** (static and dynamic) prevents destructive torsional vibrations that can fracture the shaft at critical speeds.
- **Valve timing** (via VVT — Variable Valve Timing) is the key technology behind **Toyota VVT-i, Honda i-VTEC, and BMW Valvetronic**, directly impacting fuel efficiency and emissions.
- **Connecting Rod** must withstand tensile, compressive, and buckling loads — typically designed with a **factor of safety of 3 to 5**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Piston Acceleration and Inertia Force

This is the **most critical KTU derivation** from Module 1 — it appears in nearly every ESE question bank under "IC Engine Kinematics" or "Engine Component Design."

#### Given Data:
- Crank radius = **r** (m)
- Connecting rod length = **L** (m)
- Crank angle from TDC = **θ** (rad)
- Piston mass = **m** (kg)
- Angular velocity = **ω** (rad/s)
- Rod ratio **n = L/r**

#### Step 1: Position of Piston from TDC (x)

Let the crank rotate by angle θ from TDC. The piston displacement **x** from TDC is given by:

$$x = r \left(1 - \cos\theta\right) + \dfrac{r}{n} \left(1 - \sqrt{n^2 - \sin^2\theta}\right)$$

**Geometric Origin:**
- Component of crank radius along cylinder axis = $r \cos\theta$
- Component of connecting rod along cylinder axis = $\sqrt{L^2 - (r \sin\theta)^2} = r \sqrt{n^2 - \sin^2\theta}$
- Net displacement from TDC = $(r + L) - (r \cos\theta + r \sqrt{n^2 - \sin^2\theta})$
- Simplifying: $x = r (1 - \cos\theta) + r \left(1 - \sqrt{n^2 - \sin^2\theta}\right)/n$

#### Step 2: Apply Taylor Series Approximation

For $\dfrac{r}{L} = \dfrac{1}{n} \ll 1$, expand $\sqrt{n^2 - \sin^2\theta} = n \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{n^2}}$

$$\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - \dfrac{x}{2} - \dfrac{x^2}{8} - \dots$$

Substituting (retaining only the first two terms):

$$\sqrt{n^2 - \sin^2\theta} \approx n - \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{2n} - \dfrac{\sin^4\theta}{8 n^3} - \dots$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{1}{n}\left(n - \sqrt{n^2 - \sin^2\theta}\right) \approx \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{2 n^2} = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{4n}$$

Substituting back:

$$x \approx r \left(1 - \cos\theta\right) + \dfrac{r}{4n} \left(1 - \cos 2\theta\right)$$

#### Step 3: Differentiate Twice to Get Piston Acceleration

Velocity: $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \omega \dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$

$$v = r \omega \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$$

Acceleration: $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \omega^2 \dfrac{d^2 x}{d\theta^2}$

$$a = r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The acceleration has two harmonic components — a primary (ω²) term and a secondary (2ω²) term. The secondary term becomes negligible only when n → ∞ (infinite connecting rod).

#### Step 4: Inertia Force on Piston

By Newton's Second Law, the inertia force (opposing motion) on the piston is:

$$F_I = m \cdot a = m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

This force is **directed opposite to acceleration**, i.e., along the cylinder axis.

#### Step 5: Directional Sign Convention

For **TDC**: θ = 0° → $\cos 0° = 1$ → **a is maximum positive** (piston about to decelerate).
- $a_{max} = r \omega^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$

For **BDC**: θ = 180° → $\cos 180° = -1$, $\cos 360° = 1$
- $a_{BDC} = r \omega^2 \left(-1 + \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$ (negative → acceleration is upward)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example for KTU Practice:**
> Given: $r = 5$ cm, $L = 20$ cm, $m = 1$ kg, $N = 3000$ rpm. Find inertia force at TDC and BDC.
> - ω = $2\pi N / 60 = 314.16$ rad/s
> - $n = L/r = 4$
> - $F_I \text{ (TDC)} = 1 \times 0.05 \times (314.16)^2 \times (1 + 1/4) = 1 \times 0.05 \times 98,696 \times 1.25 \approx 6168$ N
> - $F_I \text{ (BDC)} = 1 \times 0.05 \times 98,696 \times (-1 + 1/4) = -3699$ N (directed downward)

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for IC Engine Components

| Component | Primary Failure Mode | Stress Type | Material | Surface Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cylinder Block** | Thermal fatigue, vibration | Static + Cyclic | Cast Iron GG-25 / Al-Si alloy | T6 heat treatment |
| **Cylinder Head** | Thermal stress, warping | Thermal cyclic | Al-Si alloy A356 / CI | T6 precipitation hardening |
| **Cylinder Liner** | Abrasive wear | Friction + wear | Pearlitic CI + Phosphorus | Honing (Ra ≈ 0.4 μm) |
| **Piston** | Thermal fatigue, scuffing | Cyclic + Thermal | Al-Si alloy (12–18% Si) | Anodizing, graphite coating |
| **Piston Ring** | Wear, fatigue | Friction + Cyclic | Cast Iron / Steel | Chrome plating, PVD |
| **Piston Pin** | Bending fatigue | Cyclic | Low-carbon alloy steel | Case hardening (carburizing) |
| **Connecting Rod** | Buckling, fatigue | Tensile + Compressive | Forged C45 / 40Cr | Shot peening, nitriding |
| **Crankshaft** | Bending + Torsional fatigue | Rotating bending | Forged steel / SG iron | Induction hardening at journals |
| **Valves** | Thermal fatigue, corrosion | Cyclic thermal | Inconel / Austenitic SS | Stem nitriding |
| **Camshaft** | Surface fatigue, wear | Hertzian contact | Chilled CI / Forged steel | Surface hardening |

---

### 3.3 Force on Piston — Gas Force vs. Inertia Force (Analytical Comparison)

**Net force on the piston** (along cylinder axis, downward positive):

$$F_{net} = F_g - F_I = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 P_g - m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

- $F_g$ = Force due to combustion gas pressure (acts downward during power stroke).
- $F_I$ = Inertia force (acts downward during downward acceleration, upward during upward acceleration).
- During **compression stroke**, both gas resistance and inertia resist piston motion.
- During **power stroke**, gas pressure assists motion, inertia resists motion.

> [!NOTE]
> This net force is then resolved at the **crank pin** into a **tangential component** (producing torque) and a **radial component** (producing side thrust on bearings). This resolution forms the basis for **crankshaft torque calculation** in KTU Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IC Engine Cross-Section — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Below is a Mermaid block diagram mapping the **functional interaction** between all IC Engine components discussed in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stationary["STATIONARY COMPONENTS"]
        CB[Cylinder Block]
        CH[Cylinder Head]
        CS[Combustion Chamber]
        OS[Oil Sump]
        CL[Cylinder Liner]
    end

    subgraph Reciprocating["RECIPROCATING COMPONENTS"]
        P[Piston]
        PR1[Compression Ring 1]
        PR2[Compression Ring 2]
        OR[Oil Control Ring]
        PP[Piston Pin Gudgeon]
        CR[Connecting Rod]
    end

    subgraph Rotating["ROTATING COMPONENTS"]
        CK[Crankshaft]
        FW[Flywheel]
        CM[Camshaft]
    end

    subgraph ValveTrain["VALVE TRAIN COMPONENTS"]
        IV[Intake Valve]
        EV[Exhaust Valve]
        RA[Rocker Arm]
        PSR[Push Rod]
        SP[Valve Spring]
        TP[Tappet]
    end

    CB --- CL
    CB --- OS
    CB --- CK
    CH --- CS
    CH --- IV
    CH --- EV
    CH --- RA
    RA --- PSR
    PSR --- TP
    TP --- CM
    CM --- CK
    P --- PR1
    P --- PR2
    P --- OR
    P --- PP
    PP --- CR
    CR --- CK
    CK --- FW

    style Stationary fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366
    style Reciprocating fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#663300
    style Rotating fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style ValveTrain fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows and connecting lines indicate the **mechanical coupling** between components. The colored subgraphs correspond to the four functional groups — stationary, reciprocating, rotating, and valve train — exactly as classified in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### 4.2 4-Stroke SI Engine Valve Timing Diagram (Schematic Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Intake["INTAKE STROKE"]
        A1[IVO 5 to 10 BTDC]
        A2[Intake Valve OPEN]
        A3[IVC 40 to 50 ABDC]
    end

    subgraph Compression["COMPRESSION STROKE"]
        B1[Intake Valve CLOSED]
        B2[Piston moves UP]
        B3[Pressure rises]
    end

    subgraph Power["POWER STROKE"]
        C1[Spark at 10 to 15 BTDC]
        C2[Combustion]
        C3[EVO 40 to 50 BBDC]
    end

    subgraph Exhaust["EXHAUST STROKE"]
        D1[Exhaust Valve OPEN]
        D2[Piston moves UP]
        D3[EVC 5 to 10 ATDC]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    A3 -. Valve Overlap .-> D1

    style Intake fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style Compression fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style Power fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style Exhaust fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> **KTU Note:** The **valve overlap** period (between IVC and EVO) is critical — both intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously for a brief moment, allowing better scavenging of exhaust gases and improved volumetric efficiency at high RPM.

---

### 4.3 Crank-Connecting Rod-Piston Kinematic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Kinematics["CRANK SLIDER MECHANISM"]
        CRANK[Crankshaft rotates omega]
        PIN[Crank Pin at radius r]
        ROD[Connecting Rod of length L]
        PISTON[Piston moves linearly]
        CYL[Cylinder Fixed]
    end

    CRANK --> PIN
    PIN --> ROD
    ROD --> PISTON
    PISTON --> CYL
    CYL -. Reaction Force .-> PISTON

    style Kinematics fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

**Mechanism Logic:**
1. Crankshaft rotates at constant angular velocity ω.
2. Crank pin traces a circular path of radius r.
3. Connecting rod translates rotary motion to linear reciprocation of piston.
4. Piston travels between TDC and BDC inside the fixed cylinder.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a piston pin? State any two materials used for its manufacturing.

**Model Answer:**

A **piston pin (also called gudgeon pin or wrist pin)** is a cylindrical hollow pin that connects the **piston** to the **small end of the connecting rod**, allowing relative angular motion between them.

**Functions:**
- Acts as a pivot point between piston and connecting rod.
- Transmits the combustion force from the piston to the connecting rod.

**Materials Used:**
1. **Case-hardened low-carbon alloy steel** (e.g., 16MnCr5, 20MnCr5) — surface hardened to 58–62 HRC.
2. **Nitriding steel** (e.g., 31CrMoV9) — for high-performance applications.

> **Key Properties Required:** High fatigue strength, wear resistance, dimensional accuracy, and a surface finish of **0.2 μm Ra or better**.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between a wet liner and a dry liner used in IC engines.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wet Liner | Dry Liner |
|---|---|---|
| **Coolant Contact** | Outer surface in direct contact with coolant | No direct contact with coolant; pressed into block |
| **Heat Transfer** | Superior — better cooling of cylinder wall | Inferior — heat passes through block metal |
| **Replacement** | Easily replaceable | Difficult to replace |
| **Sealing** | Requires O-rings at top and bottom to prevent coolant leakage | No special sealing required |
| **Casting Complexity** | Block casting is simpler | Block requires precise bore machining |
| **Applications** | Heavy-duty diesel engines (trucks, buses) | Light-duty petrol engines |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and function of a piston** used in a 4-stroke SI engine. List any **four functions** of piston rings. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **expression for acceleration of a piston** in an IC engine. Discuss the effect of **connecting rod length (n = L/r)** on the acceleration. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (a)**

**Construction of a Piston:**

The piston is the cylindrical component that reciprocates inside the cylinder bore. Its major parts include:

1. **Piston Crown (Head):** Top surface exposed to combustion gases. Shaped to promote proper combustion (flat, dome, or bowl).
2. **Piston Ring Grooves:** Machined circumferential grooves (typically 3 per piston — 2 for compression rings, 1 for oil control ring).
3. **Piston Skirt:** Lower cylindrical portion that provides guidance and prevents contact with cylinder wall. Often **slipper skirt** type to reduce friction.
4. **Piston Pin Bore:** Hole through the piston bosses for the gudgeon pin. Offset by **1–2 mm** toward the thrust side to reduce slap noise.
5. **Piston Bosses:** Thickened regions around the pin bore to provide structural support.

**Materials:** Aluminum-Silicon alloys (Al-12% Si or Al-18% Si with Cu, Ni, Mg additions). The silicon content provides:
- Low coefficient of thermal expansion.
- High wear resistance.
- Good thermal conductivity (~150 W/m·K).

**Four Functions of Piston Rings:**
1. **Seal combustion gases** — prevent leakage of high-pressure gases past the piston (compression ring).
2. **Control oil consumption** — scrape excess oil from cylinder wall back to sump (oil control ring).
3. **Transfer heat** — conduct heat from piston crown to cylinder wall (~30–40% of total heat dissipation).
4. **Maintain piston position** — keep piston centered in cylinder, preventing metal-to-metal contact.

**Sketch:** (refer to Section 4 Mermaid diagram — node P represents piston with rings PR1, PR2, OR)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (b)**

**Derivation of Piston Acceleration:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the complete step-by-step derivation. The key steps are:

1. Piston displacement from TDC:
$$x = r \left(1 - \cos\theta\right) + \dfrac{r}{4n} \left(1 - \cos 2\theta\right)$$

2. Velocity (differentiating w.r.t. time):
$$v = r\omega \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$$

3. Acceleration (second derivative):
$$a = r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

**Effect of Connecting Rod Length (n = L/r):**

- **Primary acceleration component** ($r\omega^2 \cos\theta$) is **independent of n**.
- **Secondary acceleration component** ($r\omega^2 \cos 2\theta / n$) **decreases as n increases**.
- For **n → ∞** (infinite rod), secondary component vanishes → pure simple harmonic motion (SHM). This is the idealized case assumed in basic kinematics.
- For **n = 4** (typical automotive value), secondary component is ~25% of primary — significant enough to be considered in design.
- For **n = 3 to 3.5** (high-performance engines), secondary component is larger — up to ~33%, causing higher secondary shaking forces.
- **Trade-off:** Shorter connecting rod → more compact engine but higher vibration. Longer rod → smoother operation but taller engine block.

**Final Formula for Inertia Force:**

$$F_I = m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right) \quad \text{[Newtons]}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]

**(a)** Describe the **components of a valve train** in an OHV (Overhead Valve) engine with a neat sketch. Explain the **valve timing diagram** for a 4-stroke SI engine. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **construction and function of a crankshaft**. List the **firing order** for a 4-cylinder in-line engine and explain its significance. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (a)**

**Components of OHV Valve Train** (refer to Section 4 Mermaid diagram, subgraph ValveTrain):

1. **Camshaft** — Rotates at half engine speed (1:2 ratio via timing chain/belt). Has cam lobes for each valve.
2. **Tappet (Lifter)** — Sits on the cam lobe, moves up when cam lifts.
3. **Push Rod** — Hollow steel tube connecting tappet to rocker arm (transmits motion across the cylinder head/block boundary).
4. **Rocker Arm** — Pivoted lever that converts the upward push rod motion into downward valve opening motion.
5. **Valve** — Mushroom-shaped poppet valve with stem, head, and seat.
6. **Valve Spring** — Helical spring that closes the valve when cam lobe passes.

**Valve Timing Diagram for 4-Stroke SI Engine:**

| Event | Crank Angle (Typical) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **IVO (Intake Valve Opens)** | 5°–10° BTDC | Intake opens before TDC to use ram effect |
| **IVC (Intake Valve Closes)** | 40°–50° ABDC | Closes after BDC to use inertia of incoming charge |
| **EVO (Exhaust Valve Opens)** | 40°–50° BBDC | Opens before BDC to release exhaust gases |
| **EVC (Exhaust Valve Closes)** | 5°–10° ATDC | Closes after TDC for better scavenging |
| **Valve Overlap** | ~10°–20° | Both intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously |

**Significance:** Valve overlap allows the **scavenging of exhaust gases** and improves **volumetric efficiency** at high RPM, but at low RPM/load it can increase HC emissions.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (b)**

**Crankshaft Construction:**

The crankshaft is the main rotating member of the engine. Its key parts:

1. **Main Journals** — Supported by main bearings in the cylinder block. Typically **2 main bearings** (end journals) plus intermediate bearings (e.g., 5-main-bearing crankshaft for inline-4).
2. **Crank Pins (Rod Journals)** — Offset from the main axis by radius r; the connecting rods are mounted on these.
3. **Crank Webs (Cheeks)** — Connect main journals to crank pins; carry counterweights.
4. **Counterweights** — Opposite to crank pins to balance the rotating mass and reduce bearing loads.
5. **Front End** — Drives the camshaft (via gear/belt/chain), water pump, and alternator.
6. **Rear End** — Mounts the flywheel (manual transmission) or flexplate (automatic).

**Firing Order for 4-Cylinder Inline Engine:**

Most common: **1 — 3 — 4 — 2**

**Significance:**
- Ensures **even power delivery** — one cylinder fires every 180° of crankshaft rotation.
- Provides **dynamic balance** by alternating firing between front and rear cylinder pairs.
- Reduces **primary and secondary rocking couples** in the engine.
- Avoids resonance in the crankshaft torsional vibrations.
- Improves **engine smoothness** and NVH characteristics.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deduction in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Missing "Units" in Numerical Answers:** A formula like $F_I = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ without units (N) earns only **2 out of 3 marks** in derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting Sign Convention:** Inertia force is **opposite to acceleration direction** — many students write $F = +ma$ instead of $F = -ma$, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Not Labeling the Sketches:** The KTU examiner strictly awards marks for **labeled diagrams**. A piston diagram without "piston rings," "gudgeon pin," "skirt," "crown" labels loses up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing IVO with TDC:** Many students mark IVO *exactly at* TDC. Always mention the **5°–10° BTDC** lead angle.
> 5. **Mixing Up OHV and OHC:** OHC (Overhead Cam) engines do **not** have push rods. OHV engines do. Confusing these = **1–2 mark loss**.
> 6. **Neglecting "n" in Piston Acceleration:** Writing only $a = r\omega^2 \cos\theta$ without the secondary term $\frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}$ results in **incomplete derivation** — KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Missing Engineering Units:** Always state **bore in mm, stroke in mm, force in N, pressure in bar** — mixing units (e.g., m and cm) is a common 1-mark loss.
> 8. **Not Mentioning Material Properties:** For component-based questions, KTU expects at least **one material name** with a key property (e.g., "Al-Si alloy, lightweight with good thermal conductivity") — vague answers lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — IC Engine Components:**

#### 🔧 Stationary Components
- ✅ **Cylinder Block** — main structural member; houses cylinders, crankshaft, camshaft; made of CI or Al-Si alloy.
- ✅ **Cylinder Head** — closes top of cylinder; houses valves, spark plug/injector; made of Al-alloy for better heat transfer.
- ✅ **Oil Sump** — lubricant reservoir; wet sump vs. dry sump types.
- ✅ **Cylinder Liner** — wet liner (cooling water contact) vs. dry liner (no contact); material = pearlitic CI + Phosphorus.

#### ⚙️ Reciprocating Components
- ✅ **Piston** — Al-Si alloy (12–18% Si); crown, ring grooves, skirt, pin boss; bowl-in-crown for diesel.
- ✅ **Piston Rings** — 2 compression rings + 1 oil control ring per piston; chrome-plated upper ring.
- ✅ **Piston Pin (Gudgeon)** — Hollow cylindrical pin; diameter ≈ 0.28 × Bore; case-hardened alloy steel.
- ✅ **Connecting Rod** — I-section forged steel; n = L/r = 3.5–4.5; small end to piston pin, big end to crank pin.

#### 🔁 Rotating Components
- ✅ **Crankshaft** — forged steel or SG iron; main journals + crank pins + counterweights; firing order 1-3-4-2 for inline-4.
- ✅ **Flywheel** — stores rotational inertia; mounted on rear of crankshaft.

#### 🎯 Valve Train
- ✅ **OHV vs. OHC vs. DOHC** — knows the difference; push rods present only in OHV.
- ✅ **Cam profile** — controls lift, duration, and timing of valve events.
- ✅ **Valve timing diagram** — IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC, overlap angles; all in degrees of crank rotation.
- ✅ **VVT (Variable Valve Timing)** — modern technology; Honda i-VTEC, Toyota VVT-i, BMW Valvetronic.

#### 📐 Critical Formulas
- ✅ Piston acceleration: $a = r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$
- ✅ Inertia force: $F_I = m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$
- ✅ Swept volume: $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$
- ✅ Compression ratio: $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$
- ✅ Piston pin diameter: $d_p \approx 0.28 D$
- ✅ Piston speed: $V_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ m/s ≤ 20 m/s

#### 🚗 Engineering Intuition
- ✅ Always link design to **real-world engineering**: aluminum blocks for weight reduction, forged steel crankshafts for strength, chrome rings for wear resistance, VVT for fuel economy.
- ✅ KTU values **application-based answers** — state the *why* behind each material, dimension, and design choice.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Practice drawing the **valve timing diagram** and **piston acceleration curve** (a vs. θ for 0° to 360°) — these two graphical questions alone account for **8–10 marks** in KTU Module 1 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion Process - A/F Ratio, Self-Ignition Temperature, Octane and Cetane Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Combustion Process - A/F Ratio, Self-Ignition Temperature, Octane and Cetane Number ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F Ratio) **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** The **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)** is defined as the mass of air supplied to the cylinder per unit mass of fuel during the induction stroke of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine. Mathematically: $$A/F = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$ - **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio:** The chemically ideal ratio at which all the fuel (C) and all the air (O₂) are completely consumed, leaving neither excess oxygen nor unburnt fuel. - **Theoretical values:** - Petrol (Gasoline): **14.7 : 1** - Diesel: **14.5 : 1** - Hydrogen: **34.3 : 1** - Methanol: **6.4 : 1** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equivalence Ratio (φ):** A normalized form of A/F ratio used in KTU derivations. > $$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$$ > - φ < 1 → **Lean mixture** (excess air) > - φ = 1 → **Stoichiometric mixture** > - φ > 1 → **Rich mixture** (excess fuel) **Intuitive Analogy — The Campfire Logic:** Imagine lighting a campfire. If you place one large log on a tiny match, the log will not burn completely (it is *rich* — too much fuel, not enough air). Conversely, if you blow a huge gust of wind on a small piece of paper, the paper burns quickly and the wind just carries away unreacted air (it is *lean* — too much air). The **stoichiometric A/F** is the "Goldilocks" condition where the fuel and air are perfectly matched — like a well-tuned campfire that produces maximum heat with no smoke and no wasted wood. --- ### 1.2 Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT) / Auto-Ignition Temperature **Formal Definition:** The **Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT)** is the **minimum temperature** to which a solid, liquid, or gaseous fuel must be heated **without any external spark or flame** to initiate self-sustained combustion. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):** > SIT ≠ Flash Point ≠ Fire Point > - **Flash Point:** Lowest temperature at which vapours ignite momentarily on application of a flame. > - **Fire Point:** Lowest temperature at which vapours sustain combustion for ≥ 5 seconds. > - **SIT:** Ignition **without** any external flame — purely by thermal energy. **Typical SIT Values (Examinable):** | Fuel | Self-Ignition Temperature (°C) | | :--- | :---: | | n-Heptane (RON 0) | **223** | | Iso-Octane (RON 100) | **418** | | n-Cetane (CN 100) | **215** | | α-Methylnaphthalene (CN 0) | **565** | | Petrol (Gasoline) | **280 – 456** | | Diesel | **210** | | Methane | **537** | | Hydrogen | **500** | **Intuitive Analogy — The Pressure Cooker:** Think of a pressure cooker on a stove. The flame heats the vessel; eventually, the inner surface becomes so hot that the food particles touching the wall start **burning on their own** — no spark needed. This "spontaneous catching fire" is auto-ignition. In a diesel engine, the SIT is what allows fuel to ignite the moment it contacts the superheated compressed air. --- ### 1.3 Octane Number (ON) **Formal Definition:** The **Octane Number** of a fuel is the **percentage of iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) by volume** in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane (C₇H₁₆) that has the **same knocking tendency** as the fuel under test, when evaluated in a standard CFR engine. - **Iso-octane:** Assigned ON = 100 (highly knock-resistant) - **n-Heptane:** Assigned ON = 0 (highly knock-prone) **Two Engine Test Conditions:** | Test Method | Engine Speed | Intake Air Temp | Typical KTU Use | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **RON** (Research Octane Number) | 600 rpm | 25 °C | **City driving** | | **MON** (Motor Octane Number) | 900 rpm | 152 °C | **Highway/load** | | **(R+M)/2** Posted at pumps | — | — | **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** | --- ### 1.4 Cetane Number (CN) **Formal Definition:** The **Cetane Number** is the **percentage of n-cetane (C₁₆H₃₄) by volume** in a mixture of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene that has the **same ignition delay** as the test fuel in a standard CI engine. - **n-Cetane:** Assigned CN = 100 (very short ignition delay, ignites rapidly) - **α-Methylnaphthalene:** Assigned CN = 0 (very long ignition delay, hard to ignite) - **Typical diesel fuel:** CN = **45 – 55** **Intuitive Analogy — The Slow vs. Quick Matchstick:** Imagine two matchsticks. One has a thick head that takes 5 seconds to flare up (α-methylnaphthalene), and the other flares up in 0.1 seconds (n-cetane). Diesel fuel should flare up **very quickly** when injected into hot compressed air. The higher the cetane number, the "quicker-flaring" the fuel. Slow-flaring fuels cause **diesel knock** because too much fuel accumulates before ignition. --- ### 1.5 Inverse Octane-Cetane Relationship > [!IMPORTANT] > **A fundamental KTU question (asked almost every cycle):** > "Explain why a high cetane number fuel has a low octane number, and vice versa." > **Reason:** A high-CN fuel (e.g., long straight-chain paraffins like n-cetane) has a **low SIT** — it self-ignites easily. This is **excellent for CI engines** but **terrible for SI engines** (causes knock). A high-ON fuel (e.g., branched aromatics like iso-octane) has a **high SIT** — it resists auto-ignition, ideal for SI engines, but **cannot self-ignite reliably in a CI engine**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Stoichiometric Combustion Logic (Step-by-Step) For a generic hydrocarbon fuel **CₓHᵧ** burned with atmospheric air (O₂ + 3.76 N₂): 1. **Oxygen balance** — one mole of C needs 1 mole O₂; one mole of H needs 0.25 mole O₂. 2. **Nitrogen carrier** — every mole of O₂ is accompanied by 3.76 moles of N₂ (since air is 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume). 3. **Total air required per mole fuel:** (x + y/4) moles of air mixture. 4. **Mass of air** = moles of air mixture × molecular weight of air (M_air = 29 kg/kmol). 5. **Mass of fuel** = 12x + y (using atomic masses C = 12, H = 1). 6. **Compute the mass ratio.** ### 2.2 Why Equivalence Ratio (φ) Matters in Real Engines | Operating Regime | Equivalence Ratio (φ) | λ = 1/φ | SI Engine Behaviour | CI Engine Behaviour | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | Very Rich | 1.3 – 1.5 | 0.67 – 0.77 | **Maximum power** (full throttle) | Smoke, unburnt C | | Rich (best power) | 1.10 | 0.91 | **Peak power, low knock limit** | Marginal | | Stoichiometric | 1.00 | 1.00 | **3-way catalyst operation** | Standard benchmark | | Lean (best economy) | 0.88 | 1.14 | **Maximum fuel economy** | — | | Very Lean | 0.6 – 0.8 | 1.25 – 1.67 | Misfire, high HC emissions | — | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory formulas for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the structure, not just the final number.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | | 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $(A/F) = m_{air} / m_{fuel}$ | m_air, m_fuel | kg/kg | | 2 | Fuel-Air Ratio (mass) | $(F/A) = 1 / (A/F)$ | Inverse of A/F | kg/kg | | 3 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = (F/A)_{act} / (F/A)_{stoich}$ | Ratio of ratios | dimensionless | | 4 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = 1/\phi = (A/F)_{act} / (A/F)_{stoich}$ | Inverse equivalence | dimensionless | | 5 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand | $n_{O_2} = x + y/4$ | For CₓHᵧ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | | 6 | Stoichiometric air demand | $n_{air} = 4.76(x + y/4)$ | 4.76 = 1 + 3.76 | kmol air / kmol fuel | | 7 | Mass of air per kg fuel | $m_{air} = 4.76 \cdot 29 \cdot (x + y/4)$ | M_air = 29 | kg air / kmol fuel | | 8 | Mass of fuel per kmol | $m_{fuel} = 12x + y$ | Atomic masses C=12, H=1 | kg / kmol | | 9 | Stoichiometric A/F | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{138.04 \cdot (x + y/4)}{12x + y}$ | Universal | kg air / kg fuel | | 10 | Heat of combustion (Dulong) | $GCV = 33800\,C + 144000\,(H - O/8) + 9410\,S$ | Mass fractions of C,H,O,S | kJ/kg | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Catalytic Converter Tuning:** Modern 3-way catalytic converters only work efficiently at **φ ≈ 1 ± 0.01**. The lambda sensor (O₂ sensor) continuously reports λ to the ECU for closed-loop A/F control. - **Diesel Common-Rail Systems:** Multiple injections (pilot, main, post) are timed to manage **in-cylinder pressure rise rate** — directly tied to cetane number. - **Octane Requirement Increase (ORI):** Engines accumulate deposits over time that raise their effective octane requirement. The fuel must always be specified with a margin of ≥ 3 RON above the engine's ORI. - **Alternative Fuels:** Biodiesel (CN ≈ 50–55) vs. petrodiesel (CN ≈ 45–50). Hydrogen (CN ≈ 0) is unsuitable for CI without ignition improvers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for a Generic Hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ **Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation.** $$C_x H_y + n\left(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2\right) \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + 3.76\,n\,N_2$$ **Step 2 — Balance the oxygen atoms.** On the LHS, oxygen is supplied only as O₂, giving 2n atoms. On the RHS, oxygen is bound in CO₂ (2x atoms) and H₂O (y atoms). $$2n = 2x + \frac{y}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for n (moles of O₂ per mole of fuel).** $$n = x + \frac{y}{4}$$ **Step 4 — Determine the moles of air required.** Since 1 mole of O₂ is accompanied by 3.76 moles of N₂, 1 mole of O₂ corresponds to (1 + 3.76) = 4.76 moles of air. $$n_{air} = 4.76 \times n = 4.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Compute the mass of air per mole of fuel.** The molecular weight of air M_air = 28.97 ≈ 29 kg/kmol. $$m_{air} = n_{air} \times M_{air} = 4.76 \times 29 \times \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)$$ $$m_{air} = 138.04\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \quad \text{[kg air per kmol fuel]}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the mass of fuel per mole.** $$m_{fuel} = 12x + y \quad \text{[kg per kmol]}$$ **Step 7 — Form the A/F ratio.** $$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{138.04\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)}{12x + y}$$ --- ### 3.2 Numerical Evaluation: Stoichiometric A/F for Iso-Octane C₈H₁₈ For iso-octane: **x = 8, y = 18** **Step 1 — O₂ requirement:** $$n_{O_2} = 8 + \frac{18}{4} = 8 + 4.5 = 12.5 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 2 — Air requirement:** $$n_{air} = 4.76 \times 12.5 = 59.5 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of air:** $$m_{air} = 59.5 \times 29 = 1725.5 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of fuel:** $$m_{fuel} = 12(8) + 18 = 96 + 18 = 114 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 5 — Final A/F ratio:** $$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{1725.5}{114} = 15.13 \quad (\text{kg air / kg fuel})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why petrol shows A/F = 14.7, not 15.13:** > Commercial gasoline is a *mixture* of hydrocarbons (paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics, olefins), not pure iso-octane. Its average empirical formula is approximately **CH₁.₈₅**, which yields A/F ≈ 14.7. KTU examiners accept **14.7 for petrol** and **15.13 for pure octane**. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Evaluation: Stoichiometric A/F for n-Cetane C₁₆H₃₄ For n-cetane: **x = 16, y = 34** **Step 1 — O₂ requirement:** $$n_{O_2} = 16 + \frac{34}{4} = 16 + 8.5 = 24.5 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of air:** $$m_{air} = 4.76 \times 24.5 \times 29 = 3381.1 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of fuel:** $$m_{fuel} = 12(16) + 34 = 192 + 34 = 226 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 4 — Final A/F ratio:** $$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{3381.1}{226} = 14.96 \approx 15.0 \quad (\text{kg air / kg fuel})$$ > [!NOTE] > Pure n-cetane gives ~15:1; commercial diesel (with aromatic content) gives **14.5:1** — the KTU standard. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of Standard Reference Fuels | Property | Iso-Octane | n-Heptane | n-Cetane | α-Methylnaphthalene | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Molecular formula | C₈H₁₈ | C₇H₁₆ | C₁₆H₃₄ | C₁₁H₁₀ | | Reference scale | Octane | Octane | Cetane | Cetane | | Assigned value | RON = 100, MON = 100 | RON = 0, MON = 0 | CN = 100 | CN = 0 | | Self-Ignition Temp. (°C) | **418** | **223** | **215** | **565** | | Knock resistance in SI | Excellent | Poor | Very poor | N/A | | Ignition delay in CI | N/A | N/A | Excellent | Very poor | | Structure | Branched | Straight chain | Straight chain | Aromatic ring | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Combustion Process & Fuel Property Interrelations ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Intake Air + Fuel] --> B{Mixture Preparation} B -->|SI Engine| C[Carburettor / Port Injection] B -->|CI Engine| D[Direct Injection into Cylinder] C --> E[Induction into Cylinder] D --> F[High Pressure Compression] E --> G[Compression Stroke SI] F --> H[Auto-Ignition via SIT] G --> I[Spark Ignition] I --> J[Combustion Quality] H --> J J --> K{Knock Analysis} K -->|SI Engine| L[Octane Number Check] K -->|CI Engine| M[Cetane Number Check] L --> N[RON / MON Test CFR Engine] M --> O[CN Test CFR Engine] N --> P[Anti-Knock Index AKI] O --> Q[Ignition Delay Measurement] P --> R[Engine Calibration] Q --> R R --> S[Emission Control ECU] S --> T[Lambda Sensor Feedback] T --> B AF[Air-Fuel Ratio Module] --> AF1[Stoichiometric phi = 1] AF1 --> AF2[Equivalence Ratio phi] AF2 --> AF3[Lambda Sensor lambda] AF3 --> J style A fill:#e1f5ff style J fill:#fff4e1 style K fill:#ffe1e1 style R fill:#e1ffe1 style AF fill:#f0e1ff ``` ### 4.2 Flowchart: Octane vs. Cetane Decision Logic in Engine Selection ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fuel Type] --> B{Hydrocarbon Structure?} B -->|Branched / Aromatic| C[High Octane Number] B -->|Long Straight Chain| D[High Cetane Number] B -->|Short Chain / Alcohol| E[Both moderate] C --> F{Self-Ignition Temperature?} D --> F E --> F F -->|High SIT > 350 deg C| G[Suitable for SI Engine] F -->|Low SIT < 250 deg C| H[Suitable for CI Engine] G --> I[Resists Auto-Ignition
Prevents Knock] H --> J[Fast Self-Ignition
Short Ignition Delay] I --> K[Higher Compression Ratio
Better Thermal Efficiency] J --> L[Smooth Combustion
Lower Noise] style A fill:#e1f5ff style G fill:#d4edda style H fill:#f8d7da style I fill:#d1ecf1 style J fill:#fff3cd ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: A/F Mixture Spectrum and Engine Performance Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "A/F RATIO SPECTRUM (Petrol SI Engine)" R1[Very Rich
A/F = 9 to 11
phi = 1.3 to 1.6] --> R2[Rich
A/F = 12 to 13
phi = 1.10] R2 --> R3[Stoichiometric
A/F = 14.7
phi = 1.0] R3 --> R4[Lean
A/F = 16 to 18
phi = 0.88] R4 --> R5[Very Lean
A/F = 19 to 21
phi = 0.7] end subgraph "ENGINE OUTCOME" O1[Peak Power
High CO, HC] --> O2[Best Power
Some Knock Risk] O2 --> O3[Best Catalyst
Balanced] O3 --> O4[Best Economy
Higher NOx] O4 --> O5[Misfire
High HC] end R1 -.- O1 R2 -.- O2 R3 -.- O3 R4 -.- O4 R5 -.- O5 style R3 fill:#d4edda style O3 fill:#d4edda ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q.A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define air-fuel ratio. State the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol and diesel engines. Explain why a diesel engine can operate at higher compression ratios than a petrol engine.* **Model Answer:** **Air-Fuel Ratio:** It is the ratio of the mass of air to the mass of fuel supplied to the engine cylinder. - Stoichiometric A/F of petrol: **14.7 : 1** - Stoichiometric A/F of diesel: **14.5 : 1** A diesel engine can operate at higher compression ratios (14:1 to 22:1) compared to a petrol engine (8:1 to 12:1) because diesel fuel has a **low self-ignition temperature (≈ 210 °C)** and high cetane number, enabling auto-ignition. Petrol has a high SIT (≈ 280–456 °C) and would knock at high compression ratios. Higher CR in diesels yields higher thermal efficiency (35–45%) versus petrol (25–35%). --- **Q.A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is meant by cetane number? How is it determined experimentally? Why is a high cetane number not desirable for SI engine fuels?* **Model Answer:** **Cetane Number (CN):** It is the percentage by volume of n-cetane (C₁₆H₃₄) in a reference mixture of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene that has the same ignition delay as the test fuel under standard CFR engine conditions. **Determination:** The test fuel is run in a single-cylinder, variable compression ratio CI engine. The compression ratio is adjusted until the fuel begins to knock. The reference fuel blend giving the same ignition delay determines the cetane number. A high cetane number indicates **low self-ignition temperature**, meaning the fuel auto-ignites easily. In an SI engine, this causes **premature auto-ignition** of the end-gas, producing the metallic pinging sound known as **engine knock**, which can damage pistons, spark plugs, and bearings. Hence SI engines require fuels with **low cetane number (high octane number)**. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Q.B1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of a hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Final answer: $$\boxed{\frac{A}{F}\bigg|_{stoich} = \frac{4.76 \times 29 \times \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)}{12x + y} = \frac{138.04\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)}{12x + y}}$$ **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** - Combustion equation with N₂ term — 1 mark - Oxygen balance (2n = 2x + y/2) — 1 mark - Air moles (4.76) — 1 mark - Mass of air (× 29) — 1 mark - Mass of fuel (12x + y) — 1 mark - Final ratio assembly — 2 marks **(b)** A sample of petrol has a volumetric analysis of 85% iso-octane and 15% n-heptane. Find the octane number of the blend. Why is the RON higher than the MON for most fuels? **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **Octane Number of the blend:** By definition, the octane number is the percentage of iso-octane in the reference mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that has the same knocking tendency. For the given blend: **85% iso-octane ⇒ Octane Number = 85.** **Why RON > MON for most fuels:** - RON is measured at a **lower engine speed (600 rpm)** and **lower intake temperature (25 °C)**, with a variable compression ratio. - MON is measured at a **higher engine speed (900 rpm)** and **higher intake temperature (152 °C)**, with advanced ignition timing. The higher speed and temperature in MON testing causes **greater end-gas auto-ignition tendency**, resulting in more knock. Therefore, the same fuel shows a **lower MON than RON**. The difference (RON – MON) is called **sensitivity**, and is high for aromatic and olefinic fuels. --- ### **Q.B2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Question Bank]** **(a)** What is self-ignition temperature? Explain its significance in SI and CI engines with suitable examples. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT):** The minimum temperature to which a fuel must be heated, **without any external flame or spark**, to initiate self-sustained combustion. Also called auto-ignition temperature. **Significance in CI Engine:** - CI engines rely entirely on **SIT** for combustion. The high compression ratio (14:1 to 22:1) raises air temperature to **700–900 °C**, well above the SIT of diesel (≈ 210 °C). - Lower SIT → shorter ignition delay → smoother combustion. - Fuels with high CN (e.g., n-cetane, SIT = 215 °C) are preferred. - Example: **Diesel knock** occurs when the fuel has a high SIT (low CN), causing delayed ignition and rapid pressure rise. **Significance in SI Engine:** - SIT is the **upper temperature limit** for the end-gas before TDC. If end-gas temperature exceeds SIT, **premature auto-ignition** causes knock. - Higher SIT fuels (e.g., iso-octane, SIT = 418 °C) resist knock. - Example: **Paraffinic fuels** with low SIT (like n-heptane) cause severe knock, while **aromatic fuels** (toluene, xylene) have high SIT and high RON. **(b)** Compare octane number and cetane number in terms of (i) reference fuel, (ii) engine type, (iii) knock/ignition delay behaviour, and (iv) structure–property relationship. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Octane Number | Cetane Number | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) Reference fuel (high) | Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) | n-Cetane (C₁₆H₃₄) | | (ii) Reference fuel (low) | n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆) | α-Methylnaphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀) | | (iii) Engine type | Spark Ignition (SI) | Compression Ignition (CI) | | (iv) Desired property | **Knock resistance** | **Short ignition delay** | | (v) Bad property | Knocking tendency | Diesel knock (hard starting) | | (vi) Best structure | Branched / aromatic | Long straight chain (paraffin) | | (vii) Typical fuel value | Petrol: 90–98 RON | Diesel: 45–55 CN | | (viii) Inverse relationship | High ON ⇒ Low CN | High CN ⇒ Low ON | **Key Insight:** Octane and cetane numbers are **inversely related** because a fuel that resists auto-ignition (high SIT) is good for SI but bad for CI, and vice versa. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Mistake:** Writing "Octane number is measured in a CI engine." → **Lose 2 marks.** It is measured in a **variable compression ratio SI engine** (CFR engine). > 2. **Mistake:** Using the wrong value of 3.76 in derivation. → **Lose 1 mark.** Always show: air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂ (from 79/21 ratio). > 3. **Mistake:** Confusing Flash Point and Self-Ignition Temperature. → **Lose 2 marks.** Flash point uses an external flame; SIT does not. > 4. **Mistake:** Writing "CN is measured by varying fuel composition" → **Lose 1 mark.** The compression ratio is varied, not the fuel. > 5. **Mistake:** Stating RON > MON for ALL fuels → **Lose 1 mark.** RON = MON for n-heptane, iso-octane, and some alcohols (sensitivity = 0). > 6. **Mistake:** Using atomic mass of N₂ = 14 in molecular weight of air (should be 28) → **Lose 1 mark.** Air molecular weight = 0.21×32 + 0.79×28 = 28.84 ≈ 29. > 7. **Mistake:** Stoichiometric A/F formula without "4.76" factor (i.e., using pure O₂) → **Lose 2 marks.** Always use air, not oxygen, for engine A/F. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final 2-minute revision list before the exam hall. Cover every bullet.** - ✅ **A/F Ratio:** m_air / m_fuel; petrol stoich = **14.7:1**, diesel stoich = **14.5:1**. - ✅ **Master Formula:** A/F = 138.04(x + y/4) / (12x + y) for CₓHᵧ. - ✅ **Equivalence Ratio φ** = (F/A)_act / (F/A)_stoich; **Lambda λ** = 1/φ. - ✅ **φ < 1 (lean):** Better economy, more NOx, lower power. **φ > 1 (rich):** More power, more CO & HC. - ✅ **SIT:** Minimum temperature for self-ignition **without external spark**. - ✅ **SIT of n-heptane (223 °C) << Iso-octane (418 °C)** — explains why n-heptane knocks. - ✅ **SIT of n-cetane (215 °C) << α-methylnaphthalene (565 °C)** — explains cetane rating. - ✅ **Octane Number** = % iso-octane in iso-octane + n-heptane blend; for **SI engines**. - ✅ **RON** at 600 rpm, 25 °C; **MON** at 900 rpm, 152 °C; **AKI** = (R+M)/2. - ✅ **Cetane Number** = % n-cetane in n-cetane + α-methylnaphthalene blend; for **CI engines**. - ✅ **Octane ↔ Cetane are INVERSELY related** — high ON means low CN. - ✅ **Branched/Aromatic structure** → high ON, high SIT → SI fuel. - ✅ **Long straight-chain paraffin** → high CN, low SIT → CI fuel. - ✅ **4.76 = (1 + 3.76)** is the air-to-O₂ mole ratio — must be memorized. - ✅ **Molecular weight of air = 29 kg/kmol** (NOT 28). - ✅ **3-way catalytic converter operates only at φ ≈ 1** (lambda window ± 1%). - ✅ **Sensitivity = RON – MON**; high for aromatics/olefins, zero for paraffins. - ✅ **Diesel knock** = high SIT, low CN; **Petrol knock** = low SIT, low ON. - ✅ **Hydrogen (CN = 0)** cannot be used in CI engines without ignition improvers like diethyl ether.

topics.title: Carburettors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Carburettors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carburettor (Carburetor / Carburettor):** A carburettor is a mechanical device that mixes air with gasoline (petrol) in the correct air-fuel (A/F) ratio for combustion in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine. It atomizes the fuel, mixes it with the incoming air stream, and delivers a homogeneous combustible mixture to the engine cylinders based on the operating conditions (load and speed).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the carburettor is classified under the **Fuel Supply System** of a conventional SI engine, functioning as a **calibrated metering device** governed by the **Venturi Principle** and the **Bernoulli Equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Engine Fuel System Block:** Fuel Tank → Fuel Pump → Fuel Strainer → Carburettor (Metering + Mixing) → Intake Manifold → Cylinder.
> The carburettor is the **heart** of the gasoline fuel delivery system (excluding modern MPFI systems).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a carburettor as the **"chemical bartender"** of your engine:

- A bartender in a busy bar must pour exactly the right amount of whiskey (fuel) for every sip of mixer (air) the customer (engine) demands, regardless of how fast they gulp it down (RPM) or how thirsty they are (load).
- The carburettor performs this exact role. It uses a **narrowed throat (Venturi)** in the air passage. As air rushes through this narrowing, it speeds up (just like putting your thumb over a garden hose). The fast-moving air creates a low-pressure region.
- This low pressure **sucks fuel** from a tiny jet (like a straw) into the air stream, atomizing it into a fine mist — ready to be burned.

## 1.3 Essential Design Objectives of a Carburettor

| Objective | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| Vaporize / Atomize fuel | Creates combustible mixture for rapid flame propagation |
| Meter correct A/F ratio | **Stoichiometric = 14.7 : 1** by mass for petrol |
| Supply variable mixture | Engine load varies from idle to full load |
| Smooth throttle response | Avoid hesitation and engine stumble |
| Cold-start enrichment | Choke provides rich mixture when cold |
| Wide-open throttle enrichment | Power valve activates for max power output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU Board Value):**
> - **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio (Petrol):** $A/F = 14.7:1$ by mass
> - **Calorific Value of Petrol:** **44 MJ/kg** (≈ 10,500 kcal/kg)
> - **Density of Petrol:** **0.74 g/cm³** (≈ 740 kg/m³)
> - **Density of Air:** **1.2 kg/m³** at NTP (293 K, 1 atm)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Venturi Effect

The carburettor operates on the **Venturi Principle**, which is a direct application of **Bernoulli's Equation** for an incompressible, steady, frictionless flow.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Venturi:
1. Air enters the carburettor through the **air horn** under the suction created by the descending piston.
2. The air passage narrows at the **throat (Venturi)**, causing a local increase in velocity.
3. By Bernoulli's principle, increased velocity → **decreased static pressure**.
4. The fuel discharge nozzle is exposed to atmospheric pressure (via the float chamber).
5. The **pressure differential** between the float chamber and the throat causes fuel to be drawn up and discharged into the air stream.

## 2.2 Constructional Anatomy of a Simple Carburettor

A simple (elementary) carburettor consists of the following key components:

1. **Float Chamber**: A reservoir of fuel maintained at a constant level using a **float** and **needle valve**. The fuel level is kept **slightly below** the fuel discharge nozzle to prevent leakage.
2. **Float and Needle Valve**: A buoyant float actuates a tapered needle valve to admit fuel when level falls and cut off fuel when the level is restored. The level remains **2–3 mm below the nozzle tip**.
3. **Venturi Throat**: The narrowest cross-section of the air passage — the metering zone.
4. **Fuel Discharge Nozzle (Main Jet)**: A calibrated orifice through which fuel is metered into the throat.
5. **Throttle Valve (Butterfly Valve)**: A rotatable disc that controls the mass of mixture entering the cylinder, thereby governing **engine load** and **speed**.
6. **Choke Valve**: A butterfly valve located upstream of the venturi, used to restrict airflow during **cold starting**, thereby enriching the mixture.
7. **Auxiliary Air Bleed**: A small air-bleed hole that mixes with the main fuel jet to improve atomization at lower flow rates.

## 2.3 Defects of a Simple (Elementary) Carburettor

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Ideal" Simple Carburettor is NOT used in practice.** Why? Because it fails to deliver a constant A/F ratio across the entire operating range.

| Defect | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Variation in A/F ratio with speed** | At low air velocity, fuel discharge is disproportionately high → rich mixture at low speeds. At high air velocity, air mass flow increases more than fuel discharge → lean mixture at high speeds. |
| **No provision for starting enrichment** | During cold start, the engine is cold → fuel does not vaporize well. A richer mixture is needed. |
| **No provision for acceleration enrichment** | Sudden throttle opening causes momentary lean puff (engine stumbles). |
| **No idle system** | At idle, low manifold vacuum causes insufficient fuel flow. |
| **No power enrichment** | At WOT (Wide Open Throttle), maximum power requires a slightly rich mixture (A/F ≈ 12:1). |

To overcome these defects, a **practical (compensating) carburettor** uses multiple circuits and systems.

## 2.4 Circuits of a Practical Carburettor

A modern Solex-type carburettor typically integrates **5 circuits**:

| Circuit | Function | Operating Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Float / Fuel Reservoir Circuit** | Maintains constant fuel level in float bowl | All conditions |
| **Idle / Slow-Speed Circuit** | Provides rich mixture at idle and low loads | 0–5% throttle opening |
| **Main Metering (Economizer) Circuit** | Supplies stoichiometric mixture at cruising | 5–75% throttle opening |
| **Power Enrichment Circuit** | Enriches mixture for maximum power | 75–100% throttle opening |
| **Accelerating Pump Circuit** | Provides extra fuel shot during sudden throttle opening | Transient (a few seconds) |
| **Choke / Starting Circuit** | Provides extra rich mixture during cold start | Cold cranking |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are direct KTU board favorites. Memorize the variable meanings and units.**

**Governing Equations (Air Side):**

$$P_a + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_a^2 = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2 = \text{constant (Bernoulli)}$$

$$V_t = V_a \sqrt{\frac{P_a - P_t}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_a^2 + (P_a - P_t)}}$$

$$V_t = \frac{A_a}{A_t} \cdot V_a \quad \text{(Continuity equation)}$$

**Governing Equations (Fuel Side):**

$$V_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot h_f}$$

$$V_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{(P_a - P_t)}{\rho_f}}$$

**Air-Fuel Ratio:**

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{\rho_a \cdot A_t \cdot V_t}{\rho_f \cdot A_f \cdot V_f}$$

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f} \cdot \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \frac{1}{C_{df}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{V_t^2}{2g \cdot h_f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mnemonic (Ideal Simple Carburettor):**
> $$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{ideal}} = C \cdot \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{h_f}}$$
> where $h_f$ is the head of fuel in the float chamber and $C$ is a constant. This shows A/F varies with the square root of air velocity → not constant.

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $P_a$ | Atmospheric pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| $P_t$ | Throat pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| $V_a$ | Air velocity at inlet | m/s |
| $V_t$ | Air velocity at throat | m/s |
| $\rho_a$ | Density of air | **1.2 kg/m³** |
| $\rho_f$ | Density of fuel | **740 kg/m³** |
| $A_t$ | Throat cross-section | m² |
| $A_f$ | Fuel nozzle area | m² |
| $C_{df}$ | Discharge coefficient of fuel nozzle | **0.6 – 0.8** |
| $h_f$ | Effective head of fuel | m |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\dot{m}_a, \dot{m}_f$ | Mass flow rates of air and fuel | kg/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The carburettor's principle — converting a pressure differential into a precise mass flow — is mirrored in:
- **Bunsen Burner** (gas-air mixing at domestic and laboratory scale)
- **Medical Nebulizers** (respiratory drug delivery)
- **Paint Spray Guns** (industrial atomization)
- **Aircraft Float-Type Carburettors** (Marvel-Schebler, Bendix-Stromberg)
- **Modern GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection)** inherits carburettor metering philosophy but injects fuel directly into the cylinder.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio of an Ideal (Elementary) Carburettor

### Given Conditions
- Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow (idealized).
- Air drawn through the venturi from atmosphere to throat.
- Fuel drawn from the float chamber (at pressure $P_a$) to the throat (at pressure $P_t$).

### Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Air Inlet (point 1) and Throat (point 2)

At the inlet, the area is large → $V_1 \approx 0$.
At the throat, velocity is $V_t$.

$$P_a + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_1^2 = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2$$

Since $V_1 \approx 0$ (large inlet area):

$$P_a = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2$$

The **pressure drop** across the venturi is therefore:

$$P_a - P_t = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2$$

### Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation to the Fuel Flow

The fuel starts in the float chamber at pressure $P_a$ with negligible velocity ($V_f \approx 0$ in the chamber) and is discharged into the throat at pressure $P_t$ with velocity $V_f$.

$$P_a + 0 = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho_f V_f^2$$

$$P_a - P_t = \frac{1}{2} \rho_f V_f^2$$

### Step 3: Equate the Two Pressure Drops (Same Pressure Differential Drives Both)

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho_f V_f^2$$

$$\rho_a V_t^2 = \rho_f V_f^2$$

$$\frac{V_f}{V_t} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

### Step 4: Apply Continuity to Obtain Mass Flow Rates

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a A_t V_t$$

$$\dot{m}_f = \rho_f A_f V_f$$

### Step 5: Form the Air-Fuel Ratio

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{\rho_a A_t V_t}{\rho_f A_f V_f}$$

Substitute $V_f$ from Step 3:

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{\rho_a A_t V_t}{\rho_f A_f \cdot V_t \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}}$$

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

### Step 6: Final Simplified Expression (KTU Board Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Ideal Carburettor A/F Ratio:**
> $$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}}$$
> 
> This is a **purely constant** ratio, independent of engine speed. In practice, real carburettors deviate from this because of:
> - Compressibility of air at high speeds
> - Friction losses
> - Incomplete fuel atomization at low flow rates

### Step 7: Plug in Numerical Constants

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{740}}$$

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{0.001622}$$

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot 0.04027 \approx \frac{A_t}{25 \cdot A_f}$$

This is the famous **"Factor of 25"** rule used by KTU examiners to set ideal problems.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Standard)

### Problem Statement
> A carburettor has a venturi throat diameter of **25 mm** and a fuel nozzle diameter of **1.25 mm**. The fuel density is 740 kg/m³, air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Find the ideal A/F ratio. State whether the mixture is richer or leaner than stoichiometric.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Compute areas.

$$A_t = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.00125)^2 = 1.227 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Area ratio.

$$\frac{A_t}{A_f} = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{1.227 \times 10^{-6}} = 400$$

**Step 3:** Plug into A/F formula.

$$\frac{A}{F} = 400 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{740}} = 400 \times 0.04027 \approx 16.11$$

**Step 4:** Compare with stoichiometric.

$$\frac{A}{F}_{\text{actual}} = 16.11 \quad \text{vs} \quad \frac{A}{F}_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$$

Since 16.11 > 14.7, the mixture is **LEANER** than stoichiometric. The carburettor is **under-metering fuel** — a correction is required (e.g., larger fuel nozzle or auxiliary air bleed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A practical Solex carburettor is designed such that at the cruising (economy) range, $A/F \approx 14.7 \pm 0.5$. This is achieved via **compensating devices** (air bleed, emulsion tube, power valve).

## 3.3 Derivation: Fuel Discharge Velocity Through the Nozzle

Using Torricelli's theorem with a discharge coefficient $C_{df}$:

$$V_f = C_{df} \sqrt{2gh_f}$$

Substituting $h_f$ expressed in terms of pressure differential (for cases where float chamber is vented separately):

$$V_f = C_{df} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_t)}{\rho_f}}$$

Combined with Bernoulli for air (with $C_{da}$ as air discharge coefficient):

$$V_t = C_{da} \cdot A_t \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_t)}{\rho_a}}$$

The **practical (non-ideal) A/F ratio** becomes:

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{C_{da}}{C_{df}} \cdot \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In practice**, $C_{da}$ and $C_{df}$ are **not constant** — they vary with Reynolds number, which itself varies with engine speed. This non-constancy is what creates the A/F deviation in a real carburettor, and is exactly what compensating circuits correct for.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Carburettor Selection

| Engine Application | Recommended Carburettor Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Small 2-wheeler (100–150 cc) | **Simple constant-vacuum carburettor** | Cost-effective, light, low power demands |
| Mid-size motorcycle (150–500 cc) | **CV (Constant Velocity) carburettor** | Smooth throttle response, no flat spots |
| Passenger car (1.0–2.0 L) | **Multi-venturi, multi-jet Solex** | Wide operating range, fuel economy, emissions |
| High-performance car / racing | **Multiple S.U. or Weber carburettors** | Maximum airflow, power enrichment critical |
| Aircraft piston engine | **Marvel-Schebler float-type** with idle cut-off | Anti-icing, inverted-flight fuel delivery |
| Marine outboard | **Down-draft with fuel pump** | Resistance to wave-induced fuel surge |
| Vintage / Classic | **Single venturi, fixed-jet** | Period-correct, mechanically simple |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Simple Carburettor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Atmospheric Air] --> node2[Air Horn Entry]
    node2 --> node3[Choke Valve]
    node3 --> node4[Venturi Throat]
    node4 --> node5[Throttle Butterfly Valve]
    node5 --> node6[Intake Manifold]
    node6 --> node7[Engine Cylinder]
    node7 -->|Piston Suction| node3

    node8[Fuel Tank] --> node9[Fuel Pump]
    node9 --> node10[Float Chamber]
    node10 --> node11[Float + Needle Valve]
    node11 --> node12[Fuel Level Maintained Below Nozzle]
    node12 --> node13[Main Fuel Jet]
    node13 --> node14[Fuel Discharge Nozzle]
    node14 --> node4

    node15[Vent to Atmosphere] --> node10

    node4 -.->|Pressure Drop| node13
    node13 -.->|Fuel Sucked Up| node4
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Solex-Type Compensating Carburettor (Multi-Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Atmosphere] --> nodeB[Choke Valve]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compensating Air Bleed]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Main Venturi Throat]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Auxiliary Venturi]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Throttle Valve]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Intake Manifold]

    nodeH[Fuel Supply Line] --> nodeI[Needle Valve]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Float Bowl]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Main Metering Jet]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Idle Jet]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Power Jet]
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Accelerating Pump]

    nodeK --> nodeD
    nodeL --> nodeF
    nodeM --> nodeD
    nodeN --> nodeF

    nodeO[Vacuum Diaphragm] -.->|Mechanical Feedback| nodeM
    nodeP[Throttle Linkage] -.->|Sudden Opening| nodeN

    subgraph FloatChamber["Float Circuit"]
        nodeJ
        nodeI
    end

    subgraph IdleSystem["Idle Circuit 0 to 5 percent throttle"]
        nodeL
    end

    subgraph MainSystem["Main Economizer Circuit 5 to 75 percent"]
        nodeK
        nodeC
    end

    subgraph PowerSystem["Power Enrichment Circuit 75 to 100 percent"]
        nodeM
        nodeO
    end

    subgraph AccSystem["Accelerating Pump Circuit"]
        nodeN
```
