```mermaid
        nodeP
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Elementary (Simple) Carburettor with All Labelled Parts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA[Atmospheric Pressure] --> P1[Float Chamber At P_a]
    P1 --> P2[Needle and Seat]
    P2 --> P3[Fuel Inlet from Pump]

    FC[Fuel In] --> P3
    P3 --> P1

    P1 --> P4[Fuel Discharge Nozzle]
    P4 --> P5[Venturi Throat at P_t]
    P5 --> P6[Throttle Valve]
    P6 --> P7[Intake Manifold Vacuum]

    A1[Air Inlet] --> P8[Choke]
    P8 --> P5

    P5 -.->|Pressure Difference P_a minus P_t| P4

    subgraph FloatCtrl["Float Level Control"]
        P1
        P2
    end

    subgraph Metering["Fuel Metering Zone"]
        P4
        P5
    end

    subgraph Mixture["Mixture Formation"]
        P6
        P7
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** Air enters from $A_1$, passes through the **choke**, accelerates through the **venturi throat** (at $P_t$, low pressure), and exits past the **throttle** to the manifold. Fuel from the float chamber (at $P_a$, atmospheric) is pushed/drawn through the nozzle into the throat because $P_a > P_t$. The float + needle valve maintains a constant fuel level.

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Which Carburettor Circuit is Active?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Engine Condition?}
    start -->|Cold Crank| choke[Choke Circuit Active]
    start -->|Idle 0 to 5 percent| idle[Idle Circuit Active]
    start -->|Cruise 5 to 75 percent| main[Main Economizer Circuit Active]
    start -->|WOT 75 to 100 percent| power[Power Enrichment Active]
    start -->|Sudden Throttle Open| accel[Accelerating Pump Shot]

    choke --> mix1[Rich Mixture A slash F = 9 to 12]
    idle --> mix2[Rich Idle A slash F = 11 to 13]
    main --> mix3[Stoichiometric A slash F = 14.7]
    power --> mix4[Rich Power A slash F = 12 to 13]
    accel --> mix5[Transient Rich Shot]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answers Below — Type to Learn by Heart**

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the working principle of a carburettor. What is the role of the venturi?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A carburettor works on the **Venturi Principle**, a direct application of **Bernoulli's Equation**. When the engine piston descends, it creates suction that draws air through the carburettor. The air passage has a constriction (venturi throat) where the cross-sectional area decreases. By continuity, air velocity increases at the throat. By Bernoulli, the increased velocity leads to a drop in static pressure at the throat. This pressure differential between the float chamber (at atmospheric pressure) and the throat causes fuel to be sucked up through the main jet and atomized into the air stream. The role of the venturi is therefore to **create the low-pressure region that meters and atomizes the fuel**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three defects of a simple (elementary) carburettor. How is the main defect corrected in a practical carburettor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three defects are:
1. **Variation in A/F ratio with engine speed** — fuel discharge does not scale linearly with air flow.
2. **No provision for starting enrichment** — the simple carburettor cannot deliver a rich mixture for cold cranking.
3. **No provision for acceleration enrichment** — sudden throttle opening causes a lean puff.

**Correction:** A practical carburettor (e.g., Solex) uses an **auxiliary air-bleed** (also called a compensating air bleed) around the main fuel jet. The bleed admits a small quantity of air that mixes with the fuel before it leaves the nozzle, so the fuel discharge becomes nearly proportional to air flow, giving a more constant A/F ratio. Additional systems (choke, power jet, accelerating pump) handle starting, power, and acceleration.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **ESE Pattern:** Question with two parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. KTU may offer internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (complete alternative).

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

#### Q. (a) With a neat sketch, explain the working of a simple (elementary) carburettor. List its defects. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid Diagram):**
- Draw the air horn, choke, venturi throat, throttle valve, float chamber with float + needle, fuel nozzle, and main jet. Label all parts.

**Working — Stepwise Explanation (5 marks):**
1. Air enters the air horn from atmosphere.
2. The descending piston creates suction → air rushes in.
3. The venturi throat accelerates this air and drops its static pressure to $P_t$.
4. Fuel in the float chamber is at $P_a$ (atmospheric) maintained by the float-valve system.
5. Pressure differential $P_a - P_t$ causes fuel to flow up the discharge nozzle.
6. The fuel jet is atomized by the high-velocity air stream → combustible mixture.
7. The mixture passes through the throttle (butterfly) valve into the intake manifold.

**Defects (2 marks):**
- A/F varies with engine speed (not constant).
- No provision for cold start, idle, acceleration, or WOT enrichment.

---

#### Q. (b) Derive an expression for the air-fuel ratio of an ideal simple carburettor in terms of densities and flow areas. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow.
- Air velocity at inlet ≈ 0.
- Float chamber pressure = atmospheric pressure $P_a$.
- No losses in nozzle ($C_{df} = 1$).

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation for air (1.5 marks):**
$$P_a + 0 = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2$$
$$\Rightarrow P_a - P_t = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's equation for fuel (1.5 marks):**
$$P_a + 0 = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho_f V_f^2$$
$$\Rightarrow P_a - P_t = \frac{1}{2} \rho_f V_f^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3: Equate (i) and (ii) (1 mark):**
$$\frac{1}{2} \rho_a V_t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho_f V_f^2$$
$$\frac{V_f}{V_t} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4: Apply continuity (1 mark):**
$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a A_t V_t, \quad \dot{m}_f = \rho_f A_f V_f$$

**Step 5: Form A/F ratio (1 mark):**
$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{\rho_a A_t V_t}{\rho_f A_f V_f} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

**Final Result (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{\frac{A}{F} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}}$$

**Conclusion:** The A/F ratio of an ideal carburettor is **independent of engine speed** and depends only on geometric areas and fluid densities. With $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³ and $\rho_f = 740$ kg/m³, the A/F ratio is approximately $\frac{A_t}{A_f} \times 0.0403$.

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme Alternative]**

#### Q. (a) Explain the construction and working of a Solex carburettor with a neat sketch. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
The Solex carburettor is a **multi-venturi, down-draft** compensating carburettor. It has:
- A **float chamber** with a float and needle valve for constant fuel level.
- A **main venturi** and an **auxiliary venturi** concentric with the main venturi.
- A **main fuel jet** fitted in the float chamber and a **fuel discharge nozzle** opening into the auxiliary venturi.
- An **auxiliary air-bleed** (compensating device) drawing air from the atmosphere into the main jet, just above the discharge nozzle.
- An **idle jet** and an **idle air-bleed** for slow-speed operation.
- A **power jet** (economizer) controlled by a vacuum diaphragm.
- An **accelerating pump** (mechanical plunger type) connected to the throttle linkage.
- A **choke butterfly valve** at the air horn entry.
- A **throttle butterfly valve** at the manifold end.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- At **cruising** (medium throttle), air flows through both venturis. The auxiliary air-bleed admits a small amount of air into the main fuel stream, emulsifying it. This compensation ensures the A/F ratio stays near stoichiometric.
- At **idle**, the main jet flow is insufficient due to low manifold vacuum. The idle jet, fed through an idle port downstream of the throttle, supplies the rich mixture needed at idle.
- At **WOT (full load)**, the vacuum diaphragm actuates the power jet, enriching the mixture.
- At **sudden acceleration**, the throttle linkage pushes a plunger in the accelerating pump, injecting an extra shot of fuel for ~1 second.
- At **cold start**, the choke butterfly closes partially, raising manifold vacuum and drawing excess fuel → rich cold-start mixture.

---

#### Q. (b) A carburettor has a venturi throat diameter of 28 mm and fuel nozzle diameter of 1.4 mm. Take $\rho_a$ = 1.2 kg/m³ and $\rho_f$ = 740 kg/m³. Calculate the theoretical A/F ratio. Is the mixture rich or lean? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute throat area (1 mark):**
$$A_t = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.028)^2 = 6.158 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute nozzle area (1 mark):**
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0014)^2 = 1.539 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Area ratio (1 mark):**
$$\frac{A_t}{A_f} = \frac{6.158 \times 10^{-4}}{1.539 \times 10^{-6}} = 400$$

**Step 4: Density ratio (1 mark):**
$$\sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{740}} = \sqrt{0.001622} = 0.04027$$

**Step 5: A/F ratio (1.5 marks):**
$$\frac{A}{F} = 400 \times 0.04027 = 16.11$$

**Step 6: Compare with stoichiometric (1.5 marks):**
Since 16.11 > 14.7 (stoichiometric), the mixture is **LEANER** than stoichiometric by approximately 9.6%.

**To correct** → increase the fuel nozzle diameter, or add an air-bleed for compensation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** A/F is a ratio (dimensionless), but if you write "16.11 kg/kg" you lose 0.5 marks. State it as "A/F = 16.11:1" or simply "16.11".
> 2. **Mixing diameter vs. area:** Many students compute A/F using diameters directly, which is **wrong**. Always use $\frac{\pi}{4}d^2$ for areas.
> 3. **Confusing throttle with choke:** The throttle controls **load** (and indirectly speed), the choke controls **starting enrichment**. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 4. **Ignoring the empirical factor of ~25:** Use the shortcut $A/F \approx \frac{A_t}{25 A_f}$ for quick cross-checks. If your detailed answer doesn't approximately match, recompute.
> 5. **Not labeling the sketch:** In Q.(a), the sketch carries up to 3 marks. Label every part (float, needle, venturi, throttle, choke, jets). Unlabelled diagrams fetch zero.
> 6. **Skipping the comparison with stoichiometric:** Numerical answers must always end with a "rich/lean/stoichiometric" statement. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall):**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** Carburettor = mechanical device that mixes air + petrol in correct A/F ratio using Venturi principle.
> 
> ✅ **Working Principle:** Bernoulli + Continuity → pressure drop at venturi throat → fuel sucked up through main jet.
> 
> ✅ **Ideal A/F Formula:**
> $$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{A_t}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} \approx \frac{A_t}{25 \cdot A_f}$$
> 
> ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F (Petrol) = 14.7:1** (by mass).
> 
> ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $\rho_a$ = **1.2 kg/m³**, $\rho_f$ = **740 kg/m³**, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, CV of petrol = **44 MJ/kg**.
> 
> ✅ **Main Components:** Float chamber, float + needle, venturi throat, fuel discharge nozzle, main jet, throttle (load control), choke (cold start).
> 
> ✅ **5 Circuits of a practical carburettor:**
> 1. Float circuit (constant level)
> 2. Idle / slow-speed circuit
> 3. Main metering (economizer) circuit
> 4. Power enrichment circuit
> 5. Accelerating pump circuit
> (Plus the Choke/Starting circuit)
> 
> ✅ **Defects of a simple carburettor:**
> 1. A/F varies with speed (most critical)
> 2. No starting enrichment
> 3. No acceleration enrichment
> 4. No idle / power circuits
> 
> ✅ **Compensation method:** Auxiliary air-bleed around the main fuel jet (the heart of the Solex design).
> 
> ✅ **Throttle** = controls engine **load**; **Choke** = controls **starting enrichment**.
> 
> ✅ **Carburettor is being phased out** in modern cars by **MPFI / GDI** systems due to emissions and efficiency norms (Euro 6/BS-VI), but remains the **conceptual foundation** for understanding engine fuel metering.
> 
> ✅ **Sketch must include:** Air horn, choke, venturi, float bowl, float, needle valve, main jet, fuel discharge nozzle, throttle valve, intake manifold connection. **LABEL EVERYTHING.**
> 
> ✅ **One-line memory aid:** *"Bigger Venturi, Leaner Mix"* — because $A/F \propto A_t$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)* is a fuel delivery system in a spark-ignition (SI) engine in which **each cylinder is supplied with fuel by its own individual electronic fuel injector**, mounted in the intake port (just upstream of the intake valve). The injection pressure is typically **0.3 – 0.5 MPa (3 – 5 bar)** and the timing/duration is governed by the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology used by KTU Examiners:** "Port Injection", "Indirect Injection (IDI for SI)", "Sequential MPFI", "Batch Fire", "Simultaneous Fire".

### Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)
Imagine a row of six students (cylinders) drinking water from individual bottles. Instead of one huge tank spilling water on all of them (the old carburetor way), each student has a personal helper (injector) who hands them a precisely measured sip exactly when they need it. That personal delivery is **MPFI** — one injector per cylinder, placed right at the "mouth" of the cylinder (intake port).

---

## 1.2 Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)* is a direct-injection diesel system where a high-pressure **"common rail"** accumulator (running at **100 – 200 MPa / 1000 – 2000 bar**) feeds individual solenoid- or piezo-actuated injectors. The rail pressure is generated by a radial-piston HP pump and is decoupled from engine speed and injection events.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a high-rise apartment building with a huge overhead water tank (the **common rail**) kept pressurised by pumps 24/7. The moment a tap (injector) opens, water rushes out at enormous pressure because the tank is always primed. The pressure is independent of how many taps open or when. That is exactly how a common rail works for diesel fuel.

---

## 1.3 Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)* — also called Direct Injection Spark Ignition (DISI) — is an SI-engine system where fuel is injected **directly into the combustion chamber** at pressures of **5 – 20 MPa (50 – 200 bar)** by solenoid or piezo injectors. The stoichiometric air–fuel ratio of **14.7 : 1** by mass, and stratified or homogeneous charge modes are both used.

### Intuitive Analogy
If MPFI is feeding a baby milk *near* the mouth, **GDI is feeding the baby directly into the stomach** — the fuel goes straight into the cylinder, allowing a much leaner, more controlled burn and lower fuel consumption (≈ 15 % better than port injection).

---

## 1.4 Solenoid vs. Piezo Injectors

| Feature | Solenoid Injector | Piezo Injector |
|---|---|---|
| **Actuation** | Electromagnetic coil | Piezoelectric crystal stack |
| **Response time** | ~ 0.2 – 0.4 ms | ~ 0.02 – 0.05 ms |
| **No. of injections per cycle** | Up to 3 – 4 | Up to 7 – 9 |
| **Fuel pressure used** | 100 – 160 MPa | 150 – 200 MPa |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (≈ 2 – 3×) |
| **Common in** | MPFI, basic CRDI | Modern CRDI, GDI |

### Analogy
A **solenoid injector** is like a doorbell — you press a button, an electromagnet pulls a plunger, click-open. A **piezo injector** is like a finger-flick — a tiny piezo crystal changes shape when voltage is applied, flicking the nozzle open and shut many times per cycle.

---

## 1.5 Naturally Aspirated vs. Forced Induction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naturally Aspirated (NA):** Air enters the engine purely due to the downward stroke of the piston creating vacuum (no external compressor). The pressure inside the intake manifold ≈ **– 0.3 to – 0.5 bar** (gauge) or **0.7 – 0.9 bar absolute**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forced Induction (Turbo / Supercharger):** An external device compresses the intake air above atmospheric pressure. **Boost pressure** is the gauge pressure above atmosphere, typically **0.2 – 1.5 bar** for production cars.

### Analogy
- **Naturally aspirated** = breathing through a straw (atmospheric push only).
- **Supercharger** = using a hand pump (mechanically driven) to push air in.
- **Turbocharger** = using the engine's own exhaust fumes to spin a turbine that pumps air back in (exhaust energy recycling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants & Metrics to Memorise for KTU:**
> - Stoichiometric A/F (petrol) = **14.7 : 1**
> - Stoichiometric A/F (diesel) = **14.5 : 1**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure = **1.01325 bar (101.325 kPa)**
> - Adiabatic index γ for air = **1.4**
> - Density of air at STP = **1.225 kg/m³**
> - Compression ratio — NA petrol: 8 – 12 : 1, GDI: 9.5 – 14 : 1, Diesel CRDI: 14 – 22 : 1
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MPFI System — Working Logic Steps

1. **Fuel Sourcing:** Fuel is drawn from the tank by an electric **transfer pump** and passed through an **in-line fuel filter**.
2. **Low-Pressure Stage:** A **fuel pressure regulator** maintains a constant differential pressure of about **3.0 – 3.5 bar** across the injectors (relative to manifold vacuum).
3. **High-Pressure Routing:** Fuel flows through a **fuel rail (manifold)** that distributes equal pressure to each injector.
4. **Sensing Inputs (ECU):** Sensors feed real-time data: **Crankshaft position (CKP), Camshaft position (CMP), Mass Air Flow (MAF), Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP), Throttle Position (TPS), Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT), Oxygen / Lambda (O₂) sensor**.
5. **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM):** The ECU commands each injector solenoid open for a calculated **injector pulse width (IPW)** in **milliseconds**.
6. **Sequential vs. Batch Mode:** Modern MPFI is *sequential* (one injector fires per cylinder per cycle, timed to intake stroke). Older systems use *batch-fire* (injectors fire in pairs).
7. **Closed-Loop Correction:** The O₂ sensor feeds back λ value; ECU adjusts IPW by ± 25 % around the base map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** MPFI replaced carburetors in the 1980s–90s, giving 10 – 15 % better fuel economy, 20 % lower HC/CO emissions, and crisp cold-start behaviour.

---

## 2.2 CRDI System — Working Logic Steps

1. **Transfer Pump (Low-Pressure, ~ 5 bar):** Pulls diesel from the tank, sends it through the filter to the **gear-type high-pressure pump**.
2. **High-Pressure Pump (Radial Piston, ~ 135 – 200 MPa):** Driven by the camshaft, it pressurises fuel into the **common rail**.
3. **Common Rail Accumulator:** Stores the pressurised fuel. The rail pressure is monitored by a **rail pressure sensor (RPS)** and controlled by the **Pressure Control Valve (PCV)** and **suction control valve (SCV)** on the HP pump.
4. **ECU Decision:** ECU computes injection timing (BTDC/ATDC), duration, number of pilot injections (1 – 7) and rail pressure.
5. **Injector Actuation:** Solenoid or piezo injectors open against the rail pressure to inject fuel in multiple events — **pilot → main → after**.
6. **Direct Combustion:** Fuel is sprayed through **6 – 9 hole nozzles** at 1500 – 2000 bar directly into the cylinder at 14 – 22 : 1 compression.

---

## 2.3 GDI System — Working Logic Steps

1. **High-Pressure Pump:** Camshaft-driven, generates **5 – 20 MPa**.
2. **High-Pressure Rail:** Stores pressurised fuel; pressure is **load-dependent** (higher under high load).
3. **Injector Mounted in Cylinder Head:** Six-hole nozzle aimed at the spark plug region.
4. **Two Operating Modes:**
   - **Homogeneous Mode (low/medium load):** Injector fires during **intake stroke**; air-fuel mix fills the chamber evenly. Compression ratio limited to ~ 12 : 1 (to avoid knock).
   - **Stratified Mode (low load / idle):** Injector fires during **compression stroke**; a rich cloud forms around the spark plug while the rest of the chamber is mostly air. Lean combustion → A/F ≈ 25 – 40 : 1 in the bulk zone.
5. **Knock Suppression:** Stratified charge allows the use of very lean mixtures, eliminating knock. This is why GDI engines can use higher compression ratios.
6. **Combustion Control:** Multiple injections per cycle (up to 5 events) for **noise reduction and emissions control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GDI Particulate Concern:** Direct injection of petrol can produce **particulate matter (PM2.5)** similar to diesel. Hence, modern GDI vehicles use **Gasoline Particulate Filters (GPF)**.

---

## 2.4 Forced Induction — Working Logic

### A. Turbocharger
- Driven by **exhaust gas energy** via a radial-inflow turbine.
- Shaft couples the turbine to a centrifugal compressor on the intake side.
- Operating range: **100 000 – 300 000 rpm** (typical).
- Equipped with **wastegate** (to limit max boost) and **variable-geometry turbo (VGT)** on diesels.

### B. Supercharger
- Driven mechanically via belt/pulley from the crankshaft.
- Types: **Roots, Twin-screw (Lysholm), Centrifugal (similar to turbo compressor).**
- Draws engine power (~ 5 – 15 %) to drive itself, but provides instant response (no turbo-lag).
- No wastegate needed (over-boost avoided by mechanical relief).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{a,act}}{m_{a,th}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | — |
| 2 | $m_{a,th} = \rho_a \times V_d \times \dfrac{N}{2} \times 60$ | Theoretical air mass per second (4-stroke) | kg/s |
| 3 | $\text{A}/\text{F} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Air-fuel ratio | — |
| 4 | $P_{brake} = \dfrac{\eta_{th,br} \times m_f \times \text{CV}}{t}$ | Brake power from fuel rate | kW |
| 5 | $\eta_{th,br} = \dfrac{P_b}{m_f \times \text{CV}}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | — |
| 6 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| 7 | $P_{boost} = P_{manifold} - P_{atm}$ | Boost pressure (gauge) | bar |
| 8 | $P_{indicated} = \dfrac{\eta_{th,i} \times m_f \times \text{CV}}{t}$ | Indicated power | kW |
| 9 | $W_{comp} = m_a \times C_p \times T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Compressor work (isentropic) | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $\rho_{boost} = \rho_{atm} \times \left(\dfrac{P_{manifold}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1/\gamma}$ | Boosted charge density | kg/m³ |
| 11 | $\dfrac{P_{boosted}}{P_{NA}} = \dfrac{\eta_v \times P_{manifold}}{\eta_v \times P_{atm}}$ | Power gain from boost (≈ pressure ratio) | — |
| 12 | $\text{IPW} = \dfrac{m_f}{n \times q_{inj}}$ | Injector pulse width (mass method) | ms |
| 13 | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{V_d}$ | Mean Effective Pressure | bar |
| 14 | $\text{Lambda } \lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio inverse | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of every fuel-mapping, boost-curve, calibration and emissions-development task in any automotive OEM (Bosch, Delphi, Denso, Continental, Maruti, Tata, Mahindra R&D centres).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through **exhaustive** analytical steps that are typical in KTU 14-mark problems. All variables are defined, and every algebraic step is shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Efficiency of a Naturally Aspirated vs. Forced Induction Engine

**Given:**
- Bore $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, Stroke $L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.09 \text{ m}$
- Number of cylinders $k = 4$, Engine speed $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, Atmospheric temperature $T_{atm} = 300 \text{ K}$
- Volumetric efficiency NA: $\eta_v^{NA} = 0.85$
- Boost pressure (turbo) $P_{boost} = 0.6 \text{ bar}$ (gauge), so $P_{manifold} = 1.613 \text{ bar}$
- Air density at STP: $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\gamma = 1.4$

**Step 1 — Swept volume of one cylinder**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 (0.09)
$$

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.09
$$

$$
V_s = 0.000452389 \text{ m}^3 = 452.4 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Total displacement**

$$
V_d = k \times V_s = 4 \times 452.4 = 1809.56 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 1.81 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Air mass induced per cycle in NA engine (4-stroke, intake every 2 revs)**

$$
m_{a,th}^{NA} = \rho_a \times \eta_v \times V_d
$$

$$
m_{a,th}^{NA} = 1.225 \times 0.85 \times 1.80956 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
m_{a,th}^{NA} = 1.225 \times 0.85 \times 0.00180956 = 0.001884 \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Air mass flow per second**

$$
\dot{m}_a^{NA} = m_{a,th}^{NA} \times \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a^{NA} = 0.001884 \times \dfrac{3000}{120} = 0.001884 \times 25 = 0.0471 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Density at boosted pressure (isothermal assumption for charge cooling)**

$$
\rho_{boost} = \rho_{atm} \times \dfrac{P_{manifold}}{P_{atm}} = 1.225 \times \dfrac{1.613}{1.013}
$$

$$
\rho_{boost} = 1.225 \times 1.5923 = 1.951 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Air mass in turbocharged engine (assuming $\eta_v$ improves to 0.95 with boost-cooling/intercooler)**

$$
m_{a,th}^{TC} = \rho_{boost} \times \eta_v^{TC} \times V_d
$$

$$
m_{a,th}^{TC} = 1.951 \times 0.95 \times 0.00180956 = 0.003354 \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step 7 — Air mass flow per second (turbocharged)**

$$
\dot{m}_a^{TC} = 0.003354 \times 25 = 0.08385 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 8 — Percentage increase in air mass flow (and therefore in indicated power)**

$$
\% \text{ Increase} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a^{TC} - \dot{m}_a^{NA}}{\dot{m}_a^{NA}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Increase} = \dfrac{0.08385 - 0.0471}{0.0471} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.03675}{0.0471} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Increase} = 78.0 \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A 0.6 bar boost produced ~ 78 % more air mass, which roughly translates to ~ 70 – 80 % power gain (after accounting for heat-transfer and mechanical losses). This is why even a small turbo yields dramatic torque gains.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brake Power and BSFC of a CRDI Diesel Engine

**Given:**
- 4-cyl CRDI, $V_d = 1.5 \text{ L} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$, $N = 3600 \text{ rpm}$
- $\eta_v = 0.92$, BMEP = 9.5 bar = 9.5 × 10⁵ Pa
- Calorific value of diesel CV = 43 000 kJ/kg
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.85$, indicated thermal efficiency $\eta_{th,i} = 0.40$

**Step 1 — Indicated Power from BMEP**

$$
P_i = \dfrac{\text{BMEP}_{ind} \times V_d \times N}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{[for 4-stroke]}
$$

First, find indicated BMEP from brake BMEP:

$$
\text{BMEP}_i = \dfrac{\text{BMEP}_b}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{9.5}{0.85} = 11.18 \text{ bar} = 11.18 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
P_i = \dfrac{11.18 \times 10^5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600}{120}
$$

$$
P_i = \dfrac{11.18 \times 1.5 \times 36}{120} \times 10^2 = \dfrac{603.72}{120} \times 100
$$

$$
P_i = 503.1 \text{ kW (wait, recheck unit conversion)}
$$

Let me redo carefully:

$$
P_i = \dfrac{\text{BMEP}_i \times L \times A \times N}{2 \times 60}
$$

Since $V_d = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$, and $\text{BMEP}_i$ in Pa:

$$
P_i = \dfrac{11.18 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \times 3600 /60}{2}
$$

$$
P_i = \dfrac{11.18 \times 10^5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 60}{2}
$$

$$
P_i = \dfrac{11.18 \times 1.5 \times 60}{2} \times 10^2
$$

$$
P_i = \dfrac{1006.2}{2} \times 100 = 503.1 \times 100 = 50310 \text{ W} = 50.31 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Brake Power**

$$
P_b = P_i \times \eta_m = 50.31 \times 0.85 = 42.76 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel consumption from indicated efficiency**

$$
P_i = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} \times \eta_{th,i}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_i}{\text{CV} \times \eta_{th,i}} = \dfrac{50.31}{43000 \times 0.40} = \dfrac{50.31}{17200} = 2.925 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

Convert to kg/h:

$$
\dot{m}_f = 2.925 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 10.53 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — BSFC**

$$
\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{10.53}{42.76} = 0.2462 \text{ kg/kWh} = 246.2 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BSFC} \approx 246 \text{ g/kWh, typical for a CRDI diesel}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compressor Power of a Turbocharger

**Given:**
- Air mass flow $\dot{m}_a = 0.1 \text{ kg/s}$
- $T_1 = 323 \text{ K}$ (after-cooler), $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 1.8 \text{ bar}$
- $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$

**Step 1 — Pressure ratio**

$$
r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{1.8}{1.013} = 1.777
$$

**Step 2 — Isentropic temperature ratio**

$$
\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (1.777)^{0.4/1.4} = (1.777)^{0.2857}
$$

Take log: $\ln(1.777) = 0.5754$. Multiply by 0.2857: $0.1644$. Exponential: $e^{0.1644} = 1.1787$.

$$
T_2 = 323 \times 1.1787 = 380.7 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Compressor work per kg**

$$
w_c = C_p \times (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (380.7 - 323) = 1.005 \times 57.7 = 57.99 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Compressor power**

$$
P_c = \dot{m}_a \times w_c = 0.1 \times 57.99 = 5.799 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — With isentropic efficiency 75 %**

$$
P_{c,actual} = \dfrac{P_c}{\eta_c} = \dfrac{5.799}{0.75} = 7.73 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{c,actual} \approx 7.73 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Injector Selection & Fuel Delivery Logic

| Step | Logic | Governing Equation / Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ECU receives engine speed | $N$ from CKP sensor |
| 2 | ECU receives load | MAP or MAF sensor |
| 3 | ECU receives coolant temp | ECT sensor (for warm-up enrichment) |
| 4 | ECU receives air temp | IAT sensor (for density correction) |
| 5 | ECU calculates base IPW from fuel map | $IPW_{base} = f(N, MAP)$ |
| 6 | ECU applies corrections | $IPW = IPW_{base} \times (1 + \alpha_{cool} + \alpha_{start} - \alpha_{lambda})$ |
| 7 | ECU fires injector | Pulse-width modulated solenoid |
| 8 | Lambda sensor feedback | $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.03$ in closed loop |
| 9 | Long-term fuel trim updated | Adaptive memory in ECU |
| 10 | Diagnostic checks | MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) on fault |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[EXHAUST ENERGY] --> H2[TURBINE]
    H2 --> H3[TURBOCHARGER COMPRESSES AIR]
    H4[CRANKSHAFT MECHANICAL ENERGY] --> H5[BELT]
    H5 --> H6[SUPERCHARGER COMPRESSES AIR]
    H3 --> H7[BOOSTED INTAKE]
    H6 --> H7
```

---

## 4.9 Combined Forced-Induction + Direct-Injection Architecture (Modern Turbo-GDI)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AIR[AMBIENT AIR] --> FA[FILTER]
    FA --> CMP[COMPRESSOR]
    CMP --> IC[INTERCOOLER]
    IC --> TB[THROTTLE]
    TB --> IM[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    IM --> CC[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    HP[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP 15 MPA] --> RAIL[FUEL RAIL]
    RAIL --> INJ[GDI PIEZO INJECTOR]
    INJ --> CC
    CC --> EXM[EXHAUST MANIFOLD]
    EXM --> TUR[TURBINE]
    TUR --> CAT[CATALYTIC CONVERTER]
    TUR -. shaft .-> CMP
    WG2[WASTEGATE] -. bypass .-> EXM
    ECU6[ECU] --> WG2
    ECU6 --> INJ
    ECU6 --> HP
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern, **Rev. Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L2 – L4**, and the **CO mapping (CO2 / CO3 of PEMET742)**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between MPFI and GDI fuel injection systems with respect to injection pressure, injection location, atomization, and starting behaviour under cold conditions.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

| Aspect | MPFI | GDI |
|---|---|---|
| Injection pressure | 0.3 – 0.5 MPa | 5 – 20 MPa |
| Location of injector | Intake port | Directly in cylinder |
| Atomization | Good, but wall-wetting possible | Excellent, very fine spray |
| Cold start | Easy (fuel vaporises on hot port wall) | Slightly harder (cold chamber walls, fuel impingement) — needs **injection timing retardation + multiple injections** |
| Compression ratio | 9 – 11 : 1 | 10 – 14 : 1 (lean stratified) |
| Specific power | Moderate | 15 – 25 % higher |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Explain in brief the role of a *wastegate* in a turbocharged petrol engine. What happens if the wastegate fails open?**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- The **wastegate** is a pressure-controlled bypass valve that opens when manifold pressure (boost) reaches a preset limit (typically 0.6 – 1.2 bar).
- It diverts exhaust gases away from the turbine wheel, capping maximum boost to protect the engine from **over-boost / detonation / piston/connecting-rod damage**.
- **If wastegate fails OPEN:** Turbine receives no exhaust, no boost, severe power loss, sluggish response, vehicle behaves as naturally aspirated.
- **If wastegate fails CLOSED:** Over-boost, engine knock, possible piston/head damage, ECU will cut fuel via MAP sensor override.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Two alternative questions are provided below — students answer **either** Q-A or Q-B.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a **neat labelled schematic diagram**, explain the working of a **CRDI system** and the function of the **common rail**. List the major components. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke CRDI diesel engine has a total displacement of **1.5 L**. At a speed of **3500 rpm**, the BMEP is **9.5 bar**, mechanical efficiency is **85 %**, and the indicated thermal efficiency is **38 %**. Calorific value of diesel = **43 000 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
> - (i) Indicated Power
> - (ii) Brake Power
> - (iii) Fuel consumption rate in kg/h
> - (iv) BSFC in g/kWh (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**
A neat schematic is shown in **Section 4.2** of these notes (CRDI Functional Block Diagram).

**Working Steps (recap from Section 2.2):**
1. The **transfer pump** pulls diesel from the tank at low pressure (~ 5 bar) and supplies it to the **high-pressure radial piston pump**.
2. The HP pump pressurises fuel to **135 – 200 MPa** and pushes it into the **common rail** (a pressurised accumulator).
3. The **rail pressure sensor** monitors pressure; ECU commands the **pressure control valve (PCV)** to bleed excess fuel back, maintaining target pressure.
4. ECU computes injection timing and quantity, and energises the **solenoid or piezo injector**.
5. The injector opens against rail pressure and sprays fuel through **multi-hole nozzle** (6 – 9 holes) directly into the **combustion chamber** at the precise time of injection.
6. **Pilot → main → after injection** sequence is used to reduce **diesel knock and NOₓ emissions**.

**Major Components of CRDI:**
- Fuel tank, fuel filter, transfer pump, HP pump, common rail, rail pressure sensor, pressure control valve, injectors (solenoid / piezo), ECU, various sensors (CKP, CMP, MAP, IAT, ECT).

**Function of Common Rail:** Acts as a high-pressure fuel accumulator that decouples pressure generation from injection events, allowing multiple precisely-timed injections per cycle at constant pressure regardless of engine speed.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $V_d = 1.5 \text{ L} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$, $N = 3500 \text{ rpm}$, $\text{BMEP}_b = 9.5 \text{ bar} = 9.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $\eta_m = 0.85$, $\eta_{th,i} = 0.38$, $CV = 43\,000 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $k = 4$, 4-stroke.

**Step 1 — Indicated BMEP**

$$
\text{BMEP}_i = \dfrac{\text{BMEP}_b}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{9.5}{0.85} = 11.176 \text{ bar} = 11.176 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Indicated Power (4-stroke)**

$$
P_i = \dfrac{\text{BMEP}_i \times V_d \times N}{2 \times 60}
$$

$$
P_i = \dfrac{11.176 \times 10^5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3500}{120}
$$

$$
P_i = \dfrac{11.176 \times 1.5 \times 35}{12} \times 10^2 = \dfrac{586.74}{12} \times 100
$$

$$
P_i = 48.895 \times 100 = 4889.5 \text{ W} = 48.90 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Brake Power**

$$
P_b = P_i \times \eta_m = 48.90 \times 0.85 = 41.56 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel Consumption Rate**

$$
P_i = \dot{m}_f \times CV \times \eta_{th,i}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_i}{CV \times \eta_{th,i}} = \dfrac{48.90}{43000 \times 0.38}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{48.90}{16340} = 2.992 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

Convert to kg/h:

$$
\dot{m}_f = 2.992 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 10.77 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — BSFC**

$$
\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{10.77}{41.56} = 0.2592 \text{ kg/kWh} = 259.2 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $P_i = 48.90 \text{ kW}$
- $P_b = 41.56 \text{ kW}$
- $\dot{m}_f = 10.77 \text{ kg/h}$
- $\text{BSFC} = 259.2 \text{ g/kWh}$

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the working of a **turbocharger**. Compare turbocharger and supercharger with respect to drive source, response, boost range, parasitic loss, and typical applications. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder, **turbocharged petrol GDI engine** running at **4000 rpm** has a volumetric efficiency of **95 %** and a manifold pressure of **1.8 bar (absolute)**. Ambient pressure = 1.013 bar, ambient temperature = 35 °C. The air-fuel ratio maintained is **stoichiometric (14.7 : 1)**, and the engine BMEP is **16 bar**. Calorific value of petrol = **44 000 kJ/kg**. Mechanical efficiency = 0.9. Find:
> - (i) Air density at manifold conditions
> - (ii) Air mass flow rate in kg/s (use displacement 1.8 L)
> - (iii) Indicated power
> - (iv) Brake thermal efficiency (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

A neat schematic is shown in **Section 4.6** of these notes (Turbocharger Functional Block Diagram).

**Working of Turbocharger:**
1. Hot exhaust gases from the engine's exhaust manifold strike the **turbine wheel** and spin it at very high speeds (100 000 – 300 000 rpm).
2. The turbine is mounted on a common shaft with a **centrifugal compressor**.
3. Spinning of the compressor draws in fresh air, compresses it, and delivers it to the **intake manifold** at above-atmospheric pressure (boost).
4. The compressed, hot air is then cooled by an **intercooler (charge air cooler)** to increase density.
5. The **wastegate** (or **VGT**) controls maximum boost.

**Turbocharger vs. Supercharger Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Turbocharger | Supercharger |
|---|---|---|
| Drive source | Exhaust gas energy | Belt from crankshaft |
| Response (lag) | Turbo-lag below ~ 2000 rpm | Instant response |
| Parasitic loss | None (free energy from exhaust) | 5 – 15 % of engine power |
| Boost range | 0.4 – 3.0 bar | 0.2 – 1.0 bar |
| Complexity | High (VNT, wastegate, intercooler) | Lower (pulley + bypass) |
| Typical use | Modern petrol/diesel cars, trucks | Dragsters, vintage cars, some SUVs |
| Heat in intake | High (intercooler needed) | High (intercooler advised) |

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $N = 4000 \text{ rpm}$, $\eta_v = 0.95$, $P_{manifold} = 1.8 \text{ bar (abs)}$, $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, $T_{atm} = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$, A/F = 14.7, BMEP = 16 bar, $V_d = 1.8 \text{ L} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$, CV = 44 000 kJ/kg, $\eta_m = 0.9$, 4-stroke.

**Step 1 — Air density at ambient conditions**

$$
\rho_{atm} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{R \times T_{atm}} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^5}{287 \times 308} = \dfrac{101300}{88496} = 1.145 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Air density at manifold (boost) conditions**

$$
\rho_{manifold} = \rho_{atm} \times \dfrac{P_{manifold}}{P_{atm}} = 1.145 \times \dfrac{1.8}{1.013} = 1.145 \times 1.777 = 2.035 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Air mass flow rate (4-stroke, intake every 2 revs)**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \rho_{manifold} \times \eta_v \times V_d \times \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 2.035 \times 0.95 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{4000}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 2.035 \times 0.95 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 33.33
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 2.035 \times 0.95 \times 0.06 = 0.1160 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\text{A/F}} = \dfrac{0.1160}{14.7} = 7.891 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Brake Power from BMEP**

$$
P_b = \dfrac{\text{BMEP}_b \times V_d \times N}{2 \times 60}
$$

$$
P_b = \dfrac{16 \times 10^5 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 4000}{120}
$$

$$
P_b = \dfrac{16 \times 1.8 \times 40}{12} \times 10^2 = \dfrac{1152}{12} \times 100 = 96 \times 100 = 9600 \text{ W} = 96 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Indicated Power**

$$
P_i = \dfrac{P_b}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{96}{0.9} = 106.67 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Brake Thermal Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{th,br} = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} = \dfrac{96}{7.891 \times 10^{-3} \times 44000}
$$

$$
\eta_{th,br} = \dfrac{96}{347.2} = 0.2765 = 27.65 \%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\rho_{manifold} = 2.035 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\dot{m}_a = 0.1160 \text{ kg/s}$
- $P_i = 106.67 \text{ kW}$
- $\eta_{th,br} = 27.65 \%$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Using the wrong 4-stroke / 2-stroke formula** — remember $N/2$ in the denominator for 4-stroke and $N$ for 2-stroke. Many students write $P = \text{BMEP} \times V_d \times N$ without dividing by 2, losing ~ 3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing BMEP_i and BMEP_b** — Indicated power uses *indicated* BMEP; brake power uses *brake* BMEP. Don't divide the same BMEP by $\eta_m$ and then multiply by $\eta_m$ again (null error).
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the absolute vs gauge pressure distinction in turbo problems.** The manifold pressure given in KTU problems is *always absolute*. Use $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar in density conversions.
> 
> 4. **Unit errors in fuel consumption:** $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/s must be multiplied by **3600** to convert to kg/h. Many students forget and get BSFC wrong.
> 
> 5. **Diagram labelling:** KTU examiners deduct 1 – 2 marks if a CRDI/Turbo schematic is drawn without a **rail pressure sensor** or **wastegate** label.
> 
> 6. **GDI and knock:** Many students wrongly state that GDI engines can use *any* compression ratio. In **homogeneous mode** the CR is limited by knock; only **stratified mode** allows higher CR.
> 
> 7. **Piezo vs. Solenoid confusion:** Piezo injectors do **not** use an electromagnetic coil. Stating "piezo injectors use a coil" loses 2 marks instantly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets (Before Exam):**
> 
> - **MPFI** = 1 injector per cylinder, in the **intake port**, pressure ≈ 3.5 bar, controlled by ECU.
> - **CRDI** = diesel, **direct injection**, common rail accumulator at 1500 – 2000 bar, 3 – 7 injections per cycle (pilot + main + after).
> - **GDI** = petrol, **direct injection**, 50 – 200 bar, two modes: **homogeneous** (medium load) and **stratified** (low load).
> - **GDI advantage:** Higher CR possible due to charge cooling, ~ 15 % fuel economy improvement over MPFI.
> - **GDI drawback:** Higher particulate emissions → requires **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)**.
> - **Solenoid injector:** Uses **electromagnetic coil**; response time 0.2 – 0.4 ms; cheaper.
> - **Piezo injector:** Uses **piezoelectric crystal stack**; response time 0.02 – 0.05 ms; allows up to 9 injections per cycle; ~ 2 – 3× costlier.
> - **Naturally aspirated:** Air intake depends on piston vacuum; $P_{manifold} \le P_{atm}$.
> - **Forced induction:** Air compressed above atmospheric pressure; $P_{manifold} > P_{atm}$.
> - **Turbocharger:** Driven by **exhaust energy**; no parasitic loss; suffers from **turbo-lag**; uses **wastegate** or **VGT** for boost control.
> - **Supercharger:** Driven **mechanically** by belt from crankshaft; instant response; 5 – 15 % parasitic loss.
> - **Key equations to memorise:**
>   - 4-stroke IP / BP: $P = \dfrac{\text{BMEP} \times V_d \times N}{2 \times 60}$
>   - $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$
>   - $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \times \eta_v \times V_d \times \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60}$ (4-stroke)
>   - $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}}$
> - **Stoichiometric ratios:** petrol = 14.7 : 1, diesel = 14.5 : 1.
> - **Lambda** $\lambda = 1$ is stoichiometric; $\lambda > 1$ lean; $\lambda < 1$ rich.
> - **Boost equation:** $r_p = P_{manifold} / P_{atm}$; power gain ≈ pressure ratio × $\eta_v$ ratio.
> - **Modern trend:** **Turbo-GDI** is the dominant engine architecture of the 2020s (VW TSI, Hyundai Kappa, Honda Earth Dreams, Ford EcoBoost).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — Module 2 — PEMET742 — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spark Ignition Systems - Components, Ignition Timing, Single Coil Ignition System and Coil Over Plug Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Spark Ignition Systems - Components, Ignition Timing, Single Coil Ignition System and Coil Over Plug Ignition System # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition: Spark Ignition System > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Spark Ignition (SI) System** in an internal combustion engine is an electrical subsystem that generates, stores, and delivers a high-voltage pulse (typically **15 kV to 40 kV**) across the spark plug gap at precisely the correct instant in the combustion cycle to ignite the compressed air-fuel mixture in the cylinder. The ignition system is the heart of every petrol/gasoline engine. Without it, the air-fuel mixture would simply be compressed and exhausted without ever releasing the chemical energy that propels the vehicle. The fundamental requirement is to convert the low-voltage battery supply (**12 V**) into a high-voltage surge capable of ionizing the spark plug gap to create an electric arc. ## 1.2 The Three Functional Duties of an Ignition System An ignition system must perform three critical jobs at the right time: 1. **Generate** a high-voltage surge (induction step-up). 2. **Distribute** the surge to the correct cylinder in the firing order. 3. **Time** the spark so it occurs at the optimal crank angle relative to Top Dead Center (TDC). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Lighter vs. The Matchbox > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the ignition system as a **gas stove lighter** and the air-fuel mixture as a cloud of fine gunpowder. > > - If you strike the lighter **too early** (before the gas is in the right position), nothing useful happens. > - If you strike the lighter **too late**, the gas has already moved away. > - If your lighter produces a **weak spark** (low energy), it will not light a lean mixture. > - If the spark jumps to the **wrong burner** (wrong cylinder), the whole cooking process is ruined. > > The job of the ignition system is to ensure the spark is **strong, accurate, and timely** — every single time, for thousands of kilometers. ## 1.4 Standard Electrical Metrics in Ignition Systems | Parameter | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Primary Voltage (Vp)** | **12 V** | Battery supply | | **Secondary Voltage (Vs)** | **15 kV – 40 kV** | Required to ionize spark gap | | **Spark Duration** | **1 – 2 ms** | Time to ensure reliable kernel formation | | **Spark Energy** | **30 mJ – 80 mJ** | Energy needed to ignite mixture | | **Dwell Time** | **3 ms – 20 ms** | Charging time of primary coil | | **Air-Fuel Ratio Target** | **14.7:1 (stoichiometric)** | Reference for ignition calibration | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 demands knowledge of (a) the main components, (b) ignition timing principles, (c) Single Coil Ignition System (battery/coil/distributor type), and (d) Coil-Over-Plug (COP) system. All four pillars are covered exhaustively in this note. --- # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Functional Components of a Spark Ignition System A conventional spark ignition system comprises the following building blocks. Each has a precise function: ### A. **Battery (Energy Source)** - Standard **12 V** lead-acid accumulator. - Provides primary current to the ignition coil. - Cannot directly produce the high voltage needed; therefore requires step-up transformer action. ### B. **Ignition Switch** - Driver-controlled ON/OFF switch. - Energizes the primary circuit of the coil. ### C. **Ignition Coil (Step-Up Transformer)** - A high-ratio transformer (turns ratio typically **1:60 to 1:200**). - Has two windings: * **Primary winding**: ~150–300 turns of thick copper wire (low resistance, **0.5 – 3 Ω**). * **Secondary winding**: ~15,000–30,000 turns of fine copper wire (high resistance, **3 – 10 kΩ**). - Steps up 12 V to 15,000–40,000 V via electromagnetic induction. - Stores energy in its magnetic field: $E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$. ### D. **Contact Breaker (Mechanical System) / Electronic Control Unit (Electronic System)** - Old systems used **breaker points** (mechanical switch) opened by a cam. - Modern systems use a **transistorized ECU/igniter** triggered by a **crankshaft position sensor (CKP)** and **camshaft position sensor (CMP)**. ### E. **Distributor (Mechanical and Electronic)** - Rotates to direct the high-voltage surge to the correct cylinder. - Has a **rotor arm** (spinning contact) and **cap with terminals** (one per cylinder). - In modern systems, distributor is replaced by direct coil-to-plug wiring or individual coils. ### F. **Condenser / Capacitor** - Connected across the contact breaker points. - Prevents arcing at the points when they open. - Speeds up the collapse of the magnetic field, increasing induced voltage. - Typical value: **0.20 – 0.25 μF**. ### G. **Spark Plug (Output Device)** - Mounted in the cylinder head, projecting into the combustion chamber. - Has two electrodes separated by a **gap of 0.6 – 1.2 mm**. - High voltage ionizes the air-fuel mixture, creating a conductive plasma channel. - Spark plug heat range is selected based on engine operating conditions. ### H. **Centrifugal & Vacuum Advance Mechanisms** - **Centrifugal advance**: Adjusts timing based on engine RPM (faster engine → more advance). - **Vacuum advance**: Adjusts based on manifold vacuum (engine load). ## 2.2 Ignition Timing — The Heart of Engine Performance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ignition Timing Definition:** The precise crank-angle position (measured in degrees before or after TDC of the compression stroke) at which the spark plug fires. ### 2.2.1 Why Ignition Timing Is Critical The air-fuel mixture does **not** burn instantaneously. Combustion requires: 1. **Ignition lag (1–3 ms)**: Time for the flame kernel to grow. 2. **Flame propagation phase**: Flame front travels across the chamber at **15–25 m/s** in turbulent conditions. 3. **Completion of combustion**: Peak cylinder pressure should occur at **12° – 15° ATDC** for maximum work output. If spark occurs **exactly at TDC**, peak pressure occurs too late → power loss. Therefore, spark must occur **before TDC** (advance) to allow the pressure peak to be in the right place. ### 2.2.2 Types of Ignition Advance 1. **Initial (Static) Advance**: Set during engine manufacture/manufacturer calibration. Typically **5° – 10° BTDC**. 2. **Centrifugal Advance (RPM-based)**: Increases with engine speed. At idle ~0°, at 3000 RPM ~20°–30°. 3. **Vacuum Advance (Load-based)**: Increases at part throttle, decreases at WOT. 4. **Electronic Advance (Modern)**: Calculated by ECU from MAP, MAF, RPM, and knock sensor data. ### 2.2.3 Effects of Incorrect Timing - **Too much advance**: Knock (auto-ignition), overheating, engine damage. - **Too little advance (retarded)**: Power loss, increased exhaust temperature, poor fuel economy. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Table for KTU Numerical Problems.** The following equations are tested frequently in University Examinations. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_s = V_p \times \left(\frac{N_s}{N_p}\right)$ | Transformer turn-ratio relation between primary and secondary voltage | Volts | | 2 | $V_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's law for induced secondary EMF | Volts | | 3 | $E_{coil} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | Energy stored in primary coil magnetic field | Joules | | 4 | $\theta_d = \omega \times t_d$ | Dwell angle = angular velocity × dwell time | degrees | | 5 | $\theta_a = k_1 \cdot N$ | Centrifugal advance (linear approximation), k₁ = constant | degrees | | 6 | $\theta_v = k_2 (P_a - P_m)$ | Vacuum advance = constant × (atmospheric − manifold pressure) | degrees | | 7 | $\theta_{total} = \theta_{static} + \theta_{centrifugal} + \theta_{vacuum}$ | Total ignition advance | degrees BTDC | | 8 | $v_{flame} = \dfrac{r \cdot \omega}{tan(\alpha)}$ | Flame speed relation (α = flame cone half-angle) | m/s | | 9 | $P_{max} \text{ at } 12°-15° \text{ ATDC}$ | Empirical optimal peak pressure location | degrees ATDC | | 10 | $I_p = \dfrac{V_p}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-R_p t / L_p}\right)$ | Primary current growth equation | Amps | > [!CAUTION] > **Critical:** All turns-ratio questions require the **secondary voltage** to be computed using the dot convention: the primary current is suddenly interrupted (not just reduced), causing $dI/dt$ to be very large, hence secondary voltage becomes very large. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The ignition system is the foundation of: - **Engine starting** (cold-start cranking) - **Idling stability** - **Part-throttle fuel economy** (10–20% of total fuel saving) - **Knock control** (prevents engine destruction) - **Emissions reduction** (proper timing minimizes HC and NOx) - **Modern direct injection** systems rely on precise ignition control for stratified charge and homogeneous modes. --- # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Secondary Voltage Induced in Ignition Coil ### 3.1.1 Faraday's Law Applied to the Ignition Coil When the contact breaker opens, the primary current collapses from $I_p$ to zero in a very short time (typically **50–200 μs**). This sudden change in current causes a corresponding collapse in magnetic flux. $$ V_p = -N_p \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} $$ Since the flux links both primary and secondary windings: $$ \dfrac{V_s}{V_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} $$ Therefore, the secondary voltage becomes: $$ V_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_p $$ ### 3.1.2 Practical Energy Build-up During Dwell During the **dwell period** (when contact breaker is closed), primary current builds up exponentially. The current at the end of dwell is: $$ I_p(t) = \dfrac{V_p}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-R_p t / L_p}\right) $$ At steady state ($t \to \infty$): $I_{p,\max} = \dfrac{V_p}{R_p}$. The magnetic energy stored is: $$ E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2 $$ This energy is what gets released as a high-voltage spark when the contacts open. > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Example (KTU Style):** A coil has $L_p = 5 \text{ mH}$, $R_p = 2 \ \Omega$, $V_p = 12 \text{ V}$, dwell $t = 4 \text{ ms}$. Find stored energy. > > $I_p = \dfrac{12}{2}\left(1 - e^{-2 \times 0.004 / 0.005}\right) = 6 \times (1 - e^{-1.6}) = 6 \times 0.798 = 4.79 \text{ A}$ > > $E = 0.5 \times 0.005 \times (4.79)^2 = 0.0573 \text{ J} = 57.3 \text{ mJ}$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Required Advance Angle as a Function of Engine Speed ### 3.2.1 The Time-Equivalence Argument The combustion process requires a fixed **flame travel time** ($t_f$) of approximately **1–3 ms** for the flame front to traverse the chamber radius $r$. The crank must rotate by an angle $\theta_a$ during this time to position peak pressure correctly. $$ \theta_a = \omega \cdot t_f = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \cdot t_f $$ where $N$ is engine speed in RPM and $t_f$ is fixed flame time. ### 3.2.2 Implication Doubling the engine speed from **1000 RPM** to **2000 RPM** doubles $\omega$, hence doubles $\theta_a$. This is exactly why **centrifugal advance is required** — higher RPM needs more advance. ### 3.2.3 Full Advance Equation (Including Retard at Idle) For practical calibration: $$ \theta_a(N) = \theta_{idle} + k \cdot (N - N_{idle}) $$ where: - $\theta_{idle} \approx 5° - 10°$ BTDC - $k$ = advance rate (degrees per 1000 RPM), typically **5°–15° per 1000 RPM** ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Ignition System Architectures > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table.** This matrix appears almost every year in the KTU Automobile Engineering paper. | Parameter | Battery-Coil (Single Coil) | Distributor-less (DIS) | Coil-Over-Plug (COP) | |---|---|---|---| | **Number of Coils** | **1** (for all cylinders) | **2 (wasted spark)** or **n/2** | **n (one per cylinder)** | | **Distribution Method** | Mechanical rotor & cap | Paired cylinders | Direct plug connection | | **Spark Energy per Cylinder** | Shared, lower (40–60 mJ) | Higher (60–100 mJ) | Highest (80–120 mJ) | | **Mechanical Wear** | High (rotor, cap, points) | Moderate | Almost none | | **Maintenance** | Frequent | Low | Negligible | | **Timing Accuracy** | Low | Good | Excellent | | **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | **Voltage Available** | ~25 kV | ~30 kV | ~40 kV | | **Use in Modern Cars** | Obsolete | Older (1990s) | **Universal today** | | **Failure Mode** | Distributor cap failure | Coil pair failure | Individual coil failure | ## 3.4 Derivation: Spark Plug Gap Ionization Voltage (Paschen's Law) The voltage required to ionize the spark plug gap is given approximately by: $$ V_{breakdown} = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\left[\ln\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]} $$ where: - $p$ = gas pressure in gap - $d$ = gap distance - $A, B$ = gas-specific Townsend constants - $\gamma_{se}$ = secondary ionization coefficient At **15 bar** in-cylinder pressure, the breakdown voltage for a **0.8 mm** gap is **8 – 12 kV**, but coils are designed to deliver **20 – 40 kV** to overcome this under all operating conditions. ## 3.5 Worked Example: Dwell Angle Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine runs at 3000 RPM. Dwell time is 4 ms. Find dwell angle. **Solution:** $$ \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3000}{60} = 100 \pi \text{ rad/s} $$ $$ \theta_d = \omega \times t_d = 100 \pi \times 4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.4 \pi \text{ rad} = 72° $$ > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert RPM to rad/s. Always write: $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ explicitly. The 4-stroke nature of the engine means a complete cycle is **720°**, not 360°. --- # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Spark Ignition System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BATTERY
12V DC] --> B[IGNITION SWITCH] B --> C[PRIMARY WINDING
of Ignition Coil] C --> D[CONTACT BREAKER
or Electronic Switch] D --> E[CONDENSER
0.2 microF] D --> F[SECONDARY WINDING
of Ignition Coil] F --> G[DISTRIBUTOR
Rotor and Cap] G --> H1[SPARK PLUG
Cylinder 1] G --> H2[SPARK PLUG
Cylinder 2] G --> H3[SPARK PLUG
Cylinder 3] G --> H4[SPARK PLUG
Cylinder 4] I[CKP and CMP SENSORS] --> J[ECU / IGNITER] J --> D ``` ## 4.2 Single Coil Ignition System (Battery-Coil-Distributor Type) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SC["SINGLE COIL IGNITION SYSTEM"] BAT[Battery 12V] SW[Ignition Switch] R1[Ballast Resistor] COIL[Ignition Coil
Primary and Secondary] CON[Condenser] CB[Contact Breaker
Cam Operated] DIS[Distributor Cap
and Rotor] SP1[Spark Plug 1] SP2[Spark Plug 2] SP3[Spark Plug 3] SP4[Spark Plug 4] end BAT --> SW --> R1 --> COIL COIL --> CB CB --- CON COIL --> DIS DIS --> SP1 DIS --> SP2 DIS --> SP3 DIS --> SP4 CAM[Camshaft] --> CB CAM --> DIS ``` **Operating Sequence:** 1. Switch closes → primary current rises through ballast resistor. 2. Camshaft rotates the cam → contact breaker opens. 3. Primary current collapses → high voltage induced in secondary. 4. Rotor directs the surge to the correct cylinder's spark plug. 5. Spark ignites the mixture → cycle repeats for next cylinder. ## 4.3 Coil-Over-Plug (COP) Ignition System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COP["COIL OVER PLUG SYSTEM"] ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU] DRV[Ignition Driver
IGBT or MOSFET] C1[Coil Pack 1] C2[Coil Pack 2] C3[Coil Pack 3] C4[Coil Pack 4] SP1[Spark Plug 1] SP2[Spark Plug 2] SP3[Spark Plug 3] SP4[Spark Plug 4] CKP[CKP Sensor] CMP[CMP Sensor] KNOCK[Knock Sensor] end CKP --> ECU CMP --> ECU KNOCK --> ECU ECU --> DRV DRV --> C1 --> SP1 DRV --> C2 --> SP2 DRV --> C3 --> SP3 DRV --> C4 --> SP4 BAT[Battery 12V] --> C1 BAT --> C2 BAT --> C3 BAT --> C4 ``` **Key Features of Coil-Over-Plug:** - **One coil directly mounted on top of each spark plug** (no HT leads). - No distributor, no rotor, no wear parts. - Each cylinder has **independent timing control**. - **Wasted spark** is eliminated. - **Secondary voltage** can reach **40 kV+** because there is no lead capacitance to charge. - IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor) inside the coil module switches the primary current. ## 4.4 Ignition Timing Advance Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Idle 800 RPM
10 deg BTDC] --> B[1500 RPM
18 deg BTDC] B --> C[2500 RPM
28 deg BTDC] C --> D[3500 RPM
35 deg BTDC] D --> E[4500 RPM
38 deg BTDC] E --> F[5500 RPM
37 deg BTDC
retard begins] F --> G[6500 RPM
32 deg BTDC] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Curve:** Timing increases with RPM (centrifugal effect) up to a maximum around **35° – 40° BTDC**, after which the ECU retards timing to prevent knock and protect the engine at high loads. ## 4.5 Signal Flow: ECU Decision Making for Ignition Timing ```mermaid flowchart TD IN1[RPM from CKP] --> ECU IN2[Load from MAP or MAF] --> ECU IN3[Coolant Temperature] --> ECU IN4[Knock Sensor Signal] --> ECU IN5[Camshaft Position] --> ECU ECU --> OUT1[Calculate Total Advance] OUT1 --> OUT2[Determine Dwell Time] OUT2 --> OUT3[Trigger IGBT at Correct Angle] OUT3 --> COIL[Fire Spark Plug] KNOCK{Knock Detected?} -->|Yes| RETARD[Retard Timing by 3 to 5 deg] KNOCK -->|No| NORM[Maintain Timing] RETARD --> ECU NORM --> ECU ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Define ignition timing. Why is it necessary to advance the spark before TDC?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Ignition Timing** is the precise crank angle position at which the spark plug fires, measured in degrees before or after Top Dead Center (TDC) of the compression stroke. > > It is necessary to advance the spark before TDC because the air-fuel mixture does not burn instantaneously. A finite time (1–3 ms) is required for the flame kernel to form and the flame front to propagate across the combustion chamber. If the spark occurred exactly at TDC, the resulting peak cylinder pressure would occur too late (well past 15° ATDC), resulting in significant loss of useful work, reduced power output, and overheating of the exhaust valve. By advancing the spark, the peak pressure is positioned at **12° – 15° ATDC**, where maximum work is extracted from the combustion gases. > > **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for flame time justification, 1 mark for peak pressure positioning. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]` **State the function of a condenser in a contact-breaker type ignition system.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > A **condenser (capacitor)** of value **0.20 – 0.25 μF** is connected across the contact breaker points in parallel. Its two main functions are: > > 1. **Absorbs the back-EMF**: When the points open, the inductive primary winding generates a self-induced EMF that would otherwise cause heavy arcing across the points, leading to rapid wear and pitting. > > 2. **Speeds up collapse of magnetic field**: The capacitor allows the primary current to drop to zero almost instantaneously (in ~10 μs), which produces a much higher rate of change of flux ($d\Phi/dt$), thereby increasing the induced secondary voltage. Without the condenser, the induced voltage would be too low to jump the spark plug gap. > > **(3 marks)** — 1 mark each for the two functions and 1 mark for typical value or numerical justification. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Pattern Match]` **With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Single Coil Ignition System (Battery-Coil type). State the function of each component.** **Model Solution Outline:** **(a) Block Diagram and Working — 7 Marks** The single coil ignition system uses one ignition coil and one mechanical distributor to serve all cylinders. Working steps: 1. **Ignition switch ON** completes the primary circuit: Battery 12 V → Ignition switch → Ballast resistor → Primary winding → Contact breaker points → Ground. 2. Primary current builds up exponentially: $I_p(t) = \dfrac{V_p}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-R_p t / L_p}\right)$. 3. **Cam-driven contact breaker opens**: The lobe of the cam (driven by the distributor shaft at half engine speed for 4-stroke engines) forces the contact points apart. 4. **Magnetic field collapses** in the iron core of the coil. The condenser absorbs the back-EMF and accelerates collapse. 5. **High voltage (15–25 kV)** is induced in the secondary winding by transformer action: $V_s = V_p \times (N_s/N_p)$. 6. The high voltage travels to the **rotor arm** of the distributor, which is positioned so that the rotor tip is adjacent to the correct cap terminal. 7. The surge jumps the rotor-to-cap air gap and travels through the **HT cable** to the corresponding **spark plug**. 8. The spark plug gap ionizes, the mixture ignites, and the cycle repeats for the next cylinder in firing order. **(b) Function of Each Component — 7 Marks** | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Battery** | Provides 12 V DC source of electrical energy | | **Ignition Switch** | Driver-controlled ON/OFF for the primary circuit | | **Ballast Resistor** | Limits primary current; compensates for battery voltage variation | | **Ignition Coil** | Steps up 12 V to 15–25 kV using transformer action; stores magnetic energy | | **Condenser** | Prevents arcing at contact points; speeds up flux collapse | | **Contact Breaker** | Mechanically interrupts primary current at correct instant | | **Distributor (Rotor + Cap)** | Routes high voltage to correct cylinder; contains centrifugal and vacuum advance | | **Spark Plug** | Provides the air gap for spark generation in the combustion chamber | | **Centrifugal Advance** | Increases advance with RPM using flyweight mechanism | | **Vacuum Advance** | Increases advance at part throttle using manifold vacuum diaphragm | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently draw the **distributor rotor and cap** incorrectly — remember the rotor turns at **half engine speed** (cam speed) in a 4-stroke engine, and the firing order depends on the engine configuration. Marks are lost for incorrect firing order numbering (1-3-4-2 vs 1-2-3-4) and for failing to mention the **ballast resistor's role** in voltage compensation. Always label all 10 components in the block diagram. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Explain the construction and working of a Coil-Over-Plug (COP) ignition system. Compare it with the conventional single coil ignition system.** **Model Solution Outline:** **(a) Construction and Working of COP — 7 Marks** In a **Coil-Over-Plug (COP)** system, also called **Individual Coil Ignition**, each cylinder has its own dedicated ignition coil mounted directly on top of its spark plug, eliminating HT cables, distributor cap, and rotor. **Construction:** - **n coils** (one per cylinder), each a compact sealed module. - Each module contains: **primary winding**, **secondary winding**, **ferrite core**, and an **integrated IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)** switch. - The module plugs directly into a bore in the cylinder head, with its lower terminal connecting to the spark plug. - A **4-pin connector** supplies: 12 V battery, ground, and two control signals from the ECU. - **CKP and CMP sensors** provide crankshaft and camshaft position to the ECU. - A **knock sensor** mounted on the engine block provides feedback. **Working:** 1. ECU reads CKP/CMP signals to identify which cylinder is on compression stroke. 2. ECU computes **optimal ignition advance** from a 3-D map indexed by RPM, load, coolant temperature, and knock feedback. 3. ECU sends a **trigger pulse** to the IGBT of the appropriate coil module. 4. IGBT switches **ON** → primary current ramps up (dwell time controlled by ECU). 5. IGBT switches **OFF** → primary current collapses → secondary voltage (30–40 kV) is induced. 6. Voltage jumps the spark plug gap → mixture ignites. 7. Each cylinder fires independently; there is no wasted spark. **(b) Comparison with Single Coil System — 7 Marks** | Feature | Single Coil | Coil-Over-Plug | |---|---|---| | Number of coils | 1 | n (one per cylinder) | | Distribution | Mechanical rotor & cap | None — direct | | Wear parts | Many (rotor, cap, points) | Almost none | | Spark energy | 40–60 mJ | 80–120 mJ | | Secondary voltage | 15–25 kV | 30–40 kV | | Timing control | Mechanical (less accurate) | ECU-based (very accurate) | | Maintenance | High | Almost zero | | Cost | Low | High | | Diagnostic ease | Easy (cap visible) | Needs OBD scanner | | Reliability | Moderate | Very high | | Application | Old vehicles (pre-1990) | All modern cars | **Advantages of COP:** - No HT cable losses → full voltage reaches plug. - Knock sensor feedback allows real-time advance correction. - Compact engine bay design. - Misfire detection per cylinder. **Disadvantages of COP:** - Expensive to replace if a single coil fails. - Heat from cylinder head reduces coil life. - Complex ECU software required. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **Coil-Over-Plug (COP)** with **Distributor-less Ignition System (DIS)**. Remember: **DIS uses paired coils (wasted spark)** for 2 cylinders, while **COP uses one coil per cylinder** (no wasted spark). The IGBT inside each COP module is a critical detail that must be mentioned for full marks. Marks are also lost for not specifying the **typical secondary voltage** and **spark energy** values. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision List — Memorize Before Exam** - ☐ Ignition system converts **12 V → 15,000–40,000 V**. - ☐ Coil turns ratio: **1:60 to 1:200** (primary:secondary). - ☐ Stored coil energy: $E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$. - ☐ **Condenser value**: 0.20 – 0.25 μF. - ☐ Spark plug gap: **0.6 – 1.2 mm**. - ☐ Spark plug energy: **30 – 120 mJ**. - ☐ Spark duration: **1 – 2 ms**. - ☐ Dwell time: **3 – 20 ms** (rpm dependent). - ☐ Ignition timing: measured in **degrees BTDC**. - ☐ Peak pressure should occur at **12° – 15° ATDC**. - ☐ Centrifugal advance: increases with RPM. - ☐ Vacuum advance: increases with low load (high vacuum). - ☐ Total advance = static + centrifugal + vacuum (or ECU-calculated). - ☐ **Single Coil System**: 1 coil + mechanical distributor. - ☐ **Coil-Over-Plug**: n coils, one per cylinder, ECU-controlled, no distributor. - ☐ COP secondary voltage reaches **40 kV+**. - ☐ Knock sensor retarding: typically **3° – 5°** per knock event. - ☐ Distributor rotates at **half engine speed** (4-stroke). - ☐ IGBT in COP coil module switches primary current. - ☐ 4-stroke cycle = **720°**; dwell angle is part of this 720°. > [!CAUTION] > **Final KTU Examiner's Note:** Always draw **neat, labeled block diagrams** in the exam. Label every component. Use **correct units**. Mention **typical numerical values** to show depth. Use the comparison table format for any "compare and contrast" question. The 14-mark questions in KTU Automobile Engineering require both **theoretical explanation** and **diagram support** — missing either costs 2–3 marks easily.

topics.title: Lubrication System - Basic Circuitry, Oil Grade and Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lubrication System - Basic Circuitry, Oil Grade and Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubrication System (KTU Definition):** A *lubrication system* in an internal combustion (IC) engine is an integrated arrangement of mechanical components, oil passages (drillings/galleries), reservoirs, pumps, filters and control valves designed to deliver clean lubricating oil at the correct pressure, flow rate and temperature to all moving parts of the engine (bearings, pistons, camshaft, valve gear, timing chain, etc.) so as to minimise friction, wear, and the removal of combustion heat.

**Basic Oil Circuit:** The closed-loop path that the engine oil follows, beginning at the **sump (oil pan)** → **oil pickup/strainer** → **oil pump** → **pressure relief valve** → **oil filter** → **main oil gallery** → **branch galleries** → **bearing/journal clearances** → **drainage back to sump** is collectively called the *basic lubrication circuitry*.

**Viscosity:** Viscosity is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the applied shear stress ($\tau$) to the rate of shear deformation (velocity gradient, $\frac{du}{dy}$). The SI unit is **Pa·s (Pascal-second)** or **N·s/m²**; the CGS unit is **Poise (P)** where $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$, and **centipoise (cP) = mPa·s**.

**Oil Grade (SAE Grade):** An oil grade is a standard classification of crankcase lubricants by the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** based purely on viscosity at specified temperatures (e.g., SAE 10W, SAE 30, SAE 20W-40). "W" denotes *Winter* (low-temperature) performance.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Bloodstream" of an Engine:** Think of the lubrication system as the **bloodstream** of the engine. The *oil pan (sump)* is the **heart's reservoir**, the *oil pump* is the **heart** that pumps fluid, the *oil filter* is the **kidney** that cleans the blood, the *oil galleries* are the **arteries**, the *bearings* are the **organs** being supplied, and the *drain-back passages* are the **veins** returning the fluid. Just as blood must be the correct thickness to flow well in cold and hot weather, engine oil must have the correct **viscosity** — too thick (like cold honey) and it cannot reach the bearings quickly at start-up; too thin (like water) and it cannot form a strong oil film under high loads.

**Why Viscosity is the "DNA" of Oil:** Just as a person's blood type determines compatibility, an oil's **viscosity grade** decides whether it can protect your engine. Manufacturers print two numbers on the bottle (e.g., **20W-50**): the first (with "W") tells you how the oil flows in winter/cold starts, and the second tells you how it holds up when the engine is scorching hot at highway speeds.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

| Term | Standard / Metric |
|---|---|
| Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity ($\mu$) | **Pa·s** (SI), **Poise (P)** (CGS) |
| Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | **m²/s** (SI), **Stoke (St)** (CGS), **cSt** (practical) |
| Viscosity Index (VI) | Dimensionless number (no units) |
| Oil Pressure (normal running) | **1.5 to 4.0 bar** (typical for petrol cars) |
| SAE "W" Temperature Reference | $-18^\circ\text{C}$ to $-25^\circ\text{C}$ depending on grade |
| API Service Classification | **S** (Spark ignition) / **C** (Compression ignition) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Blocks of a Basic Lubrication Circuit

The basic lubrication circuitry consists of the following functionally interlinked sub-systems. Each part has a distinct *role* in the *why* and *how* of the lubrication process:

1. **Oil Sump (Wet Sump) / Reservoir** — *Why:* Acts as a storage tank and heat sink. *How:* Oil drains here by gravity after lubricating the engine, allowing air separation and partial cooling.

2. **Oil Strainer / Pickup Tube with Mesh Screen** — *Why:* Prevents large debris (metal shavings, gasket pieces) from entering the pump. *How:* A coarse mesh at the inlet of the pickup tube.

3. **Oil Pump (Gear / Rotor / Vane type)** — *Why:* Generates the pressure required to circulate oil. *How:* Mechanically driven by the camshaft (or crankshaft) via gears/chain, delivering a positive displacement of oil.

4. **Pressure Relief Valve** — *Why:* Protects the system from over-pressurisation (which could rupture gaskets or filters). *How:* A spring-loaded valve that opens and bypasses oil back to the sump when pressure exceeds the calibration (typically **3.5 – 5 bar**).

5. **Oil Filter (Full-Flow Type)** — *Why:* Removes microscopic contaminants (dirt, carbon, soot). *How:* A pleated paper element with a by-pass valve for cold-start or blocked-filter safety.

6. **Main Oil Gallery** — *Why:* Distributes pressurised oil to all critical components. *How:* A series of drilled passages inside the cylinder block and head.

7. **Branch Galleries & Drillings** — *Why:* Deliver oil to specific bearings (main, big-end, camshaft, rocker arm). *How:* Cross-drilled holes from the main gallery.

8. **Oil Cooler (Optional in Petrol, Standard in Diesel/Turbocharged)** — *Why:* Maintains oil temperature between **80°C – 110°C** for optimal viscosity. *How:* Air-cooled finned block or water-cooled heat exchanger.

9. **Drain-Back Passages** — *Why:* Allow gravity return of oil to the sump. *How:* Inclined drilled holes and vertical slots at the back of the cylinder block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Lubrication Systems (KTU Module-2 High-Yield):**
> 1. **Splash Lubrication** — Big-end of connecting rod dips into oil and splashes it onto cylinder walls. (Used in small 2-stroke engines, vintage cars).
> 2. **Pressure Lubrication** — Oil is delivered under pressure to main bearings and camshaft; splash used for cylinder walls.
> 3. **Splash + Pressure (Combined) System** — Most common in modern multi-cylinder engines. (e.g., Maruti, Honda, Toyota petrol engines).
> 4. **Dry-Sump System** — Oil is stored in a separate external tank; used in racing, high-performance and off-road vehicles (e.g., Chevrolet Corvette, Formula cars).
> 5. **Mist Lubrication** — Oil mixed with fuel (2-stroke petrol engines).
> 6. **Capillary / Wick lubrication** — Used for slow-moving instrument parts only.

---

### 2.2 Viscosity — The Heart of Lubricant Selection

#### 2.2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity
For a Newtonian fluid (engine oil is treated as Newtonian at moderate shear rates):

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), and $\frac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient (s⁻¹).

#### 2.2.2 Dynamic vs. Kinematic Viscosity

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$
