where $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s or cSt), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, and $\rho$ = density of oil (kg/m³).

#### 2.2.3 Viscosity Index (VI)
An empirical, dimensionless number indicating the *rate of change* of oil viscosity with temperature. Higher VI = less viscosity change with temperature = better oil.

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

where $L$, $U$, $H$ are pre-tabulated viscosities of reference oils at 40°C and 100°C.

#### 2.2.4 SAE Viscosity Classification (The "Oil Grade")
The SAE J300 standard classifies engine oils purely by viscosity:

- **Single-Grade Oils:** Have only one viscosity classification, e.g., SAE 30 (suitable for a narrow temperature range).
- **Multi-Grade Oils:** Carry two classifications, e.g., **SAE 20W-50** — behaves like SAE 20W when cold and SAE 50 when hot, achieved through **Viscosity Index Improvers (VII)** which are long-chain polymers (e.g., polyisobutylene, polymethacrylates).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the basic oil circuit (KTU Examiner Favourite):** "**S**ump → **S**trainer → **P**ump → **R**elief valve → **F**ilter → **G**allery → **B**earings → **D**rain → back to **S**ump." — **SSPRFG BDS**

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity; shear stress in oil film | $\tau$ in Pa, $\mu$ in Pa·s |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity relation | $\nu$ in m²/s |
| 3 | $F = \tau \cdot A$ | Viscous drag force on a bearing surface | N |
| 4 | $T = F \cdot r = \mu \cdot A \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous torque on a journal bearing | N·m |
| 5 | $h_{\min} = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{2\mu N}{P}}\right)$ | Minimum oil-film thickness (for hydrodynamically lubricated journal) | m |
| 6 | $\text{VI} = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | Viscosity Index empirical formula | dimensionless |
| 7 | $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ | CGS to SI conversion | — |
| 8 | $1 \text{ Stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 1 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$ | CGS to SI conversion (kinematic) | — |
| 9 | $1 \text{ cSt} = 1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$ | Practical unit (used on oil data sheets) | — |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot N}{60}$ | Theoretical pump delivery (gear pump) | m³/s |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearing Design:** Viscosity dictates the load-carrying capacity of hydrodynamic journal bearings in the crankshaft.
- **Cold-Start Wear:** At $-20^\circ\text{C}$, a multigrade **5W-30** flows ~20× faster than a monograde **SAE 30**, drastically reducing the dry-start wear.
- **Fuel Economy:** Lower-viscosity oils (e.g., **0W-20**) are mandated by modern BS-VI / Euro 6 engines to reduce hydrodynamic friction and improve mileage by 2–4%.
- **Oil-Cooling Integration:** In turbocharged diesel engines (e.g., Tata Nexon EV's prior diesel variant, BMW dCi), oil coolers are critical to keep viscosity within the optimal band.
- **Hybrid & EV Applications:** Even in hybrids (e.g., Toyota Hyryder), engine oil circuits still protect the ICE part — viscosity choice affects CO₂ emissions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Viscous Torque on a Rotating Journal Bearing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context (KTU Style):** A crankshaft journal of diameter $D$ and length $L$ rotates at $N$ rpm inside a bearing. The radial clearance (oil-film thickness) is $c$, and the oil has dynamic viscosity $\mu$. Derive an expression for the **viscous frictional torque** $T_f$ resisting rotation.

#### Step 1 — Set up the velocity gradient
The shaft surface moves at velocity $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$, while the bearing surface is stationary. Across the small film thickness $c$, the velocity drops linearly (Couette flow assumption):

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{u}{c} = \frac{\pi D N}{60 \, c}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Newton's law of viscosity
Shear stress acting on the journal surface:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{\pi D N}{60 \, c}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the total frictional force
The contact (wetted) area of the journal is $A = \pi D L$:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \frac{\pi D N}{60 \, c} \cdot (\pi D L)$$

$$F_f = \frac{\mu \, \pi^{2} D^{2} L \, N}{60 \, c}$$

#### Step 4 — Convert force to torque
The friction force acts at the journal radius $r = D/2$. Torque is force × radius:

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{\mu \, \pi^{2} D^{2} L \, N}{60 \, c} \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_f = \frac{\mu \, \pi^{2} D^{3} L \, N}{120 \, c}\,}$$

**Dimensional check:** $\mu$ [Pa·s] × $D^3$ [m³] × $L$ [m] × $N$ [1/s] / $c$ [m] = Pa·m³ = N·m ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question (4-Mark Sub-part):** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a crankshaft main journal of diameter $D = 60 \text{ mm}$, length $L = 25 \text{ mm}$, running at $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$. The oil used has dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.025 \text{ Pa·s}$ and the radial clearance $c = 50 \text{ \mu m}$. Calculate (a) the viscous frictional torque on one journal, and (b) the total viscous frictional power loss in the **5 main bearings** of the engine.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- $L = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $N = 3000 \text{ rpm} = 50 \text{ rev/s}$
- $\mu = 0.025 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $c = 50 \text{ \mu m} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

#### Sub-part (a): Viscous Torque on One Journal

$$T_f = \frac{\mu \, \pi^{2} D^{3} L \, N}{120 \, c}$$

Step 1 — Calculate $D^{3}$:
$$D^{3} = (0.06)^{3} = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}$$

Step 2 — Substitute into numerator:
$$\text{Numerator} = 0.025 \times \pi^{2} \times (2.16 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.025 \times 50$$
$$= 0.025 \times 9.8696 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 \times 50$$
$$= 0.025 \times 9.8696 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.25$$
$$= 6.663 \times 10^{-5}$$

Step 3 — Denominator:
$$\text{Denominator} = 120 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

Step 4 — Final torque:
$$T_f = \frac{6.663 \times 10^{-5}}{6.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01111 \text{ N·m} \approx 0.0111 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Result:** $T_f \approx 0.0111 \text{ N·m}$ per journal.

#### Sub-part (b): Total Power Loss in 5 Main Bearings

Total torque on 5 bearings:
$$T_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 0.0111 = 0.0555 \text{ N·m}$$

Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

Power loss:
$$P = T_{\text{total}} \cdot \omega = 0.0555 \times 314.16$$
$$\boxed{\,P \approx 17.43 \text{ W}\,}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** ~17 W of mechanical power is wasted just churning oil in the bearings. In a 70 kW engine, this is only **0.025%** — a tribute to good lubrication design!

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Oil Viscosity at Different Temperatures

The following table illustrates the **viscosity–temperature relationship** for a typical engine oil, a key KTU analytical concept:

| Temperature (°C) | Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s) | Use Case / Engine State |
|---|---|---|
| $-25$ | 3.5 | Cold winter start, must still flow (0W-30) |
| $0$ | 0.85 | Cold idle in winter |
| $40$ | 0.20 | Cold morning normal running |
| **80 – 100** | **0.025 – 0.040** | **Normal operating range (optimum)** |
| $120$ | 0.018 | Hot running, turbocharged |
| $150$ | 0.012 | Severe overheating — danger zone |

> [!WARNING]
> Above $130^\circ\text{C}$ most mineral oils lose their hydrodynamic film strength and **boundary lubrication / mixed lubrication** kicks in, accelerating wear. This is why oil coolers are mandatory in turbocharged engines.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Wet-Sump vs. Dry-Sump (KTU Comparison Question)

| Parameter | Wet Sump | Dry Sump |
|---|---|---|
| Oil storage | In the oil pan (inside engine) | External tank |
| Pump type | Single pressure pump | Pressure + Scavenge pump (2 pumps) |
| Oil capacity | Small (3–6 L) | Larger (6–15 L) |
| Oil cooling | Limited | Better (tank acts as heat exchanger) |
| Oil surge under hard cornering | Possible (oil sloshes away from pickup → starvation) | No surge (always submerged in external tank) |
| Centre of gravity | Higher | Lower (sump can be shallow) |
| Application | Family cars, commuters | Racing cars, off-roaders, performance cars |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Example | Maruti Alto K10, Honda City | Chevrolet Corvette, Porsche 911 GT3, Formula 1 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Basic Lubrication Circuit

The diagram below models the oil flow path of a typical **Wet-Sump Splash + Pressure lubrication system** used in modern multi-cylinder petrol engines.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OIL SUMP RESERVOIR] --> B[OIL STRAINER MESH]
    B --> C[OIL PUMP GEAR TYPE]
    C --> D{PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE}
    D -->|Normal flow| E[FULL FLOW OIL FILTER]
    D -->|Over pressure bypass| A
    E --> F[MAIN OIL GALLERY IN BLOCK]
    F --> G1[MAIN BEARINGS]
    F --> G2[CRANKSHAFT JOURNALS]
    F --> G3[BIG END CONNECTING ROD]
    F --> G4[CAMSHAFT BEARINGS]
    F --> G5[ROCKER ARM SHAFT]
    F --> G6[TIMING CHAIN TENSIONER]
    G1 --> H[DRAIN BACK TO SUMP]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    G6 --> H
    F -.Optional.-> I[OIL COOLER HEAT EXCHANGER]
    I --> F
    H --> A
    A --> A1[SPLASH FROM BIG END TO CYLINDER WALL]
    A1 --> A2[PISTON RING LUBRICATION]
    A2 --> H
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU Examiner expects this vocabulary):**
- The *solid arrows* indicate the **pressurised supply path** (pressure-fed).
- The *dotted arrow* indicates the **optional oil-cooler loop**.
- The bypass path back to the sump from the pressure relief valve is the **fail-safe over-pressure protection**.

---

### 4.2 Functional Comparison Flow — Monograde vs. Multigrade Oil Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COLD["COLD START -20 C"]
        M1[MONOGRADE SAE 50] --> N1[Thick, slow flow, poor lubrication]
        M2[MULTIGRADE 5W-30] --> N2[Thin, quick flow, full protection]
    end
    subgraph HOT["HOT RUNNING 100 C"]
        H1[MONOGRADE SAE 50] --> O1[Stable film, OK]
        H2[MULTIGRADE 5W-30] --> O2[Thicker due to VII, also OK]
    end
    N1 --> X1[HIGH START WEAR]
    N2 --> X2[LOW START WEAR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above schematic is the **single most common figure** in KTU Board question papers on this topic. Practice redrawing it freehand in the exam hall.

---

### 4.3 Component-Level Block Diagram — Oil Filter (Full-Flow Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pressurised oil from pump] --> IN[Inlet port]
    IN --> BP{Bypass valve spring}
    BP -->|Normal flow| FE[PLEATED PAPER FILTER ELEMENT]
    FE --> OUT[Outlet to main gallery]
    BP -->|Blocked filter cold start| OUT
    FE --> AD[Bowl and drain plug]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, CO2, L1-Remember]**
> *Define the term "Viscosity" and state its SI and CGS units.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Viscosity is the property of a fluid that resists its flow. Quantitatively, it is the ratio of shear stress to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient), as per Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$.
- **SI Unit (1 Mark):** **Pa·s (Pascal-second)** or **N·s/m²**.
- **CGS Unit (1 Mark):** **Poise (P)**, where $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$; **centipoise (cP) = 0.001 Pa·s**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, CO2, L2-Understand]**
> *Distinguish between monograde and multigrade lubricating oils with one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Monograde Oil | Multigrade Oil |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity classification | Single SAE grade (e.g., SAE 30) | Dual SAE grade (e.g., 20W-40) |
| Temperature performance | Suitable for narrow range | Wide temperature range |
| Cold-start protection | Poor in winter | Excellent |
| Additive used | None for VI | Viscosity Index Improver (VII) polymers |
| Example | SAE 30 | **SAE 20W-50**, **5W-30** |

(1 Mark for definition of each, 1 Mark for example) ✔

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks, KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, CO2, L3-Apply]**

> **Question A:** With a neat sketch, describe the **basic lubrication circuit** of a multi-cylinder passenger car engine. Explain the function of each major component. Also state the **typical operating oil pressure** range and the **sump temperature** range.

**OR**

> **Question B:** (See below)

---

**Model Solution — Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) Sketch and Component Explanation — 7 Marks**

**Sketch (3 Marks):** Draw the block-flow diagram as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. *Label clearly:* Sump → Strainer → Pump → Relief valve → Filter → Main gallery → Bearings → Drain → Sump.

**Component Functions (4 Marks — 1 Mark each for any four):**
1. **Oil Sump (Wet Sump):** Storage reservoir. Typically holds 3 – 6 L. Acts as a heat sink.
2. **Oil Pump (Gear type):** Positive displacement pump driven by the camshaft. Delivers oil at 1.5 – 4 bar.
3. **Pressure Relief Valve:** Spring-loaded valve that opens at ~3.5 – 5 bar, returning excess oil to the sump.
4. **Full-Flow Oil Filter:** Pleated paper element filters contaminants down to 25 µm. Has a by-pass valve for cold start.
5. **Main Oil Gallery + Branch Drills:** Distribute oil to the crankshaft main bearings, big-end bearings, camshaft bearings, and rocker arms.
6. **Drain-Back Passages:** Return oil by gravity to the sump.

**(b) Operating Parameters and System Types — 7 Marks**

- **Normal Oil Pressure:** **1.5 to 4.0 bar** at idle to cruising speeds (1 Mark).
- **Maximum Permissible Oil Pressure:** ~5 bar (above which relief valve opens) (1 Mark).
- **Sump Oil Temperature:** **80°C to 110°C** (optimum) (1 Mark).
- **Why oil pressure varies with RPM:** Pump delivery $Q$ ∝ $N$, so pressure rises with speed (1 Mark).
- **System types — Wet sump vs Dry sump (3 Marks):** Wet sump uses the engine oil pan; dry sump uses an external tank with separate scavenge pump. Wet sump is used in normal cars; dry sump in racing cars (e.g., Porsche 911 GT3, Formula 1).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** *Many students forget to draw the **return path** (drain-back) and the **bypass** from the relief valve. Always include arrows back to the sump. Marks are lost if the diagram is unidirectional only.*

---

#### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks, KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, CO2, L4-Analyse]**

> **Question B:** Define **Viscosity Index (VI)**. Two reference oils L and H have viscosities at 40°C as 73 cSt and 25 cSt respectively, and at 100°C as 9.3 cSt and 5.0 cSt. An unknown oil 'U' has the same viscosity as 'L' at 100°C (i.e., 9.3 cSt) but its viscosity at 40°C is 60 cSt. Calculate the **Viscosity Index of the unknown oil U**, and comment on whether it is suitable for use in a tropical climate like Kerala.

**Model Solution — Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a) Definition of Viscosity Index — 3 Marks**

- **Definition (2 Marks):** VI is an empirical, dimensionless number (developed by Dean & Davis, 1929) that indicates the *rate of change* of oil viscosity with temperature. A higher VI means the oil's viscosity changes less with temperature.
- **Standard Reference (1 Mark):** Pennsylvanian oil (VI = 100) and Gulf Coast oil (VI = 0) are the original reference oils. Modern oils use kinematic viscosity at 40°C and 100°C.

**(b) Viscosity Index Calculation — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the three viscosity values needed for the formula (1 Mark):**
- $L = 73$ cSt (viscosity of low-VI reference at 40°C)
- $H = 25$ cSt (viscosity of high-VI reference at 40°C)
- $U = 60$ cSt (viscosity of unknown oil at 40°C)

**Step 2 — Apply the VI formula (2 Marks):**
$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (2 Marks):**
$$\text{VI} = \frac{73 - 60}{73 - 25} \times 100 = \frac{13}{48} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate (2 Marks):**
$$\boxed{\,\text{VI} = 27.08 \approx 27\,}$$

**(c) Suitability Discussion — 4 Marks**

- A VI of 27 is **very low**; the oil's viscosity will drop sharply as temperature rises (1 Mark).
- Kerala's ambient temperatures of 30–38°C combined with engine running at 90–100°C will cause excessive viscosity thinning (1 Mark).
- Result: **Poor film strength at high temperatures, leading to increased wear and oil consumption** (1 Mark).
- **Conclusion:** This oil is **NOT suitable** for Kerala's tropical climate. A multigrade **SAE 20W-50** with VI > 130 (e.g., synthetic blends) is recommended (1 Mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** *Students often confuse the positions of $L$, $H$, and $U$ in the formula. Remember: $L$ is the **Low-VI** oil (which has higher 40°C viscosity for the same 100°C viscosity), and $H$ is the **High-VI** oil (lower 40°C viscosity). The unknown $U$ always lies between $L$ and $H$. Plugging them in the wrong order gives negative or > 100 VI — a guaranteed zero.*

---

### 5.3 KTU 2024 Cognitive Level Mapping

| Question Part | Marks | Bloom's Level | KTU Course Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 (Viscosity definition) | 3 | L1 – Remember | CO2 |
| Q2 (Monograde vs Multigrade) | 3 | L2 – Understand | CO2 |
| Q-A (Lubrication circuit sketch) | 14 | L3 – Apply | CO2 |
| Q-B (Viscosity Index calculation) | 14 | L4 – Analyse | CO2 |

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet Gold for KTU Exam Hall:**

- ⬛ **Basic Circuit Path:** Sump → Strainer → Pump → Relief Valve → Filter → Main Gallery → Bearings → Drain → Sump. (Memorise in this order!)

- ⬛ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — must write this with units in derivations.

- ⬛ **SI vs CGS Units:** $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$; $1 \text{ Stoke} = 1 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$.

- ⬛ **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ — convert Poise to Stokes by dividing by density in g/cm³.

- ⬛ **Viscous Torque Formula:** $T_f = \dfrac{\mu \pi^2 D^3 L N}{120 \, c}$ — *most asked derivation in KTU Board Exam 2023–24*.

- ⬛ **VI Formula:** $\text{VI} = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ — typical 7-mark analytical question.

- ⬛ **SAE "W" = Winter** — single number with W means cold-grade; dual grade (e.g., 20W-50) = multigrade.

- ⬛ **Normal Oil Pressure:** 1.5 – 4 bar; **Relief Valve Setting:** ~3.5 – 5 bar.

- ⬛ **Wet Sump** (most cars) vs **Dry Sump** (racing) — must know the difference in applications.

- ⬛ **Splash Lubrication** is used for cylinder walls; **Pressure Lubrication** for bearings.

- ⬛ **Multigrade oils** use **Viscosity Index Improvers (VII)** — polymeric additives.

- ⬛ **API Service Class:** "**S**" for petrol (Spark), "**C**" for diesel (Compression) — "**SC, SD, SE, SF, SG, SJ, SL, SM, SN, SP**" increasing performance.

- ⬛ **Oil Pump Types** (KTU expects 2): **Gear pump** (external/internal), **Rotor pump**, **Vane pump** — all are positive displacement.

- ⬛ **Filter Bypass Valve** opens at ~0.7 bar pressure drop across the filter element.

- ⬛ **Always include units** in the final answer of a numerical — examiners in Kerala are strict about this.

- ⬛ **Draw the diagram first** in 14-mark questions — it carries 3–4 marks independent of the explanation.

- ⬛ **Hot Kerala climate** → recommend **SAE 20W-50** or **15W-40** mineral/semi-synthetic, **API SN/SP** rated.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module-2 Topic Notes — Lubrication System: Basic Circuitry, Oil Grade and Viscosity.** *Best of luck for your KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations! Prepare the diagram and the VI formula — they are 2024's most likely questions.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling System - Basic Circuit Including Thermostat Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cooling System - Basic Circuit Including Thermostat Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Cooling System - Basic Circuit & Thermostat Valve

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engine Cooling System:** An arrangement of components designed to maintain the engine at its most efficient operating temperature (typically **80°C – 90°C**) by removing surplus heat from the combustion chamber walls, cylinder head, and cylinder block, and rejecting it to the surrounding atmosphere.

In a spark-ignition (SI) or compression-ignition (CI) engine, only about **30% – 35%** of the heat energy released from fuel combustion is converted into useful mechanical work at the crankshaft. The remaining energy is split between exhaust gases and the engine structure. Without a cooling arrangement, metal components would overheat, leading to **lubricant breakdown**, **thermal distortion**, **loss of volumetric efficiency**, and eventual seizure.

### 1.2 Two Broad Classifications of Cooling

| Cooling Method | Heat Transfer Medium | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Cooling** | Atmospheric air (forced or natural) | Two-wheelers (motorcycles, scooters), small generators, lawn mowers |
| **Water (Liquid) Cooling** | Water + Ethylene Glycol mixture (coolant) | Passenger cars, commercial vehicles, tractors, heavy-duty trucks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The current module emphasizes **Water/Liquid Cooling Systems**, especially the **basic circuit architecture** and the **role of the thermostat valve** in regulating coolant flow.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy - The Human Body

Think of your body during heavy exercise. Your heart pumps warm blood, your capillaries release heat through the skin, and you begin to **sweat** so evaporation can dissipate excess thermal energy. If this natural thermostat fails (fever), your body overheats and organs fail.

An automobile engine behaves identically:
- The **water pump** acts as the "heart," circulating heated coolant.
- The **radiator** behaves like "skin," exchanging heat with air.
- The **thermostat valve** is the "brain's hypothalamus," deciding whether heat should be released quickly or conserved for warm-up.
- The **coolant fan** simulates "sweating," providing forced convection when natural airflow is insufficient (idling, low speeds).

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Normal Coolant Operating Temperature:** **80°C – 90°C**
- **Coolant Boiling Point (with anti-freeze):** **105°C – 110°C** (Pure water boils at 100°C; the 50:50 glycol-water mix raises this)
- **Heat Rejected to Cooling System:** Approximately **30% – 35%** of total fuel energy
- **Thermostat Opening Temperature:** Typically **80°C – 85°C** (Wax-pellet type)
- **Specific Heat of Water:** **4.18 kJ/kg·K** (Highest among common liquids - this is why water is chosen)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Purpose of the Cooling System

The cooling system is **NOT** intended to make the engine "cold." Its purpose is **thermal regulation** - keeping the engine at the **optimum temperature window** for:

1. **Maximum Thermal Efficiency** (closer to stoichiometric combustion conditions)
2. **Proper Lubrication** (oil film viscosity is temperature dependent)
3. **Minimum Piston-Cylinder Bore Wear** (thermal expansion is controlled)
4. **Lower Exhaust Emissions** (catalytic converters work optimally at elevated temperatures)
5. **Prevention of Knocking in SI Engines** (coolant temperature influences mixture density)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counterintuitive Fact:** An engine that runs **too cool** is just as harmful as one that runs too hot. Cold operation causes poor fuel atomization, incomplete combustion, increased viscosity drag, and accelerated engine wear due to fuel washing the cylinder walls.

### 2.2 Components of a Liquid Cooling System

The basic circuit consists of:

- **Water Pump** - Centrifugal type, belt-driven from the crankshaft pulley
- **Radiator** - Heat exchanger with fins and tubes
- **Thermostat Valve** - Temperature-controlled flow regulator
- **Coolant Fan** - Electric or belt-driven, draws air through the radiator
- **Coolant Passages (Water Jackets)** - Hollow cavities cast into the engine block and cylinder head
- **Hoses & Piping** - Upper and lower radiator hoses, bypass hose, heater hose
- **Expansion/Reserve Tank** - Accommodates thermal expansion of coolant
- **Pressure Cap** - Raises coolant boiling point (typically **0.9 – 1.05 bar** above atmospheric)

### 2.3 The Basic Circuit - Operational Logic

The cooling circuit has **two operating modes** based on thermostat position:

#### Mode 1: Cold Engine (Thermostat CLOSED)
- Coolant circulates only through the **bypass circuit** (engine block → bypass hose → water pump inlet).
- **No coolant flows to the radiator** - the engine warms up rapidly.
- The fan may or may not operate.

#### Mode 2: Hot Engine (Thermostat OPEN)
- Thermostat opens at the rated temperature.
- Coolant now flows through the **main circuit**: engine block → thermostat → upper hose → radiator → lower hose → water pump → engine block.
- Heat is rejected to the atmosphere through the radiator.
- The fan activates to assist airflow at low vehicle speeds.

### 2.4 The Thermostat Valve - Working Principle

The most common type is the **Wax-Pellet Thermostat** (used in 90%+ of modern vehicles):

1. A copper cup contains a special wax compound (paraffin-based).
2. The wax has a high coefficient of thermal expansion.
3. As coolant temperature rises, the wax melts and expands significantly.
4. The expansion pushes a piston linked to a valve disc.
5. The valve disc lifts off its seat, allowing coolant to flow to the radiator.
6. A **small bleed hole or jiggle pin** prevents air locking and allows minor circulation even when closed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Types:** Bellows-type (older technology, uses volatile liquid like ether) and Solid-state electronic thermostats (used in modern luxury cars with ECU control). However, the **wax-pellet type** dominates KTU exam questions.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the most exam-relevant equations. All values are in **SI units** unless stated otherwise.

| # | Quantity / Equation | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat generated in engine (from fuel) | $Q_{gen} = m_f \times CV \times \eta_{cooling}$ | $m_f$ = fuel mass rate (kg/s), $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg), $\eta_{cooling}$ = fraction rejected to cooling |
| 2 | Heat absorbed by coolant | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m}_c \times c_p \times (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | $\dot{m}_c$ = coolant mass flow rate (kg/s), $c_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K), $T$ = temperature (°C or K) |
| 3 | Heat rejected by radiator | $Q_{rad} = U \times A \times \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A$ = radiator surface area (m²), $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = log mean temperature difference (K) |
| 4 | Air mass flow through radiator | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \times A_{face} \times v_a$ | $\rho_a$ = air density ($\approx 1.2$ kg/m³), $A_{face}$ = frontal area (m²), $v_a$ = air velocity (m/s) |
| 5 | Coolant mass flow required | $\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{Q_{rad}}{c_p \times \Delta T_{cool}}$ | $Q_{rad}$ in kW, $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K for water, $\Delta T_{cool} = 5°C - 10°C$ |
| 6 | Power absorbed by water pump | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c \times \Delta P}{\rho_c \times \eta_{pump}}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure rise (Pa), $\rho_c$ = coolant density, $\eta_{pump}$ = pump efficiency |
| 7 | Pressure cap effect on boiling point | $T_{boil} \uparrow$ with pressure (Clausius-Clapeyron relation) | Typically $T_{boil} \approx 110°C$ at **1 bar gauge** |

### 2.6 Heat Balance in an Engine

$$Q_{fuel} = Q_{brake} + Q_{cooling} + Q_{exhaust} + Q_{unaccounted}$$

Where:
- $Q_{fuel}$ = Total heat from combustion
- $Q_{brake}$ = Useful work output (brake power equivalent)
- $Q_{cooling}$ = Heat carried away by cooling system
- $Q_{exhaust}$ = Heat carried away by exhaust gases
- $Q_{unaccounted}$ = Radiation, blowby, unburnt fuel losses (typically 5% – 10%)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** In KTU numerical problems, students often confuse $c_p$ of water (**4.18 kJ/kg·K**) with $c_p$ of air (**1.005 kJ/kg·K**). Read the problem statement carefully - air cooling and water cooling calculations are completely different scales.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Heavy-Duty Diesel Trucks:** Use dual thermostats (parallel) for staged opening and better temperature control.
2. **High-Performance Sports Cars:** Use cross-flow radiators with auxiliary oil coolers.
3. **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Require cooling for battery packs and power electronics - principles identical to engine cooling.
4. **Marine Engines:** Use heat exchangers with raw seawater as the ultimate sink.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coolant Mass Flow Rate Required by the Radiator

This is the **most frequently asked KTU numerical problem** in Module 2. We derive it rigorously.

**Given Data (Typical Problem Statement):**
- Engine Brake Power, $P_b = 50 \text{ kW}$
- Fraction of fuel energy to cooling, $f_{cool} = 0.30$ (i.e., 30%)
- Calorific value of fuel, $CV = 42,000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Specific heat of water, $c_p = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- Coolant temperature rise in engine, $\Delta T_c = 10°C$ (e.g., from 85°C to 95°C)
- Density of air, $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m³}$
- Air velocity through radiator, $v_a = 15 \text{ m/s}$
- Frontal area of radiator, $A_{face} = 0.30 \text{ m²}$

**Step 1: Calculate the heat to be removed by the cooling system.**

$$P_{cool} = f_{cool} \times P_b = 0.30 \times 50 = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Apply the energy balance - heat gained by coolant equals heat from engine.**

$$Q_{cool} = \dot{m}_c \times c_p \times \Delta T_c$$

Rearranging for coolant mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{Q_{cool}}{c_p \times \Delta T_c}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{15 \text{ kW}}{4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times 10 \text{ K}}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{15}{41.8}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_c = 0.3589 \text{ kg/s} \approx 0.36 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Step 4: Convert to volumetric flow rate (litres per minute).**

$$\dot{V}_c = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{0.3589}{1000} = 3.589 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m³/s}$$

$$\dot{V}_c = 3.589 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 \text{ L/min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_c \approx 21.53 \text{ L/min}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Verification Using Air-Side Heat Balance

**Step 5: Air mass flow rate through the radiator.**

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \times A_{face} \times v_a = 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 15$$

$$\dot{m}_a = 5.4 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6: Temperature rise of air as it passes through the radiator.**

Using energy balance: Heat lost by coolant = Heat gained by air

$$\dot{m}_c \times c_{p,c} \times \Delta T_c = \dot{m}_a \times c_{p,a} \times \Delta T_a$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta T_a = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c \times c_{p,c} \times \Delta T_c}{\dot{m}_a \times c_{p,a}}$$

$$\Delta T_a = \dfrac{0.3589 \times 4.18 \times 10}{5.4 \times 1.005}$$

$$\Delta T_a = \dfrac{15.0}{5.427}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_a \approx 2.76°C}$$

This small air-side temperature rise is **realistic** - the radiator is designed for high air mass flow and low temperature differential. If $\Delta T_a$ came out very large (>30°C), it would indicate **insufficient radiator capacity** or **blockage**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Radiator Heat Transfer Area (LMTD Method)

**Step 7: Calculate the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD).**

Assume:
- Coolant inlet to radiator: $T_{c,in} = 95°C$
- Coolant outlet from radiator: $T_{c,out} = 85°C$
- Air inlet to radiator: $T_{a,in} = 35°C$ (ambient)
- Air outlet from radiator: $T_{a,out} = 35 + 2.76 = 37.76°C$

**Counter-flow arrangement analysis:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{c,in} - T_{a,out} = 95 - 37.76 = 57.24°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{c,out} - T_{a,in} = 85 - 35 = 50°C$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{57.24 - 50}{\ln(57.24 / 50)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{7.24}{\ln(1.1448)} = \dfrac{7.24}{0.1354}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LMTD} \approx 53.47°C}$$

**Step 8: Calculate required heat transfer area.**

Assume overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 150 \text{ W/m²·K}$ (typical for automotive radiator).

$$A = \dfrac{Q_{rad}}{U \times \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{15,000 \text{ W}}{150 \times 53.47}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 1.87 \text{ m²}}$$

This is a realistic frontal area expansion. A radiator with a frontal area of 0.30 m² typically has a total heat transfer area (including fins) of 1.5 – 2.5 m² due to the fin enhancement factor.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix - Problem-Solving Flowchart

| Step | Question to Ask | Equation / Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | What is being cooled? | Identify engine type and cooling medium |
| 2 | What is the heat load? | $Q = f_{cool} \times P_b$ or $Q = \dot{m}_f \times CV \times f_{cool}$ |
| 3 | What is the coolant flow rate? | $\dot{m}_c = Q / (c_p \times \Delta T_c)$ |
| 4 | Is the air side sufficient? | $\Delta T_a = \dot{m}_c c_p \Delta T_c / (\dot{m}_a c_{p,a})$ |
| 5 | What is the radiator area? | $A = Q / (U \times \Delta T_{LMTD})$ |
| 6 | What is the pump power? | $P_{pump} = \dot{V}_c \times \Delta P / \eta_{pump}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Liquid Cooling Circuit

The following flowchart illustrates the **basic cooling circuit with thermostat valve** in both operating modes (cold start and normal operation).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Block Water Jacket] --> B[Coolant Temperature Sensor]
    B --> C{Thermostat Valve Position}
    C -->|Below 80 deg C - CLOSED| D[Bypass Hose / Small Circuit]
    C -->|Above 85 deg C - OPEN| E[Upper Radiator Hose]
    D --> F[Water Pump Inlet]
    E --> G[Radiator Top Tank]
    G --> H[Radiator Core - Tubes and Fins]
    H --> I[Radiator Bottom Tank]
    I --> J[Lower Radiator Hose]
    J --> F
    F --> K[Centrifugal Water Pump]
    K --> L[Engine Block - Re circulation]
    H -.->|Forced Air Draft| M[Coolant Fan - Electric or Belt Driven]
    M -.->|Hot Air Discharge| N[Atmosphere]
    O[Reserve / Expansion Tank] -.->|Make up coolant| G
    O -.->|Steam and overflow return| P[Pressure Cap 0.9 bar]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of the Thermostat Valve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cold Engine Coolant Input] --> B[Thermostat Housing]
    B --> C[Wax Pellet Element - Copper Cup]
    C -->|Heat Absorbed| D[Wax Melts and Expands]
    D --> E[Piston Movement]
    E --> F[Valve Disc Lifts Off Seat]
    F --> G[Main Valve Opens to Radiator]
    B --> H[Jiggle Pin Bleed Hole]
    H -->|Small flow always| I[Prevents Air Lock]
    G --> J[Hot Coolant Discharged to Upper Hose]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Heat Flow in a Water-Cooled Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Combustion Chamber - Hot Gases 2000 deg C] -->|Conduction| S2[Metal Walls - Cylinder Block and Head]
    S2 -->|Convection| S3[Coolant in Water Jacket - 90 deg C]
    S3 -->|Pumped via Water Pump| S4[Radiator Tubes]
    S4 -->|Conduction through tube wall| S5[Radiator Fins - Aluminium]
    S5 -->|Convection to Air| S6[Atmospheric Air - 35 deg C]
    S7[Coolant Fan] -->|Forced Convection| S6
    S3 -.->|Loss if thermostat fails| S8[Engine Overheats - Boiling]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Heat follows a serial path - hot combustion gases $\rightarrow$ metal walls $\rightarrow$ coolant $\rightarrow$ radiator tubes $\rightarrow$ fins $\rightarrow$ atmospheric air. Each interface contributes a thermal resistance. The overall radiator effectiveness depends on minimizing these resistances.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List any six major components of a water-cooling system used in an automobile engine.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Water Pump** (centrifugal type, belt driven)
2. **Radiator** (with core, top tank, bottom tank, and pressure cap)
3. **Thermostat Valve** (wax pellet type, mounted on engine block)
4. **Coolant Fan** (electric or mechanical, behind radiator)
5. **Water Jackets** (casting cavities in block and head)
6. **Hoses and Piping** (upper, lower, bypass, and heater hoses)
7. **Reserve / Expansion Tank** (overflow reservoir)

*(Any six well-explained points fetch full 3 marks. KTU typically awards 0.5 marks per correct point.)*

---

#### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Why is the thermostat valve considered the "heart" of the cooling system temperature regulation? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

The thermostat valve regulates coolant flow between the bypass circuit and the radiator circuit based on engine temperature. It contains a **wax pellet element** that expands on heating, opening a valve at a calibrated temperature (typically **80°C – 85°C**). Below this temperature, it remains closed, forcing coolant through the bypass circuit to allow rapid engine warm-up. Above this temperature, it opens, allowing coolant flow to the radiator for heat rejection. This automatic two-mode operation ensures the engine reaches its optimal operating temperature quickly and maintains it precisely, improving fuel efficiency, reducing emissions, and minimizing engine wear.

---

### **PART B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **wax-pellet type thermostat valve** used in an automobile engine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A four-cylinder four-stroke petrol engine develops a brake power of **60 kW** at **3000 rpm**. The calorific value of the fuel is **44,000 kJ/kg** and the brake specific fuel consumption is **0.30 kg/kWh**. If **30%** of the heat input is rejected to the cooling system, calculate:
1. The mass of fuel supplied per second
2. The heat rejected to the cooling system per second
3. The coolant flow rate required if the permissible temperature rise of coolant is **10°C**
(Take specific heat of water = **4.18 kJ/kg·K**) **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Wax-Pellet Thermostat Valve:**

**Construction:**
A wax-pellet thermostat consists of a copper cup filled with a special paraffin wax compound, sealed with a rubber diaphragm. A push rod connects the diaphragm to a valve disc, which is held closed by a spring. The assembly is housed in a steel casing installed between the engine outlet and the upper radiator hose.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Cold Engine Condition (Below 80°C):** The wax is in solid state. The spring keeps the valve disc firmly on its seat. Coolant cannot flow to the radiator. It circulates only through the bypass circuit, allowing the engine to warm up rapidly.

2. **Hot Engine Condition (Above 85°C):** The coolant temperature heats the wax. The wax melts and expands (wax has a high coefficient of thermal expansion - approximately **10% volume change**). The expansion pushes the rubber diaphragm, which moves the push rod, lifting the valve disc off its seat. Coolant now flows to the radiator.

3. **A small jiggle pin** (a loose pin in the valve) allows minor circulation even when closed, preventing air locks.

*(Award 2 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for construction, 3 marks for working principle.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Mass of fuel supplied per second.**

Brake power $P_b = 60 \text{ kW}$

Brake specific fuel consumption $bsfc = 0.30 \text{ kg/kWh}$

$$\dot{m}_f = bsfc \times P_b = 0.30 \times 60 = 18 \text{ kg/h}$$

Converting to kg/s:

$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{18}{3600} = 0.005 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_f = 0.005 \text{ kg/s} = 18 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 2: Heat rejected to the cooling system per second.**

$$Q_{cool} = \dot{m}_f \times CV \times f_{cool}$$

$$Q_{cool} = 0.005 \times 44,000 \times 0.30$$

$$Q_{cool} = 66 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{cool} = 66 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 3: Coolant flow rate required.**

Using energy balance:

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{Q_{cool}}{c_p \times \Delta T_c}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{66}{4.18 \times 10}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{66}{41.8}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = 1.579 \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to L/min:

$$\dot{V}_c = \dfrac{1.579}{1000} \times 60 \times 1000 = 94.74 \text{ L/min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_c = 1.579 \text{ kg/s} \approx 94.74 \text{ L/min}}$$

*(Full 7 marks: 2 marks for part 1, 2 marks for part 2, 3 marks for part 3 with proper units and conversion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **basic liquid cooling circuit** of a multi-cylinder engine with the help of a block diagram. Discuss the difference between the **bypass circuit** and the **main radiator circuit**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A water-cooled engine has a heat dissipation rate of **20 kW** through the radiator. The coolant enters the radiator at **95°C** and leaves at **80°C**. The atmospheric air enters the radiator at **30°C** and leaves at **42°C**. Calculate the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** and the **required heat transfer area** of the radiator.

Take overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 175 \text{ W/m²·K}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Basic Liquid Cooling Circuit:**

The basic liquid cooling system of a multi-cylinder engine consists of the following major flow paths:

**Block Diagram Description:**

The water pump (driven by the crankshaft via V-belt) draws coolant from the radiator bottom tank through the lower hose and pumps it into the engine block water jackets. The coolant absorbs heat from the cylinder walls and combustion chamber. From the cylinder head, the hot coolant exits and reaches the thermostat housing.

**Two Circuit Paths:**

| Aspect | Bypass Circuit (Small Loop) | Main Circuit (Large Loop) |
|---|---|---|
| **Active When** | Thermostat is CLOSED (cold engine) | Thermostat is OPEN (hot engine) |
| **Path** | Engine head → bypass hose → water pump inlet | Engine head → thermostat → upper hose → radiator → lower hose → pump |
| **Function** | Allows rapid engine warm-up | Rejects heat to atmosphere |
| **Temperature Range** | Below 80°C | Above 85°C |
| **Heat Rejection** | None (no radiator flow) | Active heat dissipation |

The thermostat thus acts as an **automatic diverter valve**, routing coolant based on temperature.

*(Award 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for path explanation, 2 marks for difference table.)*

---

**(b) LMTD and Radiator Area Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_{c,in} = 95°C$ (coolant inlet)
- $T_{c,out} = 80°C$ (coolant outlet)
- $T_{a,in} = 30°C$ (air inlet)
- $T_{a,out} = 42°C$ (air outlet)
- $Q = 20 \text{ kW} = 20,000 \text{ W}$
- $U = 175 \text{ W/m²·K}$

**Step 1: Calculate temperature differences at both ends (assuming counter-flow).**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{c,in} - T_{a,out} = 95 - 42 = 53°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{c,out} - T_{a,in} = 80 - 30 = 50°C$$

**Step 2: Apply LMTD formula.**

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{53 - 50}{\ln(53 / 50)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{3}{\ln(1.06)} = \dfrac{3}{0.0583}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LMTD} \approx 51.49°C}$$

**Step 3: Calculate required heat transfer area.**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \times \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{20,000}{175 \times 51.49}$$

$$A = \dfrac{20,000}{9010.75}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 2.22 \text{ m²}}$$

*(Full 7 marks: 2 marks for LMTD calculation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for area, 1 mark for units.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks in Cooling System Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** $bsfc$ is often given in kg/kWh, but the answer must be in kg/s. Forgetting the $\div 3600$ step costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing $c_p$ values:** Water is **4.18 kJ/kg·K**, air is **1.005 kJ/kg·K**. Mixing them up produces drastically wrong answers.
> 3. **LMTD Numerator Sign:** The formula is $\dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$. Students often write the denominator in the numerator. Markers specifically look for the correct placement.
> 4. **Missing the Thermostat Function:** In descriptive answers, students describe the **components** but forget to explain **why** the thermostat is needed (rapid warm-up + temperature stabilization). KTU examiners specifically test this conceptual clarity.
> 5. **Forgetting the Jiggle Pin:** Many students miss describing this small but functionally critical part of the thermostat in 7-mark questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist - Cooling System Module 2:**

- [x] **Function:** Maintain engine at **80°C – 90°C** (NOT to make it cold).
- [x] **Heat Distribution:** ~30% useful work, ~30% cooling, ~35% exhaust, ~5% unaccounted.
- [x] **Two Circuits:** Bypass (small, no radiator) + Main (large, with radiator).
- [x] **Thermostat:** Wax pellet, opens at **80°C – 85°C**, has jiggle pin for air-bleed.
- [x] **Water Pump:** Centrifugal, belt-driven, mechanical (not electrical in most cars).
- [x] **Radiator:** Cross-flow or down-flow, with pressure cap (**0.9 – 1.05 bar gauge**).
- [x] **Coolant:** Water + Ethylene Glycol (50:50 mix); raises boiling point to **~110°C** and lowers freezing point to **~-35°C**.
- [x] **Fan:** Electric (modern, ECU-controlled) or mechanical (older, viscous clutch).
- [x] **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $Q_{cool} = \dot{m}_c \times c_p \times \Delta T_c$
  - $\dot{m}_c = Q_{cool} / (c_p \times \Delta T_c)$
  - $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$
  - $A = Q / (U \times \Delta T_{LMTD})$
- [x] **Exam Tip:** Always convert **bsfc** from kg/kWh to kg/s by dividing by **3600** before calculation.
- [x] **Real-World Tip:** Modern cars may use **map-controlled electronic thermostats** (BMW, Mercedes) for precise temperature management, but KTU questions focus on **conventional wax-pellet type**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes - Cooling System & Thermostat Valve**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exhaust System - 3-Way Catalytic Converter, DPF and SCR Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exhaust System - 3-Way Catalytic Converter, DPF and SCR Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Exhaust After-Treatment Systems: Introduction

Modern internal combustion engines produce harmful exhaust emissions that must be treated before being released to the atmosphere. The **Exhaust After-Treatment System** refers to the combination of devices mounted downstream of the engine exhaust manifold that chemically or physically convert toxic gases (CO, HC, NOx) and particulate matter (PM) into less harmful substances like CO₂, H₂O, and N₂.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly distinguish between **engine-based control** (in-cylinder techniques like EGR, retarded injection) and **after-treatment control** (devices fitted in the exhaust line). The three devices covered here—**TWC, DPF, and SCR**—are the three pillars of Euro-VI/BS-VI emission compliance.

---

### 1.2 Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)

**Formal Definition:**
A Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) is an exhaust after-treatment device used almost exclusively in **spark-ignition (petrol/gasoline) engines** that simultaneously performs three chemical functions—oxidation of carbon monoxide (CO) to CO₂, oxidation of unburnt hydrocarbons (HC) to CO₂ and H₂O, and reduction of nitrogen oxides (NOx) to nitrogen (N₂)—provided the engine is operated near the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (λ = 1 ± 0.01)**.

- **Operating temperature window:** **400 °C – 800 °C** (light-off temperature ≈ 250–300 °C).
- **Catalyst materials:** Platinum (Pt), Palladium (Pd), Rhodium (Rh) coated on a ceramic honeycomb substrate (Cordierite) wash-coated with γ-Al₂O₃.
- **Conversion efficiency:** Up to **98 %** for all three pollutants once fully warmed up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Three-Way"?** Because it tackles three pollutant classes (CO, HC, NOx) in a **single** device—two oxidation reactions and one reduction reaction occurring in parallel.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of the catalytic converter as a **chemical "referee" for the engine's exhaust**. Imagine a busy factory chimney releasing three different harmful smokes—black smoke (HC), grey smoke (CO), and brownish fumes (NOx). The TWC is a smart filter placed at the top of the chimney that converts all three into harmless, invisible gases, **but only if the fuel-air mixture is mixed in exactly the right proportion** (a "perfect recipe"). If the recipe is too rich (excess fuel) or too lean (excess air), the TWC gets confused and cannot perform all three jobs efficiently. The **lambda sensor (O₂ sensor)** is the "taste-tester" placed before the TWC that constantly checks the recipe and signals the ECU to adjust it.

---

### 1.3 Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)

**Formal Definition:**
A Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) is a wall-flow type exhaust filter used in **compression-ignition (diesel) engines** that physically traps and stores **particulate matter (PM) / soot** from the exhaust stream. The collected soot must be periodically burned off in a process called **regeneration** to prevent excessive back-pressure and engine damage.

- **Filtration efficiency:** **> 99 %** for particles of diameter **≥ 100 nm** and **85–95 %** for ultrafine particles (< 100 nm).
- **Substrate material:** **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** or cordierite with alternating plugged channels.
- **Soot holding capacity:** typically **5–10 g/L** of filter volume before regeneration is triggered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a DPF is needed in diesels?** Diesel engines operate with a **lean burn** (excess air) mixture, producing very low CO and HC but very high **particulate matter (PM/soot)** due to heterogeneous combustion and high-pressure fuel injection. Without a DPF, diesels would emit visible black smoke—a problem now regulated under Euro-VI/BS-VI norms.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Picture a **vacuum cleaner bag** placed in the exhaust pipe of a diesel engine. As dirty exhaust passes through, the soot particles get trapped inside the bag's porous walls. The bag can only hold a limited amount of dirt; once it is full, the suction (engine breathing) becomes too difficult. So the engine's electronic brain periodically triggers a **"self-cleaning burn"** (regeneration), heating the bag to **~ 600 °C** so that the trapped soot is incinerated into ash, which is harmlessly blown out. This cleaning cycle happens automatically every few hundred kilometers without the driver noticing.

---

### 1.4 Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)

**Formal Definition:**
Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) is a chemical exhaust after-treatment technology that uses a **nitrogen-based reductant**—typically a **32.5 % aqueous urea solution** marketed as **AdBlue® (ISO 22241)** or **DEF (Diesel Exhaust Fluid)**—injected into the hot exhaust stream. The injected urea decomposes into ammonia (NH₃), which then catalytically reacts with NOx over a **vanadium, tungsten, or zeolite-based catalyst** to produce harmless **nitrogen (N₂) and water (H₂O)**.

- **Operating temperature window:** **200 °C – 500 °C** (best efficiency 300–450 °C).
- **NOx reduction efficiency:** **80 – 95 %**.
- **UREA consumption:** approximately **3 – 5 %** of fuel consumption (by volume).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Selective"?** The catalyst selectively reacts NH₃ with NOx **rather than** with the excess O₂ present in the lean diesel exhaust. This selectivity is what allows the system to operate efficiently in a lean (oxygen-rich) environment.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you are cooking fish in a kitchen, and the smell of ammonia is filling the room. You open a small bottle of a special liquid (AdBlue) and drip it over a heated metal plate. A chemical reaction "scrubs" the bad smell (NOx) by converting it into plain water and harmless nitrogen gas. The "selectivity" simply means the metal plate is designed to react with the bad gas (NOx) **first**, not with the regular air (O₂) already in the kitchen. The drip rate (urea dosing) is automatically controlled based on how strong the bad smell (NOx level) is.

---

### 1.5 Comparative Snapshot at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask "Compare TWC, DPF, SCR" as a 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the matrix in Section 4.

| Feature | 3-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) | Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) | Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Engine type** | Petrol (SI) | Diesel (CI) | Diesel (CI) |
| **Target pollutant** | CO, HC, NOx | Particulate Matter (PM/Soot) | NOx |
| **Working principle** | Catalytic (chemical) | Physical trapping + thermal burn | Chemical reduction (urea → NH₃) |
| **Requires λ = 1?** | Yes (mandatory) | No (works in lean) | No (works in lean) |
| **Consumable** | None | None (self-cleaning) | AdBlue® / DEF (urea) |
| **Efficiency** | ~ 98 % | ~ 99 % (mass) | 80–95 % (NOx) |
| **Reaction type** | Oxidation + Reduction | Filtration + Oxidation (regen.) | Reduction only |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three-Way Catalytic Converter — Operational Logic

The TWC operates on the principle of **simultaneous redox reactions** over precious-metal catalysts. The "three-way" magic works **only** within a narrow air-fuel ratio window called the **lambda window (λ = 1 ± 0.01)**.

#### Step 1 — Oxidation of Carbon Monoxide

Carbon monoxide (a product of incomplete combustion) is oxidized to carbon dioxide when it contacts the platinum/palladium catalyst surface in the presence of excess oxygen.

- **Reaction Equation:** $2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2$
- **Catalyst:** Pt, Pd
- **Exothermic:** Releases **283 kJ/mol** of energy.

#### Step 2 — Oxidation of Unburnt Hydrocarbons

Unburnt gasoline (HC) vapours in the exhaust react with residual oxygen over the catalyst to form CO₂ and water vapour.

- **Reaction Equation:** $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Example (Octane, C₈H₁₈):** $2\,\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 25\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 16\,\text{CO}_2 + 18\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Catalyst:** Pt, Pd

#### Step 3 — Reduction of Nitrogen Oxides

Nitric oxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)—collectively NOx—are reduced back to diatomic nitrogen using CO, H₂, and HC as reducing agents. This is where **rhodium** plays its unique role, as it is the most efficient metal for NOx reduction.

- **Reaction Equation:** $2\,\text{NO} + 2\,\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\,\text{CO}_2$
- **Catalyst:** **Rh (Rhodium)** — exclusively used for NOx reduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Rhodium so critical?** Rh has a unique ability to **dissociate the strong N≡O bond** (~ 631 kJ/mol) at relatively low temperatures, which is the rate-limiting step in NOx reduction. This is why Rh is the most expensive of the three precious metals, often costing **5–10× the price of gold per gram**.

#### Step 4 — The Lambda Window & Closed-Loop Control

The conversion efficiency of the TWC is plotted against the equivalence ratio (λ). The TWC has a sharp "sweet spot" near **λ = 1**.

- **If λ < 1 (Rich mixture):** Excess fuel → low O₂ → CO and HC cannot be fully oxidized → efficiency drops.
- **If λ > 1 (Lean mixture):** Excess O₂ → NOx cannot be reduced because the reducing agents (CO, HC) are already consumed.
- **Lambda window:** **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** (a band of ±1 % around stoichiometry).

The ECU uses a **Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen (HEGO) sensor** placed **upstream** of the TWC, switching rapidly between rich and lean commands to "oscillate" the engine operation around λ = 1.

---

### 2.2 Diesel Particulate Filter — Operational Logic

#### Step 1 — Wall-Flow Filtration Mechanism

The DPF substrate is a honeycomb ceramic with **alternating channels physically plugged** at alternate ends (capped and uncapped channels). Exhaust gas is forced to flow **through the porous channel walls**, leaving soot particles behind.

- **Substrate:** SiC, porosity ~ 40–65 %, mean pore size **~ 10–20 µm**.
- **Filtration mechanism:** **Deep-bed filtration** (soot deposited within wall pores) + **cake filtration** (soot builds up on the wall surface as a porous cake).

#### Step 2 — Pressure Drop Behaviour

As soot accumulates, the back-pressure across the DPF rises. The differential pressure (ΔP) is measured by a **pressure sensor** across the filter.

- **Clean filter:** ΔP ≈ **2–5 kPa** (at typical engine conditions).
- **Loaded filter (regen. trigger threshold):** ΔP ≈ **10–20 kPa**.
- **Excessive loading (engine limp mode):** ΔP > 25 kPa.

The ΔP is used by the ECU to estimate the soot mass and schedule regeneration.

#### Step 3 — Regeneration Strategies

Regeneration is the controlled oxidation of trapped soot to CO₂. Two main strategies exist:

- **Passive Regeneration:** Occurs naturally when exhaust temperature is high (e.g., during sustained high-speed driving, **> 350 °C**). A **Fuel-Borne Catalyst (FBC)** such as cerium (Ce) or iron (Fe) additive lowers soot ignition temperature to ~ 250 °C.
- **Active Regeneration:** ECU-triggered, typically every **400–800 km**. Methods include:
  * **Late post-injection:** injects extra fuel in the expansion stroke → raises exhaust to **> 600 °C**.
  * **HC dosing injector** (CRT® — Continuously Regenerating Trap): injects fuel before the DPF oxidation catalyst.
  * **Electric heater grid** in the inlet of the DPF.

**Soot Oxidation Reaction:**
$$\text{C (soot)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$
$$\Delta H = -393.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ash accumulation** (from engine oil additives like Ca, Mg, Zn) cannot be burned off and accumulates over the DPF's life (~ 200,000 km). This is why DPF service intervals exist.

---

### 2.3 Selective Catalytic Reduction — Operational Logic

#### Step 1 — Urea Dosing and Decomposition

AdBlue® (32.5 wt% urea in demineralized water) is injected into the hot exhaust upstream of the SCR catalyst via a **dosing module** controlled by the SCR ECU.

- **Water evaporation:** $\text{H}_2\text{O(l)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O(g)}$
- **Urea thermolysis:** $\text{(NH}_2)_2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 + \text{HNCO}$ (isocyanic acid)
- **HNCO hydrolysis:** $\text{HNCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$

**Net urea decomposition:**
$$\text{(NH}_2)_2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$$

#### Step 2 — SCR Catalytic Reactions (Standard, Fast, Slow)

Three competing reactions occur on the SCR catalyst. The "**fast SCR**" reaction is the most desirable as it operates at the lowest temperature and gives the highest efficiency.

- **Standard SCR:**
$$4\,\text{NH}_3 + 4\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- **Fast SCR (most efficient):**
$$4\,\text{NH}_3 + 2\,\text{NO} + 2\,\text{NO}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- **Slow SCR (at low temp):**
$$8\,\text{NH}_3 + 6\,\text{NO}_2 \rightarrow 7\,\text{N}_2 + 12\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Fast" SCR?** The 1:1 ratio of NO:NO₂ makes the rate-limiting step faster, lowering the activation energy. Modern SCR systems use a **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** upstream to convert NO into NO₂, ensuring a 1:1 NO:NO₂ ratio for the Fast SCR reaction.

#### Step 3 — Ammonia Slip and Slip Catalyst

If too much NH₃ is dosed, unreacted ammonia ("ammonia slip") exits the tailpipe, causing a pungent smell and secondary pollution. An **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC)** placed after the SCR catalyst oxidizes any excess NH₃ to N₂ and H₂O.

- **Slip reaction:** $4\,\text{NH}_3 + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA REFERENCE FOR KTU BOARD EXAMS.** Memorize this table; numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE are frequently based on stoichiometric ratios of these reactions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Definition of equivalence ratio (lambda) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio equivalence | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{TWC}} = f(\lambda)$ | Conversion efficiency peaks at $\lambda = 1$ | % |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{\text{DPF}} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L \cdot m_{\text{soot}}}{k \cdot A}$ | DPF pressure drop ∝ soot mass (m) | Pa |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{\text{urea}} = 0.466 \times \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \dfrac{\text{NOx}_{\text{in}} - \text{NOx}_{\text{out}}}{\text{NOx}_{\text{in}}}$ | Urea dosing rate (0.466 = mol. wt. ratio $\dfrac{60}{28.8 \times \dots}$) | kg/h |
| 6 | $\text{AdBlue consumption} \approx 0.05 \times \text{Fuel consumption}$ | Rule of thumb: 5 % of fuel | L/100 km |
| 7 | $\text{NO}_x \text{ reduction} = \dfrac{[\text{NO}_x]_{\text{in}} - [\text{NO}_x]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{NO}_x]_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | SCR efficiency formula | % |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{\text{exh, regen}} \geq 600 - T_{\text{ambient}}$ | DPF active regeneration ΔT needed | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical constant **0.466** in the urea dosing formula is a KTU-favourite derived value: it is the mass ratio of urea to NOx based on molar masses (60 g/mol urea / 28 g/mol NO × 14/46 conversion factor for NO₂). Examiners may ask: "Calculate AdBlue consumption for a truck consuming 30 L/100 km."

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** TWC is a **mandatory fitment** on every petrol car sold in India (BS-VI norms since April 2020). DPF + SCR is mandatory on every diesel car/LCV/HCV sold since BS-VI.
- **Marine & Locomotive:** Heavy-duty SCR systems (with AdBlue tanks up to 200 L) are fitted on ships (IMO Tier III) and diesel locomotives to comply with marine/off-road emission norms.
- **Power Generation:** Stationary diesel gensets for hospitals/data centres use SCR + DPF to meet CPCB norms.
- **Industrial Boilers:** Coal/biomass-fired plants use SCR for NOx control (operating at 200–400 °C using low-temperature zeolite catalysts).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Urea Dosing Rate for SCR System

This is a **classic KTU numerical problem**. We derive the precise AdBlue dosing rate required to reduce a known quantity of NOx.

**Given Problem Statement (KTU standard):**
A heavy-duty diesel engine produces exhaust at the rate of **m_exh = 720 kg/h**, containing **NOx = 800 ppm (by mass)**. The SCR system must achieve **80 % NOx reduction efficiency**. Calculate: (a) the AdBlue dosing rate in kg/h, and (b) the AdBlue consumption per 100 km if the engine consumes **30 L of diesel per 100 km** (diesel density ρ = 0.835 kg/L).

---

#### Step 1: Express NOx concentration in kg/h

The total exhaust mass flow is given as **m_exh = 720 kg/h**, with NOx concentration = **800 ppm (mass basis)**.

The mass of NOx in the exhaust stream is:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NOx}} = m_{\text{exh}} \times \text{NOx concentration}$$

Substitute the values:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NOx}} = 720 \times 800 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NOx}} = 0.576\ \text{kg/h}$$

**Logical explanation:** A "ppm by mass" means parts per million by weight. Multiplying 720 kg/h by 800 × 10⁻⁶ gives the mass of NOx present per hour.

---

#### Step 2: Calculate the mass of NOx to be reduced (target)

The SCR system must achieve **80 %** reduction. So the mass of NOx that must be converted is:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NOx,reduced}} = 0.80 \times \dot{m}_{\text{NOx}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NOx,reduced}} = 0.80 \times 0.576$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NOx,reduced}} = 0.4608\ \text{kg/h}$$

---

#### Step 3: Stoichiometric NH₃ requirement

From the **Standard SCR** reaction:

$$4\,\text{NH}_3 + 4\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The molar ratio of NH₃ to NO is **1 : 1**. So to reduce 1 mole of NO, we need 1 mole of NH₃.

**Molar masses:**
- M_NO = 30 g/mol (using NO since diesel NOx is predominantly NO, ~ 90 %)
- M_NH₃ = 17 g/mol

**Mass of NH₃ required to reduce 0.4608 kg/h of NO:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NH}_3} = \dot{m}_{\text{NOx,reduced}} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{NH}_3}}{M_{\text{NO}}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NH}_3} = 0.4608 \times \dfrac{17}{30}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NH}_3} = 0.2611\ \text{kg/h}$$

---

#### Step 4: Convert NH₃ to Urea mass (AdBlue contains 32.5 % urea)

**Step 4a — Urea to NH₃ conversion:**
From the urea decomposition reaction:

$$\text{(NH}_2)_2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$$

- 1 mole of urea (60 g/mol) → 2 moles of NH₃ (2 × 17 = 34 g/mol)
- So **1 kg of NH₃** requires: $\dfrac{60}{34} = 1.765\ \text{kg of urea}$

**Step 4b — Pure urea mass required:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{urea (pure)}} = 0.2611 \times 1.765$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{urea (pure)}} = 0.461\ \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4c — AdBlue® mass required (32.5 % urea by weight):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{AdBlue}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{urea (pure)}}}{0.325}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{AdBlue}} = \dfrac{0.461}{0.325}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{AdBlue}} = 1.418\ \text{kg/h}$$

---

#### Step 5: AdBlue consumption per 100 km

**Diesel consumption per 100 km:**
$$V_{\text{diesel}} = 30\ \text{L/100 km}$$

**Diesel mass per 100 km:**
$$m_{\text{diesel}} = 30 \times 0.835 = 25.05\ \text{kg/100 km}$$

**Ratio of AdBlue to diesel (mass):**
$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{1.418 \times \text{h}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{h}} = \text{constant (since flows scale)}$$

To compute AdBlue per 100 km, we can use the rule of thumb that AdBlue consumption is approximately **5 % of fuel mass**:

$$\text{AdBlue/100 km} \approx 0.05 \times 25.05 = 1.25\ \text{kg/100 km}$$

Since AdBlue density ≈ **1.087 kg/L** (32.5 % urea solution at 20 °C):

$$V_{\text{AdBlue}} = \dfrac{1.25}{1.087}$$

$$V_{\text{AdBlue}} \approx 1.15\ \text{L/100 km}$$

---

#### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| NOx in exhaust | 0.576 kg/h |
| NOx to be reduced (80 %) | 0.4608 kg/h |
| NH₃ required | 0.261 kg/h |
| Pure urea required | 0.461 kg/h |
| AdBlue dosing rate | **1.418 kg/h** |
| AdBlue consumption | **≈ 1.15 L per 100 km** |

---

### 3.2 DPF Soot Mass Estimation from Pressure Drop

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A DPF has a clean ΔP of **3 kPa** at exhaust flow **Q = 0.2 kg/s**. After 1500 km of city driving, ΔP rises to **15 kPa**. Estimate the soot mass loaded in the DPF, given the linear relationship:

$$\Delta P = \Delta P_{\text{clean}} + K \cdot m_{\text{soot}}$$

where **K = 0.6 kPa/g**.

**Step 1 — Pressure rise due to soot:**
$$\Delta P_{\text{soot}} = \Delta P_{\text{loaded}} - \Delta P_{\text{clean}}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{soot}} = 15 - 3 = 12\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Soot mass from linear relationship:**
$$m_{\text{soot}} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{\text{soot}}}{K}$$

$$m_{\text{soot}} = \dfrac{12}{0.6} = 20\ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Verify against threshold:**
Since 20 g is approaching the typical regeneration trigger threshold (~ 25 g), the ECU should schedule active regeneration within the next 200–300 km.

---

### 3.3 Lambda Window Sensitivity for TWC

**Problem (KTU Part A – 3 marks):**
Explain with a sketch why the conversion efficiency of a TWC drops sharply when the engine operates outside the λ = 1 ± 0.01 window.

**Model Answer Outline (for KTU valuation):**
1. **Definition:** λ is the normalized air-fuel ratio (actual/stoichiometric).
2. **Within window (λ = 1 ± 0.01):** Both oxidation (CO, HC → CO₂, H₂O) and reduction (NOx → N₂) reactions proceed efficiently because O₂ supply matches stoichiometric needs. Efficiency > 95 %.
3. **Rich side (λ < 0.99):** Lack of O₂ → CO and HC cannot be fully oxidized. Excess unburnt fuel absorbs heat, dropping catalyst temperature. NOx reduction is okay but oxidation fails. **HC and CO emissions rise sharply.**
4. **Lean side (λ > 1.01):** Excess O₂ → oxidation reactions proceed, but reducing agents (CO, HC, H₂) are consumed quickly. NOx has no reducing partner. **NOx emissions rise sharply.**
5. **Conclusion:** The TWC requires closed-loop lambda control to keep oscillating around λ = 1.
