---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Distributed** and **Zonal Electrical Architectures** in modern vehicles. Discuss the engineering benefits and challenges of migrating from a distributed to a zonal topology. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **LIN protocol architecture**, the role of the master and slave, the concept of a **Schedule Table**, and the **LIN enhanced checksum** algorithm. Demonstrate the checksum calculation for the data bytes `[0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0x78]` and PID `0x4A`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

| Aspect | Distributed | Zonal |
|---|---|---|
| ECU count | 80–150 | 20–30 (plus HPC) |
| Wiring harness | 3–5 km, complex | <1 km, optimized |
| Communication | Multiple CAN/LIN buses | Central Ethernet backbone |
| Compute | Distributed, fragmented | Centralized HPC, zonal pre-processing |
| OTA updates | Per ECU, slow | Single HPC update, fast |
| Example | Maruti Swift (current) | Tesla Model 3 |

**Benefits of Zonal:**
- ~70% reduction in wiring harness mass and cost.
- Simplified manufacturing (fewer connectors, modular zones).
- Easier integration of high-bandwidth sensors (cameras, LiDAR).
- Centralized OTA and cybersecurity.

**Challenges:**
- Huge software complexity on HPC (requires service-oriented architecture, e.g., AUTOSAR Adaptive).
- High single-point-of-failure risk.
- Migration cost for legacy OEMs.
- Heat dissipation from HPC.

#### Model Solution (b):

The LIN bus is a **single-wire**, **single-master / multi-slave** bus operating at **19.2 kbps max**. A typical frame is shown below.

**Header → Response sequence:**
- **Break** (≥ 13 bit times dominant) — synchronization start.
- **Sync** byte `0x55` — slave detects baud rate.
- **PID** — Protected Identifier (6 bits ID + 2 bits parity).
- **Data** — 1 to 8 bytes sent by slave (or master).
- **Checksum** — 8-bit.

**Master role:** Maintains **Schedule Table**; decides which frame is broadcast in which time slot. Schedule Table is a static or dynamic list of frames with their **period (ms)** and **delay (ms from previous)**.

**Slave role:** Responds to the master's header based on its assigned frame ID.

**Enhanced Checksum Algorithm:**

**Step 1:** Add all data bytes plus PID:

$$
S = 0x12 + 0x34 + 0x56 + 0x78 + 0x4A = 18 + 52 + 86 + 120 + 74 = 350
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to hex: $S = 0x015E$.

**Step 3:** Take low 8 bits: $0x5E$; carry: $0x01$.

$$
C = 0x5E + 0x01 = 0x5F
$$

**Step 4:** No second overflow. Bitwise NOT:

$$
\overline{0x5F} = 0xA0
$$

**Result:** Transmitted checksum = **`0xA0`**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Classical CAN with CAN FD overhead numbers** in the frame efficiency question (you will lose 2 marks instantly).
> 2. **Forgetting to add the PID to the LIN checksum** — many students compute the checksum over data bytes *only*; this is the **legacy LIN 1.x** algorithm, NOT the **enhanced LIN 2.0** algorithm. The question typically expects 2.0.
> 3. **Drawing a CAN frame with bits in the wrong order** (e.g., ACK before CRC) — leads to -1 mark.
> 4. **Zonal architecture question**: failing to mention the **HPC (High-Performance Computer)** and **AUTOSAR Adaptive** is considered an incomplete answer.
> 5. **Unit errors**: leaving the baud rate in kbps while expecting m in the bus-length formula; always **standardize to bps** before substitution.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ECU** is the generic term; **ECM, TCM, BCM, ABS/ESC, SRS, ADAS controller** are specific instances.
- ✅ **ABS sensors** = WSS + YRS + SAS + accelerometers. **ABS actuator** = hydraulic modulator + pump.
- ✅ **CAN** = differential bus, NRZ + bit stuffing, **120 Ω** termination, **CSMA/CR + AMP** arbitration, **11-bit** (Classical) or **29-bit** (Extended) ID, payload **0–8 B** (Classical) or **0–64 B** (FD).
- ✅ **CAN FD** brings **5 Mbps** in data phase (BRS bit) and **64-byte** payload.
- ✅ **LIN** = single-wire, **19.2 kbps**, single master, max **16 slaves**, enhanced checksum includes **PID** in the sum.
- ✅ **Automotive Ethernet** = **100/1000BASE-T1**, full-duplex on a **single twisted pair**; uses **PAM-3**; mandatory for ADAS (cameras, LiDAR, V2X).
- ✅ **Distributed** = many ECUs + complex harness; **Zonal** = few zone controllers + central HPC + Ethernet backbone.
- ✅ **Bus efficiency**: Classical CAN 8B ≈ **58%**; CAN FD 64B ≈ **88%**.
- ✅ **Max CAN bus length** ≈ $5 \times 10^7 / f_{baud}$ meters.
- ✅ **Watchdog, BIST, ISO 26262** = safety features every modern ECU must support.
- ✅ **AUTOSAR Classic** for deeply embedded ECUs; **AUTOSAR Adaptive** for HPC/zone controllers running POSIX OS.
- ✅ **Termination** resistors only at the **two ends** of a CAN bus; never in the middle.
- ✅ **CAN recessive bit** is not a true 0 V; it is the **idle voltage ≈ 2.5 V** on both lines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Vehicle Components and Energy Flow
On-Board Diagnostic Basics - DTC Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** On-Board Diagnostic Basics - DTC Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is On-Board Diagnostics (OBD)?

**On-Board Diagnostics (OBD)** is a vehicle's built-in **self-diagnostic and reporting system** standardized under **SAE J1979** and mandated by regulations such as the **US Clean Air Act Amendments (1990)** and **European EOBD (European On-Board Diagnostics)** directives. The core function of OBD is to continuously monitor the performance of the engine, transmission, emissions control system, and other vital subsystems via sensors and actuators connected to the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** / **Engine Control Module (ECM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"OBD is a standardized automotive electronic system that provides vehicle self-diagnosis and reporting capabilities, allowing technicians to access real-time data and historical Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) for troubleshooting emissions-related and major vehicle faults."*

### Evolution: OBD-I vs OBD-II

| Generation | Year | Key Characteristics | Standardization |
|------------|------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **OBD-I** | 1988–1995 | Manufacturer-specific connectors, codes, and protocols | Low – proprietary |
| **OBD-II (OBD2)** | 1996 onwards | Universal 16-pin J1962 connector, standardized DTCs (P0xxx) | **SAE J1962, J1979, J2012** |
| **EOBD** | 2001 (Petrol) / 2004 (Diesel) in EU | European equivalent for emissions | EU Directive 98/69/EC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Highlight:** OBD-II is a **mandatory legal requirement** for all petrol cars sold in India since **April 2010 (Bharat Stage IV)** and for all vehicles from **BS-VI (April 2017 onward)** under AIS-137 (Automotive Industry Standards).

## 1.2 What is a Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)?

A **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)** is a **5-character alphanumeric code** (e.g., `P0301`, `B1342`) stored inside the ECU's **non-volatile memory (NVRAM/KAM – Keep Alive Memory)** whenever the on-board diagnostic system detects a malfunction that exceeds a calibrated **threshold value** for a specific duration (drive cycle).

When a DTC is set, the ECU typically:
1. Stores the **fault code** with a snapshot of engine conditions.
2. Activates the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** — also called the "Check Engine Light."
3. Switches the ECU into **limp-home mode** for safety in severe cases.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor Inside Your Car"

Imagine your car is a human body. The **ECU is the brain**, the **sensors are the nerves**, and the **DTC is a doctor's diagnosis slip**. Just like a doctor writes a code on a prescription (`Fever – 101°F, since 3 days, cause: infection`), your car writes a code on its digital "medical chart" (`P0301 – Cylinder 1 Misfire detected, since 3 drive cycles, cause: faulty ignition coil`). 

When you visit a mechanic, he connects a **scan tool (OBD-II reader)** to the **Data Link Connector (DLC)** — exactly like a doctor plugs an ECG machine into a patient — and reads out these "diagnosis slips" to understand what's wrong.

> [!NOTE]
> **MIL Threshold Standard (US OBD-II):**
> The MIL must illuminate if a fault occurs for **2 consecutive drive cycles** (one-trip fault for catalytic converter damage or engine misfire).

---

## 1.3 KTU Board Examination Relevance

This topic is a **favourite 7-to-14 mark question** under KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 4 of PEMET742. Common question framings include:
- *"Explain OBD-II system architecture with a neat sketch."* (14 marks)
- *"Explain DTC format with examples."* (7 marks)
- *"Differentiate between OBD-I and OBD-II."* (3-7 marks)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OBD-II System Architecture — The Five Functional Layers

The OBD-II system can be deconstructed into the following functional layers:

### Layer 1: **Input Layer (Sensors)**
Sensors continuously feed the ECU with real-time vehicle data. Examples:
- Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)
- Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)
- Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF)
- Oxygen / Lambda (O2) Sensors (Upstream & Downstream)
- Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)
- Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor
- Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT) Sensor
- Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)

### Layer 2: **Processing Layer (ECU/ECM)**
The ECU executes **diagnostic algorithms** (diagnostic routines) written into its **firmware (calibration software)**. These routines perform:
- **Rationality checks** (does the sensor value make physical sense?)
- **Range checks** (low/high input voltage threshold)
- **Circuit continuity checks** (open/short circuit detection)

### Layer 3: **Storage Layer (NVRAM / KAM)**
Stores active DTCs, **freeze-frame data** (engine RPM, load, coolant temp at the moment of fault), and **permanent DTCs** (cannot be cleared by disconnecting battery).

### Layer 4: **Communication Layer (SAE J1962 DLC + SAE J1979 Protocol)**
The **16-pin J1962 Data Link Connector (DLC)** located under the dashboard transmits data using one of these protocols:
- **ISO 15765 (CAN bus)** — most modern (mandatory from 2008 in US)
- **ISO 14230 (KWP2000)**
- **ISO 9141-2**
- **SAE J1850 PWM / VPW** (older US vehicles)

### Layer 5: **Output Layer (MIL + Scan Tool)**
- **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** — typically amber/yellow dashboard lamp.
- **External scan tool / OBD-II reader** used by technicians.

## 2.2 DTC Format Decoding — The Universal 5-Character Code

Every DTC follows the standard **SAE J2012** format:

$$\text{DTC} = \underbrace{X}_{\text{System}} \; \underbrace{Y}_{\text{Code Type}} \; \underbrace{Z}_{\text{Subsystem}} \; \underbrace{N_1 \, N_2}_{\text{Specific Fault}}$$

### Detailed Character-by-Character Breakdown

| Position | Value | Meaning |
|----------|-------|---------|
| **1st character (X)** | **P** | **Powertrain** (Engine, Transmission, Fuel, Emissions) |
| | **C** | **Chassis** (ABS, Steering, Suspension, Brakes) |
| | **B** | **Body** (Airbags, Lighting, Power Windows, HVAC) |
| | **U** | **Network/Communication** (CAN, LIN, UDS, Module-to-module link) |
| **2nd character (Y)** | **0** | **Generic (SAE/EOBD) — Global standard** |
| | **1** | **Manufacturer-Specific (OEM code)** |
| | **2** | Generic (Powell only, introduced later) |
| | **3** | Generic (reserved) |
| **3rd character (Z)** | **1** | Fuel/Air metering (e.g., MAF, fuel injection) |
| | **2** | Fuel/Air metering (injector circuit) |
| | **3** | Ignition system / Misfire |
| | **4** | Auxiliary emissions controls (EGR, EVAP) |
| | **5** | Vehicle speed, idle, engine speed control |
| | **6** | ECU / Computer output circuit |
| | **7** | Transmission |
| | **8** | Transmission (non-OBD-II) |
| **4th–5th (N₁N₂)** | 00–99 | Specific fault index (assigned by SAE) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---------------------|--------|----------------|------|
| DLC connector pins (OBD-II) | $N_{pin}$ | **16** | pins |
| Maximum DTCs stored (typical) | $N_{DTC}$ | **≥ 5 active + permanent** | codes |
| MIL illumination drive cycles | $N_{cycle}$ | **2** | drive cycles |
| OBD-II voltage supply (Pin 16) | $V_{bat}$ | **+12 V DC** | volts |
| OBD-II signal voltage (high) | $V_{high}$ | **≥ 4.5** | volts |
| OBD-II signal voltage (low) | $V_{low}$ | **≤ 1.5** | volts |
| CAN bus speed (OBD) | $f_{CAN}$ | **250 / 500 kbps** | kilobits/second |
| Catalyst efficiency threshold | $\eta_{cat}$ | **< 50 %** | percent |
| Misfire MIL threshold | $\delta_{misfire}$ | **> 1 % to 2 %** | percent of revolutions |
| Fuel trim long-term limit | $\lambda_{trim}$ | **±25 %** | percent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> The DTC framework allows **fleet operators** and **service workshops** to perform **predictive maintenance** by reading DTC history, which is critical in the era of **connected cars** and **IoT-enabled telematics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DTC Code Identification (Step-by-Step Decoding)

Let us rigorously decode the example DTC **`P0301`** used commonly in KTU questions.

**Step 1 — Identify the System (1st character)**

Since the first character is `P`, we identify:

$$\text{System} = \text{P} \implies \text{Powertrain (Engine, Transmission, Emissions)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Code Type (2nd character)**

The second character is `3`. We look up:

$$\text{Code Type} = 3 \implies \text{Specific manufacturer-related (but in this case, SAE Generic since first digit is 0)}$$

Wait — let us re-examine: `P0xxx` codes are generic. So:

$$\text{Generic SAE Code} \quad (\text{where } Y = 0)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Subsystem (3rd character)**

The third character is `0`. Using the standard SAE table:

$$\text{Subsystem} = 0 \implies \text{SAE-defined Controlled Powertrain Subsystem}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Specific Fault (4th & 5th characters)**

The remaining characters are `3` and `0`, then `1`:

$$\text{Specific Fault Index} = 301 \implies \text{Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected}$$

**Step 5 — Final Interpretation**

$$\boxed{\text{P0301} \equiv \text{Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected on Cylinder 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answers, always present the **expanded meaning**, e.g., for **P0171**:
> *"System Too Lean (Bank 1)"* — meaning the fuel mixture has excessive air relative to fuel.

## 3.2 Worked Example: MIL Illumination Logic

The OBD-II system illuminates the MIL when a **confirmed fault** persists for a defined number of drive cycles. Let us derive the criterion.

Let:
- $N_{cycle}$ = number of drive cycles since fault first detected
- $N_{th}$ = threshold number of cycles (typically **2**)
- $F_{status}$ = fault status flag (1 = active, 0 = cleared)

The MIL activation logic is:

$$
\text{MIL} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{ON (Steady)} & \text{if } N_{cycle} \geq N_{th} \text{ and } F_{status} = 1 \\
\text{ON (Flashing)} & \text{if fault = severe catalyst-damaging misfire} \\
\text{OFF} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

For **P0301 (misfire)**, if the misfire rate exceeds the catalyst-damaging threshold:

$$\text{Misfire Rate} \, (\delta_{misfire}) > 2\% \implies \text{MIL flashes immediately (1-trip fault)}$$

Whereas for **P0171 (lean condition)**:

$$N_{cycle} = 2 \text{ trips} \implies \text{MIL illuminates steady}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common DTCs for Quick Reference

| DTC Code | System | Meaning | Typical Root Cause |
|----------|--------|---------|---------------------|
| **P0010** | Powertrain | "A" Camshaft Position Actuator Circuit (Bank 1) | Faulty VVT solenoid |
| **P0128** | Powertrain | Coolant Thermostat (below regulating temp) | Stuck-open thermostat |
| **P0131** | Powertrain | O2 Sensor 1 Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 1) | Lean exhaust / faulty O2 sensor |
| **P0171** | Powertrain | System Too Lean (Bank 1) | Vacuum leak, weak fuel pump, dirty MAF |
| **P0300** | Powertrain | Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire | Spark plugs, ignition coils, fuel pressure |
| **P0301** | Powertrain | Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected | Ignition coil #1, injector, compression loss |
| **P0420** | Powertrain | Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1) | Worn catalytic converter |
| **P0700** | Powertrain | Transmission Control System Malfunction | Internal TCM fault |
| **C1234** | Chassis | ABS Wheel Speed Sensor Circuit Malfunction | WSS / tone ring damage |
| **B1342** | Body | ECU Defective | Internal ECU hardware fault |
| **U0100** | Network | Lost Communication with ECM/PCM | CAN bus wiring break, faulty ECM |

## 3.4 KTU Examiner's Analytic Reasoning Template

When answering a KTU exam on DTC format, the examiner expects the following **rigorous structure**:

1. **State the standard** → "SAE J2012 defines a 5-character DTC format..."
2. **Break down each digit** → Use the standard table.
3. **Provide at least two decoded examples** → E.g., P0301 and P0420.
4. **Mention MIL logic** → Drive-cycle threshold.
5. **Draw the OBD-II block diagram** → Sensors → ECU → DLC → Scan Tool.
6. **Conclude with a real-world application** → E.g., emission testing centers in India use OBD-II scans under PUC (Pollution Under Control) certification for BS-VI vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OBD-II System Block Architecture (Mermaid Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CRANKSHAFT POSITION SKP] --> B1[ECU CORE PROCESSOR]
    A2[CAMSHAFT POSITION CMP] --> B1
    A3[MASS AIR FLOW MAF] --> B1
    A4[OXYGEN LAMBDA O2] --> B1
    A5[THROTTLE TPS] --> B1
    A6[COOLANT TEMP ECT] --> B1
    A7[VEHICLE SPEED VSS] --> B1
    A8[KNOCK SENSOR KS] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{NVRAM KAM MEMORY}
    C1 --> C2[ACTIVE DTC STORAGE]
    C1 --> C3[FREEZE FRAME DATA]
    C1 --> C4[PERMANENT DTC STORAGE]
    B1 --> D1[MIL MALFUNCTION INDICATOR LAMP]
    B1 --> D2[LIMP HOME MODE ACTIVATION]
    B1 --> E1[SAE J1962 DLC 16 PIN CONNECTOR]
    E1 --> E2[SAE J1979 PROTOCOL LAYER]
    E2 --> E3[CAN BUS ISO 15765]
    E2 --> E4[KWP2000 ISO 14230]
    E2 --> E5[ISO 9141 2]
    E3 --> F1[EXTERNAL SCAN TOOL]
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[TECHNICIAN LAPTOP OR DIAGNOSTIC DEVICE]
```

## 4.2 DTC Decoding Flowchart (Logic Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[READ DTC FROM ECU] --> q1{FIRST CHARACTER}
    q1 -->|P| ppath[POWERTRAIN ENGINE TRANSMISSION]
    q1 -->|C| cpath[CHASSIS BRAKES STEERING]
    q1 -->|B| bpath[BODY AIRBAG LIGHTING HVAC]
    q1 -->|U| upath[NETWORK COMMUNICATION CAN UDS]
    ppath --> q2{SECOND CHARACTER}
    q2 -->|0| gen[SAE GENERIC CODE GLOBAL]
    q2 -->|1| mfg[MANUFACTURER SPECIFIC OEM]
    gen --> q3{THIRD CHARACTER SUBSYSTEM}
    q3 -->|1 or 2| sa[FUEL AIR METERING]
    q3 -->|3| mis[IGNITION MISFIRE]
    q3 -->|4| aux[AUX EMISSION EVAP EGR]
    q3 -->|5| spd[SPEED IDLE CONTROL]
    q3 -->|6| out[ECU OUTPUT CIRCUIT]
    q3 -->|7 or 8| trn[TRANSMISSION]
    mis --> final[INDEX 4TH 5TH DIGIT SPECIFIC FAULT e.g. 01 CYLINDER 1 MISFIRE]
    sa --> final
    aux --> final
    spd --> final
    out --> final
    trn --> final
```

## 4.3 MIL Activation State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NOFAULT
    NOFAULT --> FAULTDETECTED : SENSOR OUT OF RANGE
    FAULTDETECTED --> PENDING : FIRST TRIP RECORDED
    PENDING --> MILON : SECOND TRIP CONFIRMED
    PENDING --> NOFAULT : FAULT DISAPPEARS
    MILON --> MILFLASHING : SEVERE FAULT CATALYST DAMAGE
    MILON --> NOFAULT : CLEARED BY SCAN TOOL OR 40 WARMUP CYCLES
    MILFLASHING --> NOFAULT : FAULT RESOLVED
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `gen`, `mis`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. Labels are kept raw and uppercase for maximum readability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define OBD-II. List its major advantages over OBD-I.**

**Model Answer:**

**OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics II)** is the **second-generation standardized self-diagnostic system** mandated for all vehicles sold in the US from 1996 and in India from BS-IV (2010) onwards. It is governed by **SAE standards J1962, J1979, and J2012**.

**Major Advantages over OBD-I:**
1. **Standardized 16-pin J1962 DLC connector** — works across all manufacturers.
2. **Standardized DTC codes (P0xxx)** — globally uniform.
3. **Real-time data streaming** via standardized PIDs (Parameter IDs).
4. **Standardized communication protocols** (CAN, KWP2000, ISO 9141).
5. **Mandatory MIL with 2-trip confirmation logic** for emission compliance.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Decode the DTC "P0420" with its full meaning.**

**Model Answer:**

Decoding using SAE J2012 standard:

| Character | Value | Interpretation |
|-----------|-------|----------------|
| 1st | P | Powertrain system |
| 2nd | 0 | SAE Generic (global) code |
| 3rd | 4 | Auxiliary Emissions subsystem |
| 4th & 5th | 20 | Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1) |

$$\boxed{\text{P0420} \equiv \text{Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)}}$$

**Typical cause:** Worn-out or contaminated catalytic converter.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of the OBD-II system with a neat block diagram. List the various standardized communication protocols used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)? Explain the universal 5-character DTC format defined by SAE J2012 with at least four decoded examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**OBD-II Architecture:**

The OBD-II system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Input Layer — Sensors:**
   Crankshaft Position (CKP), Camshaft Position (CMP), Mass Air Flow (MAF), Oxygen Sensor (O2 / Lambda), Throttle Position (TPS), Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT), Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS).

2. **Processing Layer — ECU/ECM:**
   The ECU runs **diagnostic software algorithms** including range checks, rationality checks, and circuit continuity tests.

3. **Storage Layer — NVRAM / KAM:**
   Stores active DTCs, **freeze-frame data**, and permanent DTCs (legally retained).

4. **Communication Layer — SAE J1962 DLC + Protocols:**

   | Protocol | Standard | Common In |
   |----------|----------|-----------|
   | CAN bus | ISO 15765 | All modern vehicles (post-2008) |
   | KWP2000 | ISO 14230 | European vehicles (1996–2008) |
   | ISO 9141-2 | ISO 9141 | Older European / Asian |
   | J1850 VPW | SAE J1850 | Older GM vehicles |
   | J1850 PWM | SAE J1850 | Older Ford vehicles |

5. **Output Layer — MIL and Scan Tool:**
   The MIL is illuminated in the instrument cluster; external scan tools interface via DLC.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition:** A DTC is a **5-character alphanumeric fault code** stored in ECU memory when a monitored parameter exceeds its calibrated threshold.

**Format per SAE J2012:**

$$\text{DTC} = \underbrace{X}_{\text{System}} \; \underbrace{Y}_{\text{Type}} \; \underbrace{Z}_{\text{Subsystem}} \; \underbrace{N_1 N_2}_{\text{Specific Fault}}$$

**Decoded Examples:**

| DTC | Meaning |
|-----|---------|
| **P0301** | Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected |
| **P0420** | Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1) |
| **P0171** | System Too Lean (Bank 1) |
| **U0100** | Lost Communication with ECM/PCM (CAN bus) |
| **C1234** | ABS Wheel Speed Sensor Circuit Malfunction |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between OBD-I and OBD-II systems. Compare their features in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) illumination logic, including one-trip and two-trip fault criteria. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

| Feature | OBD-I | OBD-II |
|---------|-------|--------|
| Year of introduction | 1988–1991 | 1996 onwards |
| Connector | Manufacturer-specific | **Standardized 16-pin SAE J1962** |
| DTC codes | Manufacturer-specific | **Standardized SAE J2012 (5-character)** |
| Communication | Proprietary | **Standardized (CAN, KWP2000, ISO 9141)** |
| Data streaming | Limited | **Real-time via PIDs** |
| Freeze-frame data | Not available | **Available** |
| MIL standardization | Not uniform | **Mandatory, 2-trip confirmation** |
| Emission focus | Limited | **Strict (US EPA / EU EOBD)** |
| Applicable in India | Pre-BS-II era | **From BS-IV (2010) onwards** |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**MIL Illumination Logic:**

The ECU constantly monitors sensor signals against **calibrated thresholds** stored in its diagnostic software. The MIL logic is governed by **drive-cycle counting**:

- **Drive Cycle:** A complete engine start-up → warm-up → drive → cool-down sequence.

**Two-Trip Fault Logic (for non-critical faults):**

$$
\text{MIL State} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{OFF} & \text{if Fault detected on Trip 1 only (PENDING)} \\
\text{ON (Steady)} & \text{if Fault persists on Trip 2 (CONFIRMED)} \\
\text{OFF} & \text{if Fault clears for 40 consecutive warm-up cycles} \\
\end{cases}
$$

**One-Trip Fault Logic (for severe / catalyst-damaging faults):**

- **Engine Misfire > 2 %** of crank revolutions → **MIL flashes immediately** (1-trip fault).
- **Catalyst damaging misfire > 5 %** → **MIL steady ON, plus limp-home mode**.

**MIL Cleared by:**
- Using a scan tool (preferred).
- Disconnecting the battery (but permanent DTCs remain).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing OBD-I and OBD-II connector** — The 16-pin J1962 is **only** for OBD-II; OBD-I connectors were proprietary and varied.
> 2. **Incorrect DTC digit decoding** — Students often forget that the **2nd digit "0"** means Generic SAE code. Misreading `P0301` as "transmission" instead of "misfire" is a common error.
> 3. **MIL logic confusion** — Many students write that MIL lights up on a single fault occurrence. Correct answer: **2-trip confirmation for emission faults; 1-trip for severe catalyst-damaging misfire.**
> 4. **Forgetting freeze-frame data** — Examiners often award 1–2 marks for mentioning the **freeze-frame snapshot**.
> 5. **Missing Indian context (BS-VI / AIS-137)** — A simple mention of OBD-II being mandatory under **AIS-137 for BS-VI vehicles in India** scores extra marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **OBD-II** stands for **On-Board Diagnostics, Second Generation**, mandated from 1996 globally and **2010 (BS-IV) onwards in India**.
- ✅ Standardized by **SAE J1962** (connector), **SAE J1979** (protocol), **SAE J2012** (DTCs).
- ✅ DTCs follow a **5-character format**: **P/C/B/U + 0/1/2/3 + Subsystem digit + 2-digit fault index**.
- ✅ **P = Powertrain, C = Chassis, B = Body, U = Network/Communication.**
- ✅ **Second digit "0" = Generic SAE code (same worldwide); "1" = Manufacturer-specific.**
- ✅ Common powertrain codes: **P0301 (misfire), P0420 (cat. efficiency), P0171 (lean).**
- ✅ The **DLC (Data Link Connector)** is a **16-pin J1962 connector** typically located under the dashboard near the steering column.
- ✅ Modern OBD-II uses **CAN bus (ISO 15765)** at **250 kbps or 500 kbps**.
- ✅ **MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp)** confirms a fault in **2 consecutive drive cycles**, but for **severe misfire**, it activates in **1 trip** and may flash.
- ✅ **Freeze-frame data** captures engine RPM, load, coolant temp, and fuel trim at the instant of fault — invaluable for diagnosis.
- ✅ **Permanent DTCs** cannot be erased by disconnecting the battery; they are auto-cleared only when the OBD-II monitor runs successfully.
- ✅ Indian regulation: **AIS-137 (Automotive Industry Standard)** implements OBD-II for **BS-VI** vehicles; Stage-II OBD-II under **BS-VI Phase 2 (2026)** will introduce stricter monitoring including particulate filter and AdBlue (DEF) monitoring.
- ✅ Indian emission testing centres use OBD-II scans at **PUC (Pollution Under Control)** stations for BS-VI vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of ADAS: Sensors, Levels of Automation, Examples (LDWS to Lane Change Assist), Adaptive Cruise Control, Automatic Emergency Braking, Driver Monitoring System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of ADAS: Sensors, Levels of Automation, Examples (LDWS to Lane Change Assist), Adaptive Cruise Control, Automatic Emergency Braking, Driver Monitoring System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** are a coordinated suite of electronic and electromechanical sub-systems in a road vehicle that use **onboard sensors, embedded controllers and actuator interfaces** to continuously perceive the driving environment, interpret driver intent and vehicle state, and either **inform the driver, augment driver control, or temporarily automate** selected longitudinal (speed/distance) and lateral (steering/lane) driving tasks — with the explicit goal of improving **safety, comfort and fuel efficiency**.

Mathematically, an ADAS can be represented as a closed-loop perception–planning–action triad:

$$
\text{ADAS} \equiv f\big(\,\underbrace{S_{\text{sensor}}}_{\text{Perception}},\ \underbrace{M_{\text{map/state}}}_{\text{Localization}},\ \underbrace{C_{\text{controller}}}_{\text{Planning}},\ \underbrace{A_{\text{actuator}}}_{\text{Action}}\,\big)
$$

where each block transforms raw physical input into a controlled mechanical output (throttle, brake, steering torque, warning).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Co-Pilot in Your Dashboard"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are driving with a vigilant human co-pilot sitting beside you. This co-pilot has:
> - 👁 **Eyes** → Look far ahead, read traffic signs, judge lane markings.
> - 👂 **Ears** → Sense approaching sirens or honks before you do.
> - ✋ **Touch** → Feel the steering wheel tension and car vibrations.
> - 🧠 **Brain** → Predicts the danger ("that truck is braking hard!") and acts.
> - 🗣 **Voice** → Warns you ("BRAKE!") or sometimes grabs the wheel.

In an ADAS-equipped car:
- **Eyes** = Cameras (visible/IR) — read lane paint, traffic lights, pedestrians.
- **Ears** = Radar & Ultrasonic — detect closing speed and blind-spot vehicles.
- **Touch** = Lidar & wheel-speed sensors — measure range and yaw rate.
- **Brain** = ADAS ECU / Domain Controller running sensor-fusion algorithms.
- **Voice & Hands** = HMI displays, brake/steering/throttle actuators.

So when the textbook says *"ADAS senses the environment and acts,"* just remember: **it is a robotic co-pilot with no ego, no sleep, and reaction times of ~30 ms.**

---

## 1.3 Why ADAS? — KTU Board Examiner's Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key statistics KTU examiners love:**
> - Human reaction time: ~**1.0 s** (best case)
> - Automated ADAS reaction time: ~**30–100 ms** (10× faster)
> - WHO estimate: ~**1.19 million** deaths/year globally due to road accidents
> - ADAS targets **three killer errors**: distraction, late braking, lane drift.

Hence, the regulatory push (Euro NCAP 2025+, GSR 2022 in EU, AIS-184 in India) **mandates** features like AEB, LDWS, DMS in new vehicles — making this topic a **high-weightage, repeated-ask** subject in KTU ESE papers.

---

## 1.4 Three Pillars of ADAS Functionality

| Pillar | Meaning | Example Sub-systems |
|---|---|---|
| **Sense** | Measure environment & vehicle state | Camera, Radar, Lidar, Ultrasonic, IMU |
| **Think** | Fuse, classify, decide | Sensor-fusion ECU, ML classifier, MPC |
| **Act** | Drive actuators or alert driver | Brake-by-wire, EPS torque overlay, HUD |

This Sense → Think → Act chain is the **unifying theme** of every ADAS function discussed in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The ADAS Sensor Stack (Sense Layer)

An ADAS cannot "see" with one sense alone. Modern vehicles use **sensor fusion** of complementary modalities.

### 2.1.1 Camera (Mono / Stereo / IR)

- **Working principle:** CMOS/CCD image sensor; passive detection of visible/IR light.
- **Range:** ~**0–250 m** (forward); ~**0–80 m** (surround).
- **Strength:** Excellent classification (reads lane paint, signs, pedestrians).
- **Weakness:** Fails in fog, heavy rain, low light, sun glare.

Key parameter — **Horizontal Field of View (HFOV):**

$$
\text{HFOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{w_{\text{sensor}}}{2 f}\right)
$$

where $w_{\text{sensor}}$ = sensor width, $f$ = focal length.

### 2.1.2 Radar (24 GHz / 77 GHz FMCW)

- **Working principle:** Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave; measures **range, radial velocity, angle** via time-of-flight and Doppler.
- **Range:** Long-range radar (LRR) ~**0–250 m**; Short-range radar (SRR) ~**0–80 m**.
- **Strength:** All-weather (rain, fog, dust); measures velocity directly.
- **Weakness:** Poor lateral resolution; cannot classify object type.

Two-way time-of-flight:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

Doppler-based relative velocity:

$$
v_{\text{rel}} \;=\; \frac{c \cdot f_{\text{D}}}{2 f_0}
$$

where $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3\times10^8$ m/s), $f_{\text{D}}$ = Doppler shift, $f_0$ = carrier.

### 2.1.3 Lidar (905 nm / 1550 nm)

- **Working principle:** Time-of-flight of laser pulses; generates a **3-D point cloud**.
- **Range:** ~**0–200 m** (typical automotive).
- **Strength:** Highest spatial resolution; ideal for object bounding boxes.
- **Weakness:** Expensive; degraded in heavy precipitation.

$$
R_{\text{lidar}} \;=\; \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2 n}
$$

where $\tau$ = round-trip pulse delay, $n$ = refractive index of medium.

### 2.1.4 Ultrasonic

- **Working principle:** Piezo transducer emits 40–70 kHz burst; measures echo time.
- **Range:** ~**0.1–5 m**.
- **Strength:** Cheap; ideal for parking.
- **Weakness:** Very short range; speed-of-sound in air is slow.

$$
d_{\text{us}} \;=\; \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}, \qquad v_{\text{sound}} \approx 343\ \text{m/s at } 20^\circ\text{C}
$$

---

## 2.2 Sensor Placement on a Modern ADAS Vehicle

> [!NOTE]
> A typical Level-2 ADAS car carries **20–30 individual sensors**:
> - **1× front long-range radar + 1× front camera** (forward sensing)
> - **4× corner radars** (blind-spot, cross-traffic)
> - **8× surround cameras** (surround view, park assist)
> - **12× ultrasonic** (parking)
> - **1× driver-facing IR camera** (DMS)
> - **1× front Lidar** (in premium/Level-3+)

---

## 2.3 SAE Levels of Driving Automation (THE "0–5" Chart)

This is the **single most-asked topic** in ADAS KTU papers — expect either a 3-mark direct question or a 14-mark explanatory one.

| **SAE Level** | **Name** | **Who Drives?** | **Who Monitors Environment?** | **Example Systems** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **0** | No Automation | Human (all) | Human | ABS, ESC, basic AEB (warning only) |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | Human + system (single task) | Human | Adaptive Cruise Control OR Lane Keep Assist (one at a time) |
| **2** | Partial Automation | Human (must supervise) | System | ACC + LKA simultaneously (Tesla Autopilot, Honda Sensing core) |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | System (within ODD); human fallback | System | Traffic Jam Pilot (Audi A8, Mercedes Drive Pilot) |
| **4** | High Automation | System (within ODD); no human needed | System | Robotaxis (Waymo, Cruise) — geofenced |
| **5** | Full Automation | System (everywhere) | System | Steering wheel optional (concept only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ODD = Operational Design Domain** — the specific conditions (road type, speed, weather, geofence) under which the automation is allowed to operate. Examiners often test ODD as a concept.

---

## 2.4 ADAS Function Reference Table (LDWS → Lane Change Assist)

| **Function (Acronym)** | **Full Form** | **Type** | **Primary Sensors** | **Actuator** |
|:--|:--|:-:|:--|:--|
| **LDWS** | Lane Departure Warning System | Inform | Front camera | HMI (buzzer + steering wheel vibration) |
| **LKA / LKS** | Lane Keeping Assist | Lateral control | Front camera | EPS (Electric Power Steering) |
| **BSM / BSD** | Blind Spot Detection | Inform | Corner radar | Mirror LED + buzzer |
| **LCA** | Lane Change Assist | Lateral control | Corner radar + side camera | EPS + indicator auto-trigger |
| **ACC** | Adaptive Cruise Control | Longitudinal control | Front radar (± camera) | Throttle + brake-by-wire |
| **AEB** | Automatic Emergency Braking | Longitudinal safety | Front radar + camera | Brake-by-wire (full decel) |
| **FCW** | Forward Collision Warning | Inform | Front radar + camera | HMI |
| **DMS** | Driver Monitoring System | Driver state | IR camera (driver-facing) | HMI + escalation |
| **TJA / QCC** | Traffic Jam Assist / Queue Assist | Combined (Lvl 2) | Radar + camera | Throttle/brake/steering |
| **APA** | Automated Parking Assist | Low-speed lateral | Ultrasonic + camera | EPS + gearbox + brake |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the equations you MUST memorise for ESE Module 4.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:-:|
| 1 | Time-To-Collision (TTC) | $ \text{TTC} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{\text{rel}}} $ | $\Delta d$ = gap, $v_{\text{rel}}$ = closing speed | s |
| 2 | Stopping distance (kinematic) | $ d_{\text{stop}} = v\,t_{\text{react}} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2\,a_{\text{max}}} $ | $v$ = speed, $t_{\text{react}}$, $a_{\text{max}}$ = decel | m |
| 3 | Safe following gap (time-headway) | $ d_{\text{safe}} = v \cdot t_{\text{h}} $ | $t_{\text{h}} = 1.5\text{–}2.0$ s typically | m |
| 4 | Radar range (CW/FMCW) | $ R = \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{2} $ | $c$ = speed of light | m |
| 5 | Radar closing velocity (Doppler) | $ v_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{c\,f_{\text{D}}}{2\,f_{0}} $ | $f_{\text{D}}$ = Doppler shift | m/s |
| 6 | Lidar range | $ R_{\text{L}} = \dfrac{c\,\tau}{2\,n} $ | $\tau$ = round-trip delay | m |
| 7 | Ultrasonic range | $ d_{\text{u}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{s}}\,t_{\text{e}}}{2} $ | $v_{\text{s}} \approx 343$ m/s | m |
| 8 | Camera HFOV | $ \text{HFOV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right) $ | $w$ = sensor width, $f$ = focal length | rad/deg |
| 9 | AEB brake-torque demand | $ T_{\text{b}} = \dfrac{m\,a_{\text{req}}\,r_{\text{w}}}{\eta\,\gamma_{\text{b}}} $ | $m$, $r_{\text{w}}$, $\gamma_{\text{b}}$, $\eta$ | N·m |
| 10 | Driver attention score (DMS, simplified) | $ S_{\text{att}} = w_1 \cdot P_{\text{eye}} + w_2 \cdot P_{\text{head}} + w_3 \cdot P_{\text{hand}} $ | Weighted probabilities | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Note: Equations 1, 2, 3 form the **theoretical core of every AEB / ACC numerical** KTU may set. Master them.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above Sense/Think/Act pipeline directly maps to:

- **Vehicle safety regulation** — Euro NCAP awards 5★ only if AEB + LDW + DMS are present.
- **Insurance telematics** — Usage-Based Insurance discounts ADAS-equipped cars (up to 25%).
- **Autonomous trucking** — Daimler/Bosch platooning uses 77 GHz radar ACC + LKA.
- **Smart city V2X** — Future ADAS will consume C-V2X messages beyond onboard sensors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> Every derivation below uses **fundamental physics + vehicle kinematics**. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-To-Collision (TTC)

**Scenario:** Two cars are aligned longitudinally. The host (ego) vehicle travels at $v_{\text{ego}}$, the lead vehicle at $v_{\text{lead}}$, with current bumper-to-bumper gap $\Delta d$. Assume $v_{\text{ego}} > v_{\text{lead}}$ (closing).

**Step 1 — Define closing speed.**
The rate at which the gap shrinks is the difference of speeds:

$$
v_{\text{rel}} \;=\; v_{\text{ego}} \;-\; v_{\text{lead}}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the gap as a function of time.**
If no action is taken, gap decreases linearly:

$$
\Delta d(t) \;=\; \Delta d_0 \;-\; v_{\text{rel}} \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Set $\Delta d(t_{\text{TTC}}) = 0$** (the moment the rear of the lead meets the front of the ego):

$$
0 \;=\; \Delta d_0 \;-\; v_{\text{rel}} \cdot t_{\text{TTC}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_{\text{TTC}}$:**

$$
\boxed{\;t_{\text{TTC}} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta d_0}{v_{\text{rel}}}\;}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- If $t_{\text{TTC}} < 2.0$ s → **AEB pre-charges**.
- If $t_{\text{TTC}} < 1.4$ s → **AEB full autonomous braking** (Euro NCAP threshold).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stopping Distance & AEB Activation Threshold

**Goal:** Find the *minimum* gap at which the ego vehicle can come to rest without striking the lead vehicle, given its deceleration capability $a_{\text{max}}$ and reaction delay $t_{\text{react}}$ (system, not human).

**Step 1 — Distance covered during reaction (constant velocity):**

$$
d_{\text{react}} \;=\; v_{\text{ego}} \cdot t_{\text{react}}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance covered during braking (kinematic, $v_f = 0$, initial $v = v_{\text{ego}}$, decel $a = a_{\text{max}}$).** Using $v_f^{2} = v_i^{2} + 2 a s$:

$$
0 \;=\; v_{\text{ego}}^{2} \;-\; 2\,a_{\text{max}}\,d_{\text{brake}}
$$

$$
d_{\text{brake}} \;=\; \dfrac{v_{\text{ego}}^{2}}{2\,a_{\text{max}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total stopping distance:**

$$
\boxed{\;d_{\text{stop}} \;=\; v_{\text{ego}}\,t_{\text{react}} \;+\; \dfrac{v_{\text{ego}}^{2}}{2\,a_{\text{max}}}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum safe gap:**
For no collision, $\Delta d_{\min} \ge d_{\text{stop}}$:

$$
\Delta d_{\min} \;=\; v_{\text{ego}}\,t_{\text{react}} \;+\; \dfrac{v_{\text{ego}}^{2}}{2\,a_{\text{max}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical sanity check:** At $v = 100$ km/h $\approx 27.78$ m/s, $t_{\text{react}} = 0.1$ s, $a_{\text{max}} = 8$ m/s²:
> $d_{\text{stop}} = 27.78(0.1) + \dfrac{27.78^{2}}{16} \approx 2.78 + 48.2 \approx 51.0$ m.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ACC Time-Headway Control Law

**Adaptive Cruise Control** maintains a driver-set speed $v_{\text{set}}$ *unless* a slower lead vehicle is detected, in which case it switches to **gap-control** mode with time-headway $t_h$.

**Step 1 — Desired gap:**

$$
d_{\text{des}} \;=\; d_0 \;+\; v_{\text{ego}}\,t_h
$$

where $d_0$ = standstill gap (≈ 2–5 m).

**Step 2 — Gap error:**

$$
e(t) \;=\; \Delta d(t) \;-\; d_{\text{des}}
$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal acceleration demand (PID-style simplified):**

$$
a_{\text{cmd}}(t) \;=\; K_p\,e(t) \;+\; K_d\,\dot{e}(t)
$$

where $\dot e = v_{\text{lead}} - v_{\text{ego}}$ (closing speed).

**Step 4 — Saturation limits:**

$$
\boxed{\;a_{\min} \;\le\; a_{\text{cmd}} \;\le\; a_{\max}\;}, \qquad a_{\min}\approx -3.5\ \text{m/s}^{2},\ a_{\max}\approx +2.0\ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

Beyond $\vert a_{\text{cmd}} \vert > 3.5$ m/s², AEB takes over (handoff logic).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Radar Sensor Maximum Unambiguous Range

For a **FMCW radar** with sweep bandwidth $B$ and sweep time $T_s$:

**Beat frequency** for a target at range $R$:

$$
f_b \;=\; \dfrac{2\,R\,B}{c\,T_s}
$$

**Maximum range** limited by receiver SNR and $f_{b,\max}$:

$$
\boxed{\;R_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{c\,T_s\,f_{b,\max}}{2\,B}\;}
$$

**Example:** $B = 150$ MHz, $T_s = 5$ ms, $f_{b,\max} = 5$ MHz:

$$
R_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{(3\times10^{8})(5\times10^{-3})(5\times10^{6})}{2(150\times10^{6})} \;=\; 25\ \text{m}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AEB vs ACC vs DMS

| Parameter | **ACC** | **AEB** | **DMS** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Trigger condition | Lead detected & $v_{\text{rel}} < 0$ slow | $t_{\text{TTC}} < t_{\text{th}}$ | Driver attention $S_{\text{att}} <$ threshold |
| Deceleration range | $\approx 0$ to $-3.5$ m/s² | $-3.5$ to $-9.0$ m/s² | n/a (informational) |
| Driver override | Yes (clutch/brake/steer) | Yes (until last 0.5 s) | n/a |
| Sensors | Radar + camera | Radar + camera + wheel speed | IR camera + steering torque |
| Failure-mode fallback | Warn + coast | Hazard + brake-hold + E-call | Escalating alerts → LKA-off |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS (MERMAID)

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **block-level functional architecture** representation (not physical sketches), which is the format KTU examiners accept for engineering schematics.

## 4.1 ADAS Functional Architecture — Sense → Think → Act

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Front Camera] --> B1[Perception ECU]
    A2[Front Radar 77GHz] --> B1
    A3[Corner Radars] --> B1
    A4[Lidar Unit] --> B1
    A5[Ultrasonic Array] --> B1
    A6[Driver Camera IR] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{Sensor Fusion Engine}
    C1 --> D1[Object Tracker]
    C1 --> D2[Lane Estimator]
    C1 --> D3[Driver State Estimator]
    D1 --> E1[Longitudinal Planner]
    D1 --> E2[Lateral Planner]
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> F1[DMS Module]
    E1 --> G1[Brake by Wire Actuator]
    E1 --> G2[Throttle Actuator]
    E2 --> G3[Electric Power Steering]
    F1 --> H1[HMI Cluster Display]
    G1 --> H2[Vehicle Motion Output]
    G2 --> H2
    G3 --> H2
```

## 4.2 ADAS Feature Classification by Longitudinal / Lateral / Driver Domain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[ADAS Feature Tree]
    Root --> Lon[Longitudinal Domain]
    Root --> Lat[Lateral Domain]
    Root --> Drv[Driver Domain]
    Lon --> Lon1[ACC]
    Lon --> Lon2[AEB]
    Lon --> Lon3[FCW]
    Lat --> Lat1[LDWS]
    Lat --> Lat2[LKA]
    Lat --> Lat3[LCA]
    Lat --> Lat4[BSD]
    Drv --> Drv1[DMS Attention]
    Drv --> Drv2[DMS Drowsiness]
    Drv --> Drv3[Hands On Detection]
```

## 4.3 AEB Decision & Activation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sensor reads gap and relative velocity] --> S2[Compute TTC]
    S2 --> Q1{TTC greater than 3.0 s}
    Q1 -- Yes --> N1[No Action Cruise maintained]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{TTC between 1.4 and 3.0 s}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A1[FCW Audible Visual Haptic]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{TTC less than 1.4 s}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A2[Brake Precharge]
    A2 --> Q4{TTC still decreasing}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A3[Full AEB Max Decel 9 m/s2]
    Q4 -- No --> N1
    A3 --> Q5{Driver override clutch or brake}
    Q5 -- Yes --> N1
    Q5 -- No --> A4[Vehicle Held Stopped]
```

## 4.4 SAE Level 0 to 5 Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[L0 No Automation Human Drives] --> L1[L1 Driver Assist Single Task]
    L1 --> L2[L2 Partial Auto ACC plus LKA]
    L2 --> L3[L3 Conditional Auto ODD Limited]
    L3 --> L4[L4 High Auto Geofenced No Driver]
    L4 --> L5[L5 Full Auto Any Condition]
```

## 4.5 Sensor Coverage Map (Top-Down Vehicle)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vehicle_Block[Top View of Vehicle]
        direction LR
        V[Vehicle] --- C1a[Front Camera FOV 50 deg]
        V --- C2a[Front Long Range Radar Beam 20 deg]
        V --- C3a[Front Lidar Scan 120 deg]
        V --- C4a[Left Rear Corner Radar 150 deg]
        V --- C5a[Right Rear Corner Radar 150 deg]
        V --- C6a[Rear View Camera 130 deg]
        V --- C7a[Twelve Ultrasonic Pods 360 deg]
        V --- C8a[Driver IR Camera Cabin]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading tip:** Each block above is mapped 1:1 with the sensor descriptions in Section 2. In an exam, drawing even a simplified version of 4.5 with arrows for coverage earns 2 bonus marks on a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define ADAS. List any four ADAS features that operate in the longitudinal domain with their primary purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) are electronic sub-systems that use onboard sensors and controllers to perceive the driving environment and either warn the driver or automatically intervene to improve vehicle safety and comfort, without fully replacing the human driver (SAE Level 0–2).

**Four longitudinal-domain ADAS features (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** – Maintains driver-set speed and automatically adjusts to keep a safe time-headway gap from a slower lead vehicle.
2. **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** – Applies maximum brake pressure autonomously when an imminent collision is detected (low TTC).
3. **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** – Provides audible/visual/haptic warning before a likely rear-end collision.
4. **Traffic Jam Assist (TJA)** – Combines ACC + Stop-&-Go functionality for low-speed congested traffic.

*(Examiner's note: Full forms and one-line purpose is sufficient — no derivation needed at this level.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Explain the difference between SAE Level 2 and Level 3 automation with one suitable real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **Level 2 — Partial Automation** | **Level 3 — Conditional Automation** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Driving responsibility | Human driver (must monitor) | System (within ODD) |
| Driver attention | Required continuously | Can disengage eyes-off-road temporarily |
| Example | **Tesla Autopilot** (Basic) — ACC + LKA on highways | **Mercedes Drive Pilot** — system drives up to 60 km/h on mapped German Autobahns |
| Takeover time | Immediate | 10-second transition warning |

*(1.5 marks for distinction + 1.5 marks for examples with correct real-world names.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)
*Internal choice: answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.*

---

### **Question A (14 marks) — *Adaptive Cruise Control & AEB***

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled functional block diagram, describe the **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** system. Clearly distinguish between *speed-control mode* and *gap-control mode*. Mention the sensors used and the typical deceleration limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle equipped with **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** is travelling at $v = 90$ km/h on a dry road. The system reaction delay is $t_r = 0.1$ s and maximum achievable deceleration is $a_{\max} = 7.5\ \text{m/s}^2$. The lead vehicle suddenly brakes and presents a closing speed $v_{\text{rel}} = 20$ km/h at a current bumper-gap of $\Delta d = 35$ m. **(7 marks)**

Determine:
1. The current **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**.
2. The **minimum stopping distance** the ego vehicle requires.
3. Whether AEB intervention is **necessary** (justify with calculation).
4. If yes, what minimum deceleration must be commanded to **just avoid** collision (use $d_{\min} = \Delta d$).

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — ACC Functional Description (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Diagram (2 marks) + Speed vs Gap mode (2 marks) + Sensors (1.5 marks) + Limits (1.5 marks).

**Block diagram (write/draw this):**

$$
\text{Front Radar + Camera} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Target Selection} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Gap \& TTC Estimator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Longitudinal Controller} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Throttle / Brake actuator}
$$

- **Speed-control mode (CC mode):** No lead vehicle in sensor envelope → ECU commands throttle to maintain driver-set $v_{\text{set}}$; brakes only on driver input.
- **Gap-control mode:** Lead vehicle detected within ~250 m → ECU switches to a closed-loop that adjusts acceleration to maintain a *time-headway* gap $t_h$ (typically 1.5–2.0 s):
  $d_{\text{des}} = d_0 + v\,t_h$ where $d_0 \approx 2$–5 m.
- **Sensors:** 77 GHz FMCW long-range radar (range + closing speed), forward monocular camera (target classification), wheel-speed sensors (ego speed), yaw-rate sensor (curve adaptation).
- **Deceleration limits:**
  - Comfort braking cap: ~ $-3.5\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ (driver still must supervise, Level 2).
  - Beyond $-3.5\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ → **automatic escalation to AEB**.
  - Maximum brake system capability: ~ $-9.0\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ (dry asphalt).

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall callout (read before solving):** Convert **km/h → m/s** before any arithmetic — this is where 90% of KTU students lose 1–2 marks.

**Step 1 — Unit conversion.**

$$
v_{\text{ego}} = 90\ \text{km/h} = \dfrac{90}{3.6} = 25.0\ \text{m/s}, \qquad v_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{20}{3.6} = 5.56\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Current TTC** (Equation 1 from cheat sheet):

$$
t_{\text{TTC}} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{\text{rel}}} = \dfrac{35}{5.56} \approx 6.30\ \text{s}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — *No immediate AEB required at TTC > 3.0 s.*

**Step 3 — Minimum stopping distance at $a_{\max}=7.5$ m/s²:**

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = v_{\text{ego}}\,t_r + \dfrac{v_{\text{ego}}^{2}}{2\,a_{\max}} = 25.0 \times 0.1 + \dfrac{25.0^{2}}{2 \times 7.5}
$$

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = 2.5 + \dfrac{625}{15} = 2.5 + 41.67 = 44.17\ \text{m}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Decision (AEB necessary?)**

Since $d_{\text{stop}} = 44.17\ \text{m} > \Delta d = 35\ \text{m}$, **the ego cannot stop in time with $a_{\max} = 7.5$ m/s² → AEB intervention IS necessary.** **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Required deceleration for *just avoiding* collision** (set $d_{\text{stop}} = \Delta d$):

$$
\Delta d \;=\; v_{\text{ego}}\,t_r \;+\; \dfrac{v_{\text{ego}}^{2}}{2\,a_{\text{req}}}
$$

$$
35 \;=\; 2.5 \;+\; \dfrac{625}{2\,a_{\text{req}}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{625}{2\,a_{\text{req}}} = 32.5 \;\Longrightarrow\; a_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{625}{65} \approx 9.62\ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

**(2 marks)** — *This is higher than the system's 7.5 m/s² capability, meaning the collision is unavoidable unless AEB works in tandem with steering evasive action or the lead vehicle decelerates too.*

**Final Answer Box:**

$$
\boxed{\text{TTC} = 6.30\ \text{s},\quad d_{\text{stop}} = 44.17\ \text{m},\quad a_{\text{req}} = 9.62\ \text{m/s}^{2}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — *LDWS, LKA, LCA & DMS***

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of the **Lane Departure Warning System (LDWS)** and **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)**. State the sensors, actuators and the difference between them with a labelled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat functional diagram, describe the **Driver Monitoring System (DMS)**. List the key driver-state parameters it measures, the escalation logic for warnings, and explain why DMS is now mandatory under Euro NCAP 2025+ protocols. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — LDWS & LKA (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Working principle (2) + Diagram (2) + Sensors/actuators (1.5) + LDWS vs LKA table (1.5).

**Working Principle of LDWS:**
- A forward-facing CMOS camera (mounted near the rear-view mirror) captures the road ahead at 30–60 fps.
- Image-processing algorithms (edge detection, Hough transform) identify the **left and right lane markings**.
- The system computes **lateral position** of the vehicle relative to lane centre and **time-to-line-crossing (TLC)**.
- If the driver crosses a lane line **without using the indicator** and TLC < ~0.5 s → warning is triggered.

**Working Principle of LKA:**
- Uses the **same lane-detection pipeline** as LDWS but additionally commands the **Electric Power Steering (EPS)** to apply a gentle correcting torque (typically 1–3 N·m) towards lane centre.
- It is *continuous* (not a one-shot warning) and operates at speeds > 60 km/h typically.

**Block diagram:**

$$
\text{Windshield Camera} \;\to\; \text{Lane Detection (CNN/Hough)} \;\to\; \text{TLC \& Lateral Error} \;\to\; \text{Decision (warn or correct)} \;\to\; \text{EPS / HMI}
$$

| Aspect | **LDWS** | **LKA** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Action | Warn (haptic/audible) | Act (steer) |
| Actuator | Steering wheel vibration motor + buzzer | EPS motor torque |
| Driver override | Always | Yes (counter-steer > 5 N·m) |
| SAE level | 0 (informational) | 1 (lateral automation) |

**Lane Change Assist (LCA):**
- Uses **two rear-corner radars** (24 GHz SRR, ~80 m range) + **rear side cameras** to monitor blind spots.
- If the driver initiates a lane change (indicator ON) and a vehicle is detected in the adjacent lane within ~30 m → warning OR auto-counter-steer (in higher-end L2+ systems).

---

### Part (b) — Driver Monitoring System (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Diagram (2) + Parameters (2) + Escalation logic (1.5) + Euro NCAP rationale (1.5).

**Functional Diagram:**

$$
\text{IR Camera (Driver-Facing)} \;\to\; \text{Face Landmark Detection} \;\to\; \text{Eye Aspect Ratio, Head Pose, Gaze Vector} \;\to\; \text{Attention Classifier} \;\to\; \text{Escalation Logic} \;\to\; \text{HMI + ADAS Interlocks}
$$

**Key driver-state parameters measured:**

1. **Eye Aspect Ratio (EAR)** — measures blink duration & PERCLOS (Percentage of Eyelid Closure).
2. **Head Pose Estimation** — pitch, yaw, roll angles (looking away > 4 s).
3. **Gaze Direction** — whether driver is looking at road, mirrors, or phone.
4. **Hand-on-wheel detection** — capacitive steering wheel or torque sensor.
5. **Yawning / Micro-sleep detection** — via mouth landmark CNN.

**Escalation Logic (typical):**

| Level | Condition | Action |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| L1 | EAR drops, short gaze off-road | Audible chime |
| L2 | Eyes off-road > 2 s | Visual + steering vibration |
| L3 | No driver attention > 4 s | Haptic seat + ACC/LKA disengage imminent |
| L4 | > 6 s no response | LKA OFF, hazard lights ON, safe-stop initiated |

**Why DMS is mandatory (Euro NCAP 2025+):**

- 23% of fatal crashes in EU involve driver distraction/drowsiness.
- DMS complements AEB & LDW — even perfect sensors cannot prevent a crash if the **human supervisor (Level 2) is asleep**.
- Euro NCAP awards **bonus points** only if DMS is present and actively escalates.
- EU GSR (General Safety Regulation) makes DMS **legally mandatory** for all new cars from July 2024 (M1/N1 classes).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most-commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing km/h with m/s in TTC / stopping-distance problems. Always convert to m/s first. **(−2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Forgetting reaction time** — Using only $v^2/(2a)$ and skipping $v\cdot t_r$. AEB requires *both* terms. **(−1.5 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing LDWS vs LKA** — LDWS only *warns*; LKA *acts* on EPS. Many answers swap these. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Wrong SAE level examples** — Calling Tesla Autopilot "Level 3" or "self-driving" — it is Level 2. Only Mercedes Drive Pilot (Germany, 2022) and Honda Legend (Japan, 2021) are certified Level 3 commercially.
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram** — A 14-mark answer without a diagram loses at least 2 marks. Always include *Sense → Think → Act* block.
> 6. **Skipping ODD definition** — When defining SAE L3/L4, examiners expect mention of "Operational Design Domain".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this before entering the exam hall.** ✔ marks mean "I can answer this cold."

- ✔ ADAS = **Sense → Think → Act**; classification: longitudinal / lateral / driver-domain.
- ✔ **Sensors:** Camera (classification, lanes), Radar (range+velocity, all-weather), Lidar (3-D, expensive), Ultrasonic (parking).
- ✔ **SAE Levels 0–5** with responsibility matrix; **ODD** is the trigger keyword.
- ✔ **LDWS** = warn; **LKA** = act via EPS; **LCA** = uses corner radar + indicator intent.
- ✔ **ACC** = speed-control OR gap-control (time-headway $t_h \approx 1.5$–2.0 s); saturation $\vert a \vert \le 3.5$ m/s².
- ✔ **AEB** activates below $t_{\text{TTC}} \approx 1.4$ s; max decel ~9 m/s²; cannot be overridden in last 0.5 s.
- ✔ **DMS** = IR driver-facing camera; measures EAR, head pose, gaze, hands-on-wheel; 4-level escalation.
- ✔ **Master formulas:**
  $t_{\text{TTC}} = \Delta d / v_{\text{rel}}$,
  $d_{\text{stop}} = v t_r + v^{2}/(2a)$,
  $d_{\text{safe}} = v t_h$,
  $R_{\text{radar}} = c \Delta t / 2$,
  $v_{\text{rel}} = c f_D / (2 f_0)$.
- ✔ Always draw a **functional block diagram** in any ADAS answer (even a 3-mark one).
- ✔ Real-world Level-3 examples: **Mercedes Drive Pilot** (Germany), **Honda Legend** (Japan).
- ✔ **Euro NCAP 2025+ & EU GSR July 2024** mandate AEB + LDW + DMS for 5★ rating.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET742 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Autonomous Vehicles # MODULE 4 — AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / SAE J3016 Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (SAE J3016 Standard, KTU 2024 Aligned):** > An **Autonomous Vehicle (AV)** — also termed a *Self-Driving Vehicle* or *Driverless Car* — is a motorized ground vehicle that integrates an **Intelligent Control System** capable of **dynamically executing the entire Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)** with sustained execution and without human intervention, by continuously perceiving its surroundings through on-board **exteroceptive and proprioceptive sensors**, localizing itself on a high-definition (HD) map, predicting the trajectory of dynamic agents, planning a collision-free path, and actuating the steering, throttle, and braking subsystems in real time. In strict KTU-syllabus terms, the **Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)** comprises all the real-time operational and tactical functions required to operate a vehicle in on-road traffic, **excluding** the strategic functions such as trip scheduling and destination selection (which remain with the human or the fleet operator). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sustained Execution vs. Momentary Intervention:** > The defining feature of an autonomous vehicle is **sustained, end-to-end execution** of the DDT — not merely momentary corrective action (e.g., AEB or Lane Keep Assist). This distinction is what separates SAE Level 3+ systems from Level 1–2 Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot Taxi Driver" Imagine you board a taxi, but instead of a human driver, you find a **highly attentive, never-tired, never-distracted chauffeur** sitting at the wheel. This chauffeur has: | Human Trait | Autonomous Equivalent | |---|---| | Eyes (see road, signs, pedestrians) | **Camera + LiDAR + Radar sensors** | | Ears (hear sirens, horns) | **Microphone arrays + V2X receivers** | | Brain (decision making) | **AI / Deep Learning processor (NVIDIA Drive, Mobileye EyeQ)** | | Hands & Feet (steering, brake, accelerator) | **Drive-by-wire actuators (EPS, EBB, electronic throttle)** | | Internal balance & sense of speed | **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) + Wheel Odometry** | | Knowledge of city roads | **HD Maps + GPS / GNSS positioning** | The chauffeur **continuously senses → thinks → acts** in a millisecond loop. This "Sense–Plan–Act" triad is the philosophical heart of every autonomous vehicle, and the engineering reality behind every Tesla FSD, Waymo, and Cruise deployment. --- ### 1.3 SAE J3016 — The Six Levels of Driving Automation The **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) J3016 standard** is the globally accepted taxonomy referenced by KTU. It defines **six levels** from **Level 0 (No Automation)** to **Level 5 (Full Automation)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** A 3-mark question is frequently asked asking students to **list or distinguish** the SAE levels. Memorize the *driver responsibility boundary* at each level. | Level | Name | Who does what? | Example Systems | |:---:|---|---|---| | **0** | No Automation | Human driver does everything. | Conventional cars (pre-2000). | | **1** | Driver Assistance | System assists with **either** steering **or** acceleration/braking. | Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), Lane Keeping (basic). | | **2** | Partial Automation | System controls steering **and** acceleration/braking, but **human must monitor**. | Tesla Autopilot (basic), GM Super Cruise (legacy). | | **3** | Conditional Automation | System performs the entire DDT under specific conditions; **human must be ready to intervene**. | Honda Legend (Traffic Jam Pilot), Mercedes Drive Pilot. | | **4** | High Automation | System performs the entire DDT within a defined **Operational Design Domain (ODD)**; no human needed inside ODD. | Waymo One (robotaxi), Baidu Apollo Go (geofenced). | | **5** | Full Automation | System performs the entire DDT **everywhere, under all conditions**; no steering wheel required. | Conceptual only — no commercial deployment yet. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Boundary Markers for Exam Answers:** > - **L1 → L2 transition:** Longitudinal **AND** lateral control both automated. > - **L2 → L3 transition:** Human is no longer required to monitor (only fallback-ready). > - **L3 → L4 transition:** No human fallback needed **within ODD**. > - **L4 → L5 transition:** ODD becomes **unlimited** (all weather, all roads). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architecture — The Sense → Plan → Act Triad Every autonomous vehicle — whether a SAE Level 3 highway pilot or a Level 4 robotaxi — follows the canonical three-layer architecture. KTU examiners expect students to name these layers and explain the data flow between them. #### Layer 1 — Perception (Sense) Fuses raw data from heterogeneous sensors into a unified, labeled model of the world. #### Layer 2 — Planning & Decision Making (Plan) Decides *where to go next* (global route), *how to get there safely* (behavioral), and *exactly what steering angle and pedal position* to command (local trajectory). #### Layer 3 — Control & Actuation (Act) Translates planned trajectories into physical commands sent to drive-by-wire subsystems. --- ### 2.2 The Sensor Stack — "Eyes and Ears" of an AV No single sensor is sufficient. Each modality has strengths and weaknesses; the **sensor fusion** approach is mandatory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **LiDAR vs Camera vs Radar — KTU Comparison:** > KTU frequently asks students to compare sensors. Use the *weather resilience, range, resolution, cost* axis. | Sensor | Operating Principle | Range (typical) | Strengths | Weaknesses | |---|---|:---:|---|---| | **Camera (Mono/Stereo)** | CMOS image sensor, visible/IR light | 0 – 250 m | Excellent **semantic classification** (reads signs, detects color, lane markings); low cost. | Degrades in fog/rain/darkness; **no direct depth**. | | **LiDAR (Light Detection And Ranging)** | Time-of-flight of laser pulses (905 nm / 1550 nm) | 100 – 250 m | **High-resolution 3D point cloud**; cm-level accuracy; works at night. | Expensive; degraded in heavy rain/snow; no color info. | | **Radar (Radio Detection And Ranging)** | FMCW at 77 GHz (automotive) | 0 – 250 m | All-weather operation; **direct velocity** via Doppler; long range. | Low spatial resolution; poor at classifying pedestrians vs poles. | | **Ultrasonic** | Time-of-flight of 40–70 kHz acoustic pulses | 0.1 – 5 m | Extremely cheap; ideal for **parking / low-speed maneuvering**. | Very short range; useless above 30 km/h. | | **GPS / GNSS (RTK)** | Satellite trilateration | Global | Absolute global position. | ±2–5 m accuracy alone; needs RTK for cm-level. | | **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** | MEMS gyroscopes + accelerometers | N/A (local) | High-frequency motion data (>200 Hz); dead-reckoning. | **Drifts** without external correction. | | **HD Map** | Pre-surveyed centimeter-accurate map | N/A | Provides prior knowledge of lanes, signs, curbs. | Requires continual maintenance; fails on unmapped roads. | --- ### 2.3 Control Architecture — Mathematical Foundation The **kinematic bicycle model** is the standard simplified model used in AV path planning and control. It treats the front and rear axles as single wheels. **State vector:** $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, \psi, v)^{\mathsf{T}}$ Where: - $(x, y)$ — position of the rear axle in the global frame. - $\psi$ — yaw (heading) angle. - $v$ — longitudinal velocity at the rear axle. **Kinematic Bicycle Model Equations:** $$\dot{x} = v \cdot \cos(\psi)$$ $$\dot{y} = v \cdot \sin(\psi)$$ $$\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L} \cdot \tan(\delta)$$ $$\dot{v} = a$$ Where: - $L$ — wheelbase (m). - $\delta$ — steering angle of the front wheel (rad). - $a$ — longitudinal acceleration (m/s²). > [!NOTE] > **Why "bicycle"?** A real car has 4 wheels, but because steering geometry is symmetric, the inner and outer wheels on each axle can be lumped into a single equivalent wheel — leaving exactly **two wheels** (front and rear), like a bicycle. This simplifies the geometry dramatically. --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table — it is the single most exam-relevant cheat-sheet for this module.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |:---:|---|---|---| | 1 | **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** | $\mathrm{TTC} = \dfrac{d_{\mathrm{rel}}}{v_{\mathrm{rel}}}$ | $d_{\mathrm{rel}}$ = relative distance (m); $v_{\mathrm{rel}}$ = relative velocity (m/s). Critical for AEB. | | 2 | **Total Stopping Distance** | $d_{\mathrm{stop}} = v \cdot t_{\mathrm{r}} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \, \mu \, g}$ | $t_{\mathrm{r}}$ = reaction time (s); $\mu$ = tire-road friction; $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**. | | 3 | **Braking Deceleration Limit** | $a_{\max} = \mu \cdot g$ | Maximum achievable deceleration on dry asphalt: $\mu \approx 0.8$. | | 4 | **LiDAR Range Equation** | $R_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{\mathrm{t}} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{P_{\mathrm{min}} \cdot (4\pi)^{3}}}$ | Radar/LiDAR link budget; $P_{\mathrm{t}}$ = transmit power, $G$ = antenna gain, $\sigma$ = target RCS, $\lambda$ = wavelength. | | 5 | **Camera Pinhole Projection** | $u = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad v = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $(X,Y,Z)$ in camera frame; $(u,v)$ in image plane; $f$ = focal length. | | 6 | **Radar Doppler Velocity** | $v_{\mathrm{r}} = \dfrac{\Delta f \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | FMCW radar measures $\Delta f$ directly. | | 7 | **Pure-Pursuit Steering** | $\delta(t) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2 L \sin(\alpha(t))}{l_{\mathrm{d}}}\right)$ | $l_{\mathrm{d}}$ = lookahead distance; $\alpha$ = heading error to look-ahead point. | | 8 | **Stanley Steering Control** | $\delta(t) = \psi(t) + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k \cdot e(t)}{v(t)}\right)$ | $k$ = gain; $e(t)$ = cross-track error. | | 9 | **Localization Covariance Update** (EKF) | $P_{k} = (I - K_{k} H) P_{k}^{-1}$ | Extended Kalman Filter uncertainty propagation. | | 10 | **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** | Set of operating conditions (geography, weather, time, speed) | SAE Level 3/4 systems **only** operate within ODD. | | 11 | **Wheel Odometry** | $\Delta s = r_{\mathrm{w}} \cdot \Delta \theta$ | $r_{\mathrm{w}}$ = effective wheel radius; $\Delta \theta$ = wheel rotation. | | 12 | **Sensor Fusion (Kalman)** | $\hat{x}_{k} = \hat{x}_{k}^{-} + K_{k}(z_{k} - H\hat{x}_{k}^{-})$ | $K_{k}$ = Kalman gain; $z_{k}$ = measurement. | > [!WARNING] > **Vertical Pipe Rule:** In the table above, notice that absolute values are written as $\vert \Delta f \vert$ implicitly using `\vert` semantics inside fractions — **never use the literal `|` character inside a markdown table**, as it breaks rendering. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Total Stopping Distance (with AV Relevance) The stopping distance of a vehicle is the sum of (i) the **reaction distance** (during perception and decision delay) and (ii) the **braking distance** (during physical deceleration). This derivation is foundational because an autonomous vehicle's **planning layer** must compute stopping distance in real time to ensure a collision-free trajectory. **Step 1 — Reaction Distance.** During the *driver reaction time* $t_{\mathrm{r}}$, the vehicle continues at constant velocity $v$. The distance covered is: $$d_{\mathrm{react}} = v \cdot t_{\mathrm{r}}$$ For a human, $t_{\mathrm{r}} \approx 1.5$ s; for an AV, the perception-to-actuation latency is typically $t_{\mathrm{r}} \approx 0.05$–$0.15$ s — roughly an order of magnitude faster. **Step 2 — Braking Distance (Kinematic).** Once brakes are applied, deceleration $a$ (acting opposite to motion) reduces velocity from $v$ to $0$. Using the kinematic relation: $$v_{\mathrm{f}}^{2} = v_{\mathrm{i}}^{2} + 2 a \, d_{\mathrm{brake}}$$ Setting $v_{\mathrm{f}} = 0$, $v_{\mathrm{i}} = v$, and $a = -\mu g$ (negative because it opposes motion): $$0 = v^{2} - 2 \mu g \cdot d_{\mathrm{brake}}$$ Solving for $d_{\mathrm{brake}}$: $$d_{\mathrm{brake}} = \frac{v^{2}}{2 \, \mu \, g}$$ **Step 3 — Total Stopping Distance (sum).** $$\boxed{\, d_{\mathrm{stop}} = v \, t_{\mathrm{r}} + \frac{v^{2}}{2 \, \mu \, g} \,}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example.** A Level-4 AV travelling at $v = 30$ m/s ($\approx 108$ km/h) on dry asphalt ($\mu = 0.8$) with $t_{\mathrm{r}} = 0.1$ s: $$d_{\mathrm{stop}} = 30 \cdot 0.1 + \frac{30^{2}}{2 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 9.81}$$ $$d_{\mathrm{stop}} = 3.0 + \frac{900}{15.696} \approx 3.0 + 57.3 \approx 60.3 \text{ m}$$ For comparison, a human-driven car at the same speed with $t_{\mathrm{r}} = 1.5$ s: $$d_{\mathrm{stop,human}} = 30 \cdot 1.5 + 57.3 = 45.0 + 57.3 = 102.3 \text{ m}$$ This $40\%$ reduction is one of the central **safety arguments** for AV deployment. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Time-to-Collision (TTC) — The AEB Trigger TTC is the predicted time until two agents collide if they continue their current velocities and headings. The AV's **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** system triggers when TTC falls below a calibrated threshold (typically $\tau_{\mathrm{AEB}} \approx 1.4$ s for stationary targets). **Step 1 — Define relative state.** Two agents, ego (E) and target (T), with positions $\mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{E}}, \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{T}}$ and velocities $\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{E}}, \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{T}}$. **Step 2 — Compute relative distance vector.** $$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{T}} - \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{E}}, \quad d = \vert \mathbf{d} \vert$$ **Step 3 — Compute relative velocity vector.** $$\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{rel}} = \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{T}} - \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{E}}$$ **Step 4 — Project relative velocity onto relative distance direction.** The closing speed $v_{\mathrm{clos}}$ is the component of $\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{rel}}$ along the line connecting the two agents: $$v_{\mathrm{clos}} = -\frac{\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{rel}}}{\vert \mathbf{d} \vert}$$ **Step 5 — TTC is closing distance / closing rate.** $$\boxed{\, \mathrm{TTC} = \frac{d}{v_{\mathrm{clos}}} = -\frac{\vert \mathbf{d} \vert^{2}}{\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{rel}}} \,}$$ The negative sign ensures TTC is positive when agents are closing (relative velocity points toward ego). **Step 6 — Special case: 1-D rear-end scenario.** For a follower's radar with $d$ = inter-vehicle gap and $v_{\mathrm{rel}} = v_{\mathrm{leader}} - v_{\mathrm{ego}}$: $$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{d}{v_{\mathrm{ego}} - v_{\mathrm{leader}}}$$ For $d = 30$ m, $v_{\mathrm{ego}} = 25$ m/s, $v_{\mathrm{leader}} = 20$ m/s: $$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{30}{25 - 20} = 6.0 \text{ s}$$ Since $6.0 > 1.4$ s, AEB does **not** trigger — the AV only **alerts** or pre-charges the brakes. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Kalman Filter Prediction Step (for Localization) An AV localizes itself by fusing GPS, IMU, and LiDAR scan-matching. The Kalman Filter provides the optimal recursive estimate under Gaussian noise. **Step 1 — Define state transition model.** $$\mathbf{x}_{k} = F_{k} \, \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + B_{k} \, \mathbf{u}_{k} + \mathbf{w}_{k}$$ Where $F_{k}$ is the state transition matrix, $B_{k}$ the control input matrix, $\mathbf{u}_{k}$ the control vector (IMU acceleration, yaw rate), and $\mathbf{w}_{k} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_{k})$ process noise. **Step 2 — Predict state mean.** $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k}^{-} = F_{k} \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1} + B_{k} \, \mathbf{u}_{k}$$ **Step 3 — Predict covariance.** $$P_{k}^{-} = F_{k} \, P_{k-1} \, F_{k}^{\mathsf{T}} + Q_{k}$$ **Step 4 — Measurement update (when GPS arrives).** $$K_{k} = P_{k}^{-} \, H^{\mathsf{T}} \, (H \, P_{k}^{-} \, H^{\mathsf{T}} + R_{k})^{-1}$$ $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k}^{-} + K_{k} \, (\mathbf{z}_{k} - H \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k}^{-})$$ $$P_{k} = (I - K_{k} H) \, P_{k}^{-}$$ The Kalman gain $K_{k}$ adaptively weights GPS measurement noise ($R_{k}$) versus IMU dead-reckoning drift ($Q_{k}$) — a mathematical embodiment of "trust the more reliable source." > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters for AVs:** Pure GPS drifts; pure IMU drifts faster. Kalman fusion achieves centimeter-accurate localization, which is **mandatory** for lane-keeping on highways at Level 3+. ## 4.
