KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > Define the term *Autonomous Vehicle*. List the **SAE J3016 Levels of Driving Automation** and identify the level at which the system is capable of performing the **entire Dynamic Driving Task (DDT) without any human intervention within a defined Operational Design Domain (ODD)**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** An Autonomous Vehicle is a vehicle equipped with sensors, actuators, processors, and software that enable it to perceive its environment, make driving decisions, and execute the entire Dynamic Driving Task (DDT) without human input. **Six SAE Levels (1 mark):** - Level 0 — No Automation - Level 1 — Driver Assistance - Level 2 — Partial Automation - Level 3 — Conditional Automation - Level 4 — High Automation - Level 5 — Full Automation **Specific Level (1 mark):** **Level 4 — High Automation.** At Level 4, the AV performs the entire DDT within a defined Operational Design Domain (e.g., a geofenced urban area, daylight clear weather, speeds ≤ 65 km/h), and no human driver is required within the ODD. If the vehicle exits the ODD, it performs a *Minimum Risk Maneuver* (safe stop). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** > Compare **LiDAR, Radar, and Camera** as perception sensors for autonomous vehicles. Mention **one key advantage** and **one key limitation** of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Sensor | One Key Advantage | One Key Limitation | |---|---|---| | **LiDAR** (1 mark) | Provides accurate, high-resolution **3D point cloud** of surroundings; works in darkness. | **Expensive**; performance degrades in heavy rain, fog, and snow. | | **Radar** (1 mark) | **All-weather** operation; directly measures **relative velocity** via Doppler shift. | Poor spatial resolution; cannot reliably classify object type (e.g., pedestrian vs pole). | | **Camera** (1 mark) | **Semantic understanding** (reads traffic signs, signal colors, lane markings); lowest cost. | Performance **degrades in low light/fog**; no direct depth measurement from a single lens. | --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Pattern]** > **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an Autonomous Vehicle**, clearly explaining the three layers: **Perception, Planning, and Control**. List the major sensors and actuators used at each layer. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **SAE J3016 Levels of Automation** (Levels 0 to 5) with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** in distinguishing Levels 3 and 4. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Functional Architecture of an Autonomous Vehicle (7 marks)** The architecture of an AV is conventionally organized into three layers that form the **Sense–Plan–Act** triad. **(i) Perception Layer (2 marks):** This layer fuses raw sensor data into a labeled model of the world. - **Sensors used:** - **Cameras** (front, surround, rear) — lane, sign, and traffic-light detection. - **LiDAR** — 3D point cloud generation, object detection up to 250 m. - **Radar** — long-range (250 m) all-weather object and velocity detection. - **Ultrasonic** — short-range (< 5 m) parking assistance. - **GPS / GNSS + IMU** — global position and inertial motion. - **Output:** Object list (bounding boxes, class labels, velocities) + Ego pose + Occupancy grid. **(ii) Planning Layer (3 marks):** Divided into three sub-layers. - **Global Planner** — computes a route from origin to destination using the HD Map (e.g., Dijkstra's / A\* algorithm). - **Behavioral Planner** — a Finite State Machine that decides *what to do* (lane change? overtake? yield? stop?). Inputs include traffic rules, predictions of other agents, and the ODD. - **Local Planner** — generates a collision-free trajectory over the next 5–10 seconds using algorithms like **RRT\*** (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) or **optimization-based** methods (Model Predictive Control). **(iii) Control Layer (2 marks):** Converts the planned trajectory into actuator commands. - **Longitudinal controller** — regulates throttle and brake to track desired velocity. - **Lateral controller** — commands steering angle using algorithms such as **Pure Pursuit** or **Stanley Controller**. - **Actuators:** Electronic Throttle Control (ETC), Electronic Brake Booster (EBB), Electric Power Steering (EPS). > **[Suggested Block Diagram (1 mark):]** Show three boxes labelled Perception, Planning, Control with arrows flowing from Sensors → Perception → Planning → Control → Actuators → Vehicle Dynamics → back to Sensors (closing the loop). (See Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical Mermaid version.) --- **(b) SAE J3016 Levels of Automation and ODD (7 marks)** **(i) The Six Levels (4 marks):** | Level | Name | Driver / System Responsibility | |:---:|---|---| | 0 | No Automation | Driver performs all driving tasks. | | 1 | Driver Assistance | System controls **either** longitudinal **or** lateral motion. | | 2 | Partial Automation | System controls **both** longitudinal and lateral motion; driver monitors. | | 3 | Conditional Automation | System performs entire DDT; driver is fallback-ready. | | 4 | High Automation | System performs entire DDT within ODD; no driver needed inside ODD. | | 5 | Full Automation | System performs entire DDT under all conditions; no steering wheel. | **Examples (any one each, 1 mark):** - Level 1 — Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). - Level 2 — Tesla Autopilot (basic). - Level 3 — Honda Legend Traffic Jam Pilot. - Level 4 — Waymo One robotaxi (in geofenced Phoenix, USA). **(ii) Role of the Operational Design Domain (ODD) (3 marks):** The **ODD** is a precisely defined set of operating conditions under which an automated driving system is designed to function. It typically includes: - **Geographic boundaries** (e.g., geo-fenced urban area). - **Weather conditions** (e.g., clear, light rain only). - **Time of day** (daylight / well-lit night). - **Speed range** (e.g., 0 – 65 km/h). - **Road type** (e.g., mapped, lane-marked roads). **Why ODD matters (2 marks):** - At **Level 3**, the ODD may be broad (e.g., "any German Autobahn at < 130 km/h") but the system **must hand back control** with a Transition Demand if it nears ODD exit. The driver must be ready. - At **Level 4**, the system is responsible for a **Minimum Risk Maneuver** (safe stop) if the ODD ends. The driver can be asleep or absent inside the ODD. - Hence, **ODD is the precise technical boundary that distinguishes Level 3 from Level 4.** --- ### **Question B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Pattern]** > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **total stopping distance** of an autonomous vehicle. Compare the stopping distance of an AV (perception-to-actuation latency 0.1 s) and a human-driven vehicle (reaction time 1.5 s) at 30 m/s on dry asphalt ($\mu = 0.8$). **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the concept of **Time-to-Collision (TTC)**. A vehicle is following another at a distance of 30 m. Both are moving in the same direction with speeds 25 m/s (ego) and 20 m/s (leader). Determine the TTC and state whether **AEB (Automatic Emergency Braking)** should trigger, given the AEB threshold $\tau_{\mathrm{AEB}} = 1.4$ s. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Total Stopping Distance Derivation (7 marks)** The total stopping distance consists of two components: **Step 1 — Reaction Distance (1 mark):** During the perception-to-actuation delay $t_{\mathrm{r}}$, the vehicle continues at constant velocity $v$: $$d_{\mathrm{react}} = v \cdot t_{\mathrm{r}}$$ **Step 2 — Braking Distance (2 marks):** Once brakes are applied, deceleration $a = \mu g$ (opposing motion) reduces velocity from $v$ to $0$. Using kinematics: $$v_{\mathrm{f}}^{2} = v_{\mathrm{i}}^{2} - 2 \mu g \cdot d_{\mathrm{brake}}$$ Setting $v_{\mathrm{f}} = 0$: $$d_{\mathrm{brake}} = \frac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$$ **Step 3 — Total Stopping Distance (1 mark):** $$\boxed{d_{\mathrm{stop}} = v \, t_{\mathrm{r}} + \frac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Comparison (3 marks):** For $v = 30$ m/s, $\mu = 0.8$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²: *AV ($t_{\mathrm{r}} = 0.1$ s):* $$d_{\mathrm{stop,AV}} = 30 \cdot 0.1 + \frac{900}{2 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 9.81} = 3.0 + 57.3 \approx 60.3 \text{ m}$$ *Human ($t_{\mathrm{r}} = 1.5$ s):* $$d_{\mathrm{stop,human}} = 30 \cdot 1.5 + 57.3 = 45.0 + 57.3 \approx 102.3 \text{ m}$$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** The AV stops in roughly **40% less distance** than a human-driven car, demonstrating the safety potential of automation. --- **(b) Time-to-Collision (TTC) and AEB Trigger (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** TTC is the predicted time until a collision would occur if both the ego vehicle and the target agent continue at their current velocities and headings. It is the foundation of **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** decision logic. **Step 2 — 1-D Rear-End Formula (2 marks):** For two vehicles moving along the same line: $$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{d}{v_{\mathrm{ego}} - v_{\mathrm{leader}}}$$ where $d$ is the inter-vehicle gap and the numerator is positive only when the ego is faster (closing). **Step 3 — Substitution (2 marks):** Given $d = 30$ m, $v_{\mathrm{ego}} = 25$ m/s, $v_{\mathrm{leader}} = 20$ m/s: $$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{30}{25 - 20} = \frac{30}{5} = 6.0 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4 — AEB Decision (1 mark):** Since $\mathrm{TTC} = 6.0 \text{ s} > \tau_{\mathrm{AEB}} = 1.4 \text{ s}$, the AEB system **does not trigger full emergency braking**. Instead, the AV may issue a **forward-collision warning** to occupants, pre-charge the brake hydraulic system, and reduce cruise speed. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign error in TTC:** Many students write $\mathrm{TTC} = \dfrac{v_{\mathrm{ego}} - v_{\mathrm{leader}}}{d}$ — this is **inverted** and will fetch **zero marks**. Remember: distance divided by closing **rate**. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always show $v$ in m/s and $d$ in m for consistent SI units. > 3. **Confusing TTC and THW:** *Time-Headway (THW)* = $d / v_{\mathrm{ego}}$ (gap divided by **own** speed). TTC uses **relative** speed, not absolute. > 4. **Skipping the conclusion:** After computing TTC, you **must** explicitly compare it with $\tau_{\mathrm{AEB}}$ and state the AEB action — partial credit is lost otherwise. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pin this checklist before walking into the exam hall.** - **Definition:** AV = vehicle performing the entire Dynamic Driving Task (DDT) without human input. - **SAE J3016:** Memorize all 6 levels; be ready to **distinguish L3 vs L4 using ODD**. - **Sense–Plan–Act triad:** Perception → Planning → Control is the canonical architecture. - **Sensor comparison:** - **Camera** = cheap, semantic, fails in fog/dark. - **LiDAR** = accurate 3D, expensive, weather-sensitive. - **Radar** = all-weather, Doppler velocity, low resolution. - **Ultrasonic** = parking only (< 5 m). - **GPS + IMU** = global localization + dead reckoning. - **Sensor fusion** via Kalman / Extended Kalman Filter is mandatory — no single sensor is sufficient. - **Kinematic bicycle model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\psi$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\psi$, $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$. - **Stopping distance formula:** $d_{\mathrm{stop}} = v t_{\mathrm{r}} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2\mu g}$. - **TTC formula:** $\mathrm{TTC} = \dfrac{d}{v_{\mathrm{rel}}}$. - **AEB threshold:** typical $\tau_{\mathrm{AEB}} \approx 1.4$ s for stationary targets. - **ODD** = the operational envelope defining where the AV may operate (geography, weather, speed, time, road type). - **Level 4 Minimum Risk Maneuver:** safe stop when ODD is exited. - **V2X** (V2V, V2I, V2N, V2P) is the cooperative extension of autonomy. - **Controllers to remember:** Pure Pursuit (uses look-ahead) and Stanley (uses cross-track error + heading). - **Localization:** GPS alone drifts; fused with IMU + LiDAR scan-matching for cm-accuracy via EKF. - **HD Map:** pre-surveyed prior knowledge that augments real-time perception. - **Valuation tip:** Always end numerical problems with a **boxed answer** + **physical interpretation** sentence. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 4 Notes — Autonomous Vehicles** > These notes are aligned to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework. Map topics to Course Outcomes: **CO4** typically covers emerging automotive technologies including AVs and electric vehicles.
